225874246.review:
Cannot believe I slept on this game for so long.

The characters are unbelievably well written and believable. I have not felt this attached to a cast since Red Dead Redemption 2. That almost never happens for me in games, let alone horror games.

The Scottish accents and setting added so much to it. I am glad it leaned into a less familiar cultural backdrop instead of defaulting to an American one.

The idea of The Thing on an oil rig is such a rich premise, it all works incredibly well. I was not expecting it to be as good as it was.

I have seen some reviews criticize the game for being too “on rails”, but I quite liked that about it. Sometimes I want to play something that feels like an interactive movie, and this absolutely delivered on that. They also give you the option to reduce the yellow paint, which is a nice touch. Personally, I like not getting lost in the games I play, so I appreciated the guidance. It kept me interested all throughout

My only real criticisms are the occasional performance issues, plus a few nitpicks involving the pacing near the end and some of the interaction design. The ending dragged a little. I thought the game was about to wrap up at several different points, only for another character to send me off to another part of the rig to fix or activate something.

Some of the interactions also felt more tedious than immersive. A lot of them could have been handled with a single click. I understand that the game wants to make you feel physically present in the space, but in that case I would have liked the animations to be quicker and punchier. At times, they became a bit of a slog. Again, these are nitpicks.

I also initially really disliked Suze, but the game did a good job humanizing her by the end, so that issue largely resolved itself for me.

Overall, damn. This is one of my favourite horror games ever, and one of my favourite stories in games too. It is really, really well done.

225789750.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is one of those rare horror games that understands atmosphere before anything else. From the moment you step onto the oil rig, there’s this constant sense of dread hanging over every corridor, stairwell, and flooded maintenance shaft. The rig itself feels alive. Loud metal groans, distant alarms, crashing waves, steam hissing through pipes - it all creates an oppressive environment that never really lets you relax. The level design deserves huge praise because the rig feels believable, industrial, and lived in rather than just another horror map built for jump scares.

The monster design was another highlight. The creatures feel wrong in the best possible way - distorted, unnatural, almost tragic, like their personalities personified. There’s body horror here that clearly takes inspiration from classics while still managing to carve out its own identity. Combined with the lighting and sound design, every encounter becomes tense without needing excessive combat or cheap tricks. The gameplay complements this perfectly. You are not some action hero. You hide, crawl, distract, and survive - a very well thought of concept that foreshadowed why you went there to begin with. That decision keeps the fear grounded throughout the experience.

The music also deserves far more attention than it gets. The soundtrack knows exactly when to stay subtle and when to swell into something emotional or terrifying. It elevates scenes that could have otherwise just been "good horror moments" into genuinely memorable ones. The sound design is also very well done - reverb and echoes shoot out in all direction. You will literally have your character on one side of the door just listening for anything human (or inhuman) on the opposite end.

What really surprised me though was the voice acting. The Scottish cast and accents give the game an identity that feels refreshing and authentic. The banter between workers sounds natural, rough, and believable. Some exchanges genuinely leave you laughing, which makes the horror hit harder later because these characters feel human. There’s real personality in the dialogue, and it adds warmth to an otherwise bleak setting. I appreciate how they keep dialogue raw, with profanity used selectively to emphasise the character's emotions well.

That said, I do think the game could have gone further with its character relationships. The opening introduces several side characters who clearly matter deeply to the protagonist, but we only spend a short amount of time with them before the horror begins. Because of that, some of the emotional reactions later on feel like they rely on history we never fully get to experience ourselves. More time exploring those relationships would have added even more weight to the story. You understand why the protagonist cares, but you do not always *feel* it as strongly as you could have.

Graphically, the game is very strong. The storms, lighting, flooding, rusted interiors, and claustrophobic spaces all look fantastic. The visual direction works hand in hand with the horror rather than trying to overwhelm you with spectacle.

More than anything, this game reminded me of Amnesia: The Dark Descent - not because it copies it, but because it captures that same feeling of helpless immersion and environmental horror. It feels like a spiritual home to that era of horror games while still standing on its own feet, even though it's situated around one fog-dressed patch of the ocean not too far from home.

You can tell this was a game made with passion, care, and genuine love for the genre. I would absolutely love to see this universe expanded into a larger project someday, whether through another title or a more ambitious successor. The foundation is incredibly strong.

A solid 7/10 - pick it up while it's on sale or show some love when it's not. It's got a good 6+ hours of gameplay on standard difficulty.

225545678.review:
Short version, if a story set on an oilrig tempts you, get Still wakes the Deep. The location is well-realized; it seems believable (at least to the laywoman), and it looks fantastic, especially in black-and-white — which by the way is an option, and one that I whole-heartedly recommend.

That's basically it — the location is great, and it successfully sets a certain mood.
There are no fights. There are some stealth and some minor action sequences. There is a story, but I'm not giving much away when I note that it's a pretty run-off-the-mill "dug (or rather, drilled) too deep" story, and seeing that the game was in the Lovecraftian sale, I'm sure you know exactly where this is going.

As such, you should not expect all questions to be answered, all scenes to make sense — we are dealing with an unknowable horror here — and with The Chinese Room, the people who made the intentionally unclear "Dear Esther."

While SWTD asks for a very manageable commitment, I ultimately found the game slightly too long — I often wonder whether that sort of thing is economically rather than narratively driven — "If you make the game nicely short, people will expect a lower price, and that wouldn't be worth building a whole oilrig for", something like that.

That said, if the thought of basically playing John Carpenter's "The Thing" on an oilrig appeals to you, and you can stomach some hallucination sequences and unanswered questions, absolutely go for it. Oh, and yes, do yourself a favor and play it in black-and-white.

(Played on Linux. No issues. Also played on highest settings; seeing as the stunningly realized oilrig is the main selling point here, playing this on a potato would probably not be worth it.)

225479270.review:
I enjoyed Still Wakes the Deep, but I can see how someone would not. I really liked the oilrig as an environment. Plenty of claustrophobic and dark spaces with pipes, wiring and mechanical machines. The different parts of the rig do look mostly similar, but I never got bored of going through a new area. Unfortunately there's no room for exploration, as the game is super linear. You are led down a very narrow path with no alternative paths or optional things to do. The gameplay is mostly traversing the terrain. A few time you're dropped into a "sneak room", which are also very linear.

The story is fairly simple. NPCs will be telling you to go somewhere to fix something or everyone will die. Once you've fixed something, a new disaster is imminent, requiring your attention again. The game is bad at masking this and makes you feel like you're the only crew member actually trying to do something useful. The player character also has a personal story that is woven into the events, but I found that to be a distraction. I would have preferred that those emotions would have been attached to some crew member, someone I'm actively interacting with during the game. Now both the crew and the personal relationships feel distant, since the game is not long enough for both of them to have an impact.

There's a lot rough edges. Sometimes the enemies don't notice you even when you're standing right in front of them. Some forms of movement, especially in water, randomly feel like they are not doing anything. The sprinting also feels off at times. The character model's arms are rapidly moving back and forth, but you're barely moving faster than walking. In combination with the linear level design, it all makes you feel like you're not in control.

The horror parts are good. Reminded me a bit of the 1982 film The Thing. With the rarely seen oilrig environments, I really enjoyed my short time. It's very difficult to recommend at a full price due to the simple gameplay and short duration, but without factoring in the price, I can easily recommend.

225154163.review:
I think the best way to write a review is to break my thoughts into a few different parts. But, the TLDR; is that this game is a strong 8/10 and deserves a buy if you enjoy visual story experiences.

For the first part--I wouldn't say this is inherently a horror game. Although there are horror elements-really just body horror-there isn't really anything 'scary' to the game. The scariest part 
[spoiler] is messing up an item throw and not being able to find another to toss to distract the monster. This isn't a major issue though, as you can usually just run and get away. [/spoiler]  I think what does exist though is the perfect balance of cinematic horror elements that create an environment that is 'scary' but only due to a tense atmosphere. With the art style for the main monster thing there is a pretty remarkable sense of awe that the game gives you. This leads into point number two.

Point two is that the game plays like a movie. You could be watching the main character on screen play the game himself and from start to finish it would be entertaining. The game is beautiful. Max ultra graphics on a 2-4k screen really shows how much attention to detail the developers put into the game. It is gorgeous through and through, and the use of real photos / textures that were implemented strongly add to this. You can truly get lost into the game and start to question if you are watching real video. The one graphical thing that was a large let down though were the monsters that 'chase' you. They don't look good. They have strange and buggy AI. They glitch around and after the first encounter you have already seen the worst of it for the rest of the game. If they get up close, it almost looks like the texture is missing with how low poly and wonky it is. You can get the monster to bug out and sit right next to you allowing you to really get a close up. Its a nice comedy break in an otherwise serious story. I think that there was no need for monsters in this. The game could have just been about the rig failing no monsters and it would've been a 10/10 survival game against mother nature. The game also is purely a walking (crawling, jumping, and swimming) simulator. There isn't choice in anything and the paths are clearly marked with bright yellow things every ten feet. With the exception of a few water sections you can get turned around in, there isn't much here in terms of exploration which for how beautiful the game is is truly a shame. This, tied with very few (and mostly repeated) assets and things to interact with does make the game seem like a tech demo story more than a horror game. This isn't a bad thing once you accept this is a story, not a survival game. I actually didn't even remember adding this to my library, so I had no clue what I was in for. I had no expectations, and was blown away...just wishing that it was more of a survival horror game with how incredibly well executed most things here are.

Lastly, the voice acting in the game I thought was great. Paired with the graphics, its actually very believable that the characters you see are saying what they are. The atmosphere, the people, the emotions, it was done WELL. Kudos to the team, everyone on it.

Overall, I'd rate this an 8/10 and a worth it type of experience if you have a beefy computer and enjoy visual story games. If you are looking for something that will get your heart rate up, this isn't it. But, if you are looking for a highly atmospheric game that looks amazing and will have you invested in the characters and the conclusion, I can't recommend this more. Props to the development team.

Still Wakes the Deep is an epic experience. I can’t believe I slept on this game for so long. It’s another title I’ve owned for quite some time but never gave a proper shot—and that was clearly a mistake.
The game blends elements of a walking simulator with hide-and-seek horror, both genres I’m usually not a fan of. 224614980.review:
Recommended, but with one important caveat: this is not a game that blows you away with mechanics or scares you like Dead Space. It is more of a short, very atmospheric narrative horror that relies on its setting, sound design, language and the feeling of total isolation.

The real star of the game is the Beira D oil rig. Wet metal, storm, dark water below your feet, Scottish workers, industrial filth and that constant feeling that you are tiny, while the sea and whatever is beneath it could not care less about you. The game really can trigger thalassophobia. Not through cheap jumpscares, but through the sheer presence of that black depth.

The monsters are well done, more body horror than a typical creature gallery. What works best is that they are not anonymous mutants. They are members of the crew you met before. That makes the horror feel more personal. Roy’s story also hit me quite hard, because the game shows that on a collapsing rig it is not only the monster that can kill you, but also ordinary human illness, lack of medicine, lack of help and pure chaos.

Mechanically, the game is simple. You walk, climb, run, hide, and sometimes operate some machinery. The puzzles and stealth are not outstanding. On Story Mode it becomes more of an interactive horror film than a survival horror. But honestly, that worked for me, because I was not playing it for the challenge. I was playing it for the atmosphere.

I strongly recommend keeping the regional Scottish voice setting on and using subtitles. Without subtitles it can be hard to understand everything at times, but the accents add a huge amount of character. It feels like a real crew on a Scottish oil rig, not some generic horror cast.

The downsides? It is linear, short, and there is not much gameplay depth. Some achievements are missable, and full 100% requires the DLC, so completionists should know that going in. I would not call it a masterpiece.

But it worked for me really well.

It is a solid, atmospheric horror game about disaster, isolation and people trapped in a place that is dying together with them. Not the scariest game I have ever played, but one that makes up for it with a seriously powerful atmosphere.

Definitely worth playing for fans of SOMA, Amnesia, Penumbra, Moons of Madness, Conarium and maritime or industrial horror.

224048276.review:
I'm no fan of "walking simulators", but after finishing Frank Schätzing's "The Swarm" I've been longing for something sea-themed that involved melancholy vibes around it, and this game filled that void perfectly.

This is a typical "walking simulator" with a linear story without any gameplay surprises. The game is quite short, and frequent changes of pace make it not boring, which helped me get to the end.

But the story of "we hit a who-knows-what-with-our-drill-that's-trying-to-kill-us-now" kept me going in the first place. It has some "Color from outer space" mixed with "The Thing" vibes.
The characters are all simple working men and women, which made it more grounded and likeable. Voice acting is great, and I freaking love the accent. 

Graphics are great, but the game is real unoptimized. On my rig, in 1440p with DLSS (quality) it's not 60 all times, sometimes tanking below 50. Shadows are the main contributing factor to that, but turning them low -- while FPS goes above 100 -- makes the game look bleak. And for a game that relies on atmosphere it's not really a compromise I was ready to make. 

In any case, I really enjoyed the experience, thank you, dev team.

224006967.review:
This game is incredibly well‑crafted. The oil rig is packed with detail and feels both realistic and atmospheric (I can almost smell the oil and metal through the screen). Exploring it is genuinely fascinating.

The sound design is excellent, especially the way the protagonist loses their breath or starts breathing faster when scared. The whole experience feels very cinematic; it reminded me of The Thing and Soma. It caught me by surprise more than once and definitely gave me some adrenaline spikes.

The “things” moving in the dark rooms are some of the scariest creatures I’ve seen in a game. The sounds they make are… something else. Sound design is a solid 10/10.

I also loved the accents of the oil‑rig inhabitants - it adds a fresh touch, and the voice actors did a fantastic job!

223968460.review:
[H1] Drowning in silence and panic [/h1]

This game gave me that same kind of feeling I had while playing SOMA and Observation. Not exactly the same in terms of story, but close enough in terms of atmosphere and the way it messes with your head.

First of all, this is a Lovecraftian horror game. Its about surviving, walking, and just… experiencing what’s happening around you. And honestly? That’s where this game shines the most.

The atmosphere is insane. Like actually insane. You’re stuck in the middle of the ocean on an oil rig, and the game makes sure you feel that isolation every second. The sound design plays a HUGE role here. The creaking metal, the waves crashing, the distant screams it all builds up this constant tension. It’s not always loud horror or cheap jumpscare. Sometimes it’s just silence, and that silence is uncomfortable as hell.

Now about the story; no spoilers of course! The story is pretty straightforward on the surface, but the way it’s presented makes it hit harder. It’s more personal, more grounded. You’re trying to survive while everything slowly falls apart around you.

The voice acting deserves a mention. It feels real. Like actual people in a messed up situation. The accents, the panic, the confusion; it all adds to the immersion. It doesn’t feel like “game dialogue,” it feels natural. Honestly the VA did a phenomenal job here.

Gameplay wise, its a constant moving from places to places with parkour and stuff. The level design for me felt great! And, didn't feel slow at all. Its a 4-5 hours constant fast paced gameplay. So no reason to get bored at all. 

Overall, this game is all about atmosphere, tension, and immersion. It’s not trying to be something huge or overly complex. It just wants you to feel stuck, helpless, and constantly uneasy and it does that really well.

Will I remember every detail? Probably not.
Will I remember how it made me feel? Yeah… definitely.

I’d recommend this if you’re into atmospheric horror and story-driven experience with lovecraftian horror and funny accent. (Some of the dialogues were really funny. I was dying laughing XD)


Overall I rated this game 8/10. 

Can't wait to play the DLC to know more about what happens next!

Edit:- One thing I should mention is getting all of the achievement in this game is actually easy to miss but not hard to do. Well, beside the one where you have to play the whole ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ game with Scottish Gaelic. Yeah you heard me correct.

223531237.review:
[h1]An Okay Experience. From My Perspective, Not Theirs[/h1]

➕ [b]Spooky;[/b] It can be quite unnerving at times. You often get stuck in an area with a monster, and have to sneak, hide or run from it. Fighting isn't an option. But you get used to it overtime, and the horror sequences don't demand too much from you.

➕ [b]Parkour;[/b] Half the game is finding your way around a collapsing oil rig–that was already rickety to begin with. The controls are slightly more involved that just click; you have to hold down a button in order to hold onto ladders or edges. You sometimes get a QTE you have to clear, but the inputs are always the same.

➕ [b]Scottish;[/b] Every character is Scottish. It is... unique, to say the least.


⚪ [b]Yellow Paint;[/b] You can choose whether to enable or disable yellow paint. But honestly, I sometimes had trouble finding my way around even with Yellow Paint enabled.

⚪ [b]Performance;[/b] Not long after starting the game, I turned all graphics settings to minimum. That silenced the computer fans.

⚪ [b]DLC;[/b] I have not played the DLC nor do I plan to. It doesn't seem like it would add anything meaningful to the story or the experience.


➖ [b]Story;[/b] The story is... Well... Lukewarm? It's clearly more focused on the characters than explaining anything that's happening in the game. And the characters just talk about how they don't want to die.

223513645.review:
TL;DR: This game is a yellow-paint hallway.  You should play Routine instead, it's similar but much better.

//////////

While I had a decent time, I can't recommend this game unless you love the genre and you're really running out of games to play.

I have to compare it to the game Routine which I played recently.  When I reviewed that game, I complained about the difficulty, the lighting, the story, but I had to admit that minute by minute, the game was incredibly fun to play.  This game is something of the opposite: the story and visuals are really good but the gameplay feels awful.  It feels awful because the entire game is a yellow paint hallway with no player involvement whatsoever.

I played the game with the "Reduced Yellow Paint" and it did nothing to help.  I know I'm ranting a little about this but it's really astounding: I don't think there's a single room in the entire game where the player's path isn't set for them, down to the meter.  Even the parts where you evade monsters, you're just given an exact set of yellow-paint hiding spots you have to travel to in order.  Sometimes you're given a distraction object to throw, placed in the exact spot where you should throw it from.

The yellow paint gets so hilarious and extreme, you OFTEN have to run straight towards a dead end, and when you get to that dead end a wall breaks open, or the floor breaks, or the path opens up for you in some other "sudden" way.  You will see other ways to go that make more sense, but they will be locked, or blocked, or your character will just stop moving at an invisible wall until you go back to where the yellow paint is.

The craziest time it happens, you are standing on a platform with yellow paint.  About 10ft below you is another platform with yellow paint.  Across from you is a CLEARLY STUPID thing to climb on.

If you jump down, you die.

If you jump across and climb on the stupid thing, it immediately breaks and slides down.  Then you have to jump back onto the lower platform that you were just looking at.  This is the correct path.

222983071.review:
Not Recommended. (unless you like games that don't live up to their promises)

Still Wakes the Deep starts with an absolutely fantastic opening. The setting alone (an oil rig in the 1970s) is incredibly immersive and refreshingly original. The attention to detail, the performances, and the atmosphere make it feel grounded and believable. For a moment, it genuinely feels like you're about to experience a tense, realistic horror story built around isolation, danger, and human vulnerability.

Unfortunately, the game doesn’t really do anything with that premise.

Once the main "event" happens, it quickly shifts into a much more conventional horror experience: scripted sequences, stealth sections, and enemies that feel straight out of The Thing. It's not that this direction is inherently bad, it's just disappointing compared to what the game initially promises. The oil rig, which should have been the heart of the experience, ends up feeling like little more than a backdrop to a very familiar "run and hide" horror formula.

What makes this frustrating is the missed potential. A more grounded, realistic approach could have made this something truly unique. Instead, it becomes something we've seen many times before.

If you enjoy supernatural horror and cinematic experiences, you might still have a good time. But if you were hoping for a more realistic, immersive horror game that fully commits to its setting, this may leave you disappointed.

222836801.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is a game that’s managed to move me thoroughly, so what you’ll find here could be considered an unorthodox and genuinely personal (re-)view of the game’s themes.

On the surface, we’re talking about a horror game that takes place from a POV of a Scottish man trapped on a collapsing oil rig, with something ancient, unknown and corrupting, sprawling in the water. Delving deeper, we’re in fact provided a glimpse into something more unique: a portrait of a world that no longer exists, the depiction of a particularly romanticized, almost mythologized era of British masculinity, community, and faith.

The events occur during the 1970s era, and we’re met with an environment that fosters a world of shared risk, physical labor and community spirit between regular working-class men. The setting carries a certain masculinity that is apparent through raw humor and unspoken loyalty, rather than erasure of traditional roles or over the top (secretly cynical) machoism that we could so commonly see in contemporary media. Paired with this is a quiet, unsentimental Christianity that is modestly woven into the very core of the collective cultural heritage. These motifs are further accentuated, once the horrors take precedence over regular routine. Religious memorabilia, muttered prayers, superficial curses, genuine invocations, the workers holding the line together. Despite the chaos, they refuse to abandon one another in every instance and, even in most disorientating situations, right alongside their primary drive to survive, is the deeply-rooted inclination to lend aid (a.k.a. [b]Love thy neighbor[/b]).

This gives emotional depth to the atmosphere that is deeply in contrast with decadence of the ship.

Once the atmosphere of the oil-rig is established, we’re simultaneously given the base of Caz’s character, as a representing member of these values. Yet, he is far from a saint. We feel he is human, as this set-up was immediately injected with a dose of his character flaws, his stubbornness, pride, impulsiveness all of which take a tangible toll on him and his troubled family back home. By extension, these flaws make full circle and showcase the potential hurdles of the broader community of the time.

Once the catalyst event occurs, the game unfolds in nearly all its elements by consistent use of gradation (i.e. the progressive increase in intensity). This means that, as horrors persist and grow louder, so do the more subtle themes of the story, and so does their bearer, Caz. Notice the way in which the frequently heard phrase “Jesus Christ” in any iteration, follows the exact same progression. It begins as an empty emotional filler in everyday banter, advances to a superficial exclamation, then becomes a blunt curse. Later, in the second half, it turns into a genuine invocation. For example, when Finlay asks Caz if he is alone he replies with “Naw.. Naw.. Jesus.. Jesus..” — of course, the delivery is intelligent and double coded rather than preachy or ideologically charged, yet the shift in meaning is palpable. He does in fact answer her question.

Another ingenious example is one where Roy states plainly, wittingly: “Jesus loves you, Caz. Everyone else thinks you’re a ♥♥♥♥.” This line especially carries weight because Roy knows Caz’s faults ([i]and also because Roy is the archetype of “the lamb”[/i]). With this admission or realization, another motif emerges: Caz’s need for redemption. This does not happen out of nowhere, but it does ultimately lead toward the final showdown and the climax of the story.

Now, there is the gameplay rhythm, and a pattern that emerges especially later throughout the game: the descent into freezing darkness and return to warmth, anchored by the obligatory player interaction of having Caz warm his hands up by the heater as he processes his trauma and feelings of guilt through brief monologue. Given the constraints of the genre and minimal mechanics that only serve the narrative (i.e. no “survival” system) this repeated action sticks out in a way that signals its deliberateness, turning it into a solidified leitmotif.
The heater object is always the same, functioning as a symbolic checkpoint, an altar.

To me, this is reminiscent of liturgical cycles: descent and return, suffering and respite, death and resurrection in miniature. When Caz is pulled from the freezing North Sea the first thing he experiences upon regaining consciousness is a heater warming him back to life. Suddenly, he is inexplicably able to “sense” the presence of the thing, implying there’s been a shift in his perceptional awareness, a kind of sixth sense. In other words we’re privy to a pattern of the sacrament: baptism & rebirth followed by confession and communion in future instances. Understanding this shift is very important in relation to Rennick, the main representative and counterpart to basically everything discussed so far.

The office confrontation establishes just how big of a rat Rennick really is. In short, he is a self-serving, egotistical, empirical, utilitarian individual, a self proclaimed “king” of the crew, the bearer of a legalistic world view… or what I like to call: [b]Talmudic values[/b]. Rennick is the unintended voice of contemporary times that latches onto the previously established community portrait, leeching off the collective’s “fruits of labor”, all the while barking orders from his office speakers.

So, while Caz sits there being grilled and dismissed as a “♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ liability”, an actual working man’s voice breaks through the intercom with a plain warning that something “feels wrong”. Yet Rennick cannot perceive the “other”. He registers no intuition, no faith, no transcendent signal, only a procedural risk that he most definitely can be overridden by hierarchy and corporate authority, and it’s something he actually gets off to! His brief “concern” when trying to escape the oil-rig via helicopter reveals another stitch of the same patch, a hollow desire to keep Caz off his casualty list. Every action ultimately serves the “self” all the while prancing under the guise of so called “responsibility”.

Of course, he doesn’t get saved. Instead, he becomes a monster, allegorically and literally. His end is the natural conclusion of his worldview, and of all those who follow in his steps, it is symbolic of (((them))), those who have adamantly refused to acknowledge anything but the self, incapable of recognizing grace, love, self-sacrifice, nor the transcendent.

Finally this brings us to the actual horror of the game, the [b]thing[/b] itself. Through the lens of hermeneutics, the true horror is the spiritual storm and the shift that it represents. The old world of comradery, faith and sacrificial masculinity, collapsing under the weight of corporate, legalistic values, with Rennick as the embodiment of the corruption and Caz, the flawed working man, becoming the wrench that the system cannot contain. The game in no way explains the horror and this ambiguity turns the ordeal into a canvas on which the clash of moral orders is laid bare. The creature, like a true demonic entity, does not simply hunt bodies, but more importantly, it invades the mind. It forces the self to confront the buried guilt, the weakness, the shame. For Caz, this becomes a form of purification through endurance and sacrifice. For Rennick, it is the ouroboros, the doom of self-ful-filling.

Still Wakes the Deep offers more than scares. It’s a reminder of what we once had in 1975 and what we have lost in 2026. That corporate, utilitarian and legalistic values have displaced older forms of comradery, faith and masculinity. Its narrative serves us a warning that a society elevating Talmudic values will find itself blind to the abyss it creates.

It’s also a reminder that video games aren’t a way to escape reality. They’re an authentic way to enrich it.

222836582.review:
Though described as a horror game, the experience of playing "Still Wakes the Deep" feels more akin to watching a wrestling match. The great writing, dialogues, atmosphere, and voice acting — versus the myriad of repetitive objectives, occasionally unresponsive controls, and annoyingly abundant QTEs that make up for “Still Wakes the Deep's” gameplay loop. 
This results in a game that makes you clamor for the monster encounters (in which the game excels), just so you can be pulled out of the monotony of the rest of its game design. 
Nevertheless, once “Still Wakes the Deep” reaches its final moments, the humanity and emotional poignancy of its narrative pays off, making this experience still mostly worth it. 
And although one can appreciate the game's reliance on smart thrills and otherworldly suspense, in contrast with cheap scares and screamers, “Still Wakes the Deep” is, at the end of the day, simply competent yet not unforgettable.

If it was possible, I would rate this game "Maybe", but as it isn't, I think its fair to err on the side of the game.

The good: Production values are incredible, visual, sound, score, voice-work, everythings incredible, and the game performed admirably on my older machine even on epic settings (only a few hiccups)

The not-so-good: Gameplay, the little of it there is.

The game is extremely, and I mean EXTREMELY, linear, it actually feels more like a movie where you walk between scenes yourself, and the action is more or less only quicktime events which gets old very fast.

Story starts out strong, but after two or so hours nothing new really happens, its the same types of tension and hooks, just presented in vaguely different setting from last time.

Having you get THIS close to getting to your next objective, only for something to crash down in front of you, flooding rushing you away or some other event causing you to have to spend 10 more minutes traversion the narrow corridor you walk in was exciting the first and second time, but the tenth? No, now it just turned annoying.

The game can actually be played in what I would guess is a movie-like mode, where you can't die (I think), and that might actually have been a better experience than playing it regularly.

If you like visually pleasing experiences that are very, very light on gampley, then this is a treat, but if you like just a little bit of challenge, stay away.

222819309.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is not your typical horror game — it’s an experience.

The atmosphere is incredibly strong: you constantly feel isolated, the tension is growing every minute, and the game doesn’t need cheap jump scares to keep you on the edge.

What really stands out is how it plays with reality. Some moments feel almost like a nightmare — distorted spaces, strange transitions, and scenes that make you question what’s real.

The story doesn’t explain everything, and that’s exactly why it works. You’re left thinking about what really happened, what the creature was, and what Caz went through.

At the same time, the gameplay is fun - running, hiding, and surviving feels intense and immersive.

You don’t just finish this game — you keep thinking about it after.

Highly recommend playing this game if you like horror, mystery, humor, and emotional games.

222754138.review:
I'm a real scaredy-cat when it comes to (cosmic/psychological) horror games, which is why I didn't dare to play this one before. But it was clear from the start that I have to play it one day, because I simply love the setting and, according to the reviews, it seems to be a fantastic game that would match my taste quite well (horror aspect aside for now).

And what can I say? I wasn't disappointed.

I really like the setting, as expected, and the graphics are consistently superb! By the way, I did not experience performance problems described in some other reviews, even with highest quality settings (RTX 3060 Ti with 8 GB VRAM, i5-11600 with 3.9 GHz, 32 GB RAM, SSD).

The soundscape and atmosphere were great. And I especially liked the voice acting: very authentic and sometimes funny thanks to pointed sarcasm, without being silly or anything like that. I played with "regular" English subtitles because otherwise I probably wouldn't have understood a lot of things (native German speaker).

Regarding the horror aspect, I didn't find the game too creepy or scary. The monster encounters were very easy to handle: either crawl from hiding place to hiding place or sprint away, and it was always pretty clear when to do which. And it was always quite clear whether there was a potential danger in a situation or not, apart from occasional jump scares, but which were not too wild either. More often than this, I was "startled" by the fully modeled player character: [i]Whoa, what was that? Oh, that was just my own left arm again[/i] (coming into view by certain movements). It's hard to believe I'm saying this, but the game could even be a bit scarier for me. Some things were actually quite funny: when I first encountered Rennick in his transformed form, I almost burst out laughing.

This is not meant as criticism, no, I really enjoyed it. And even if I say that it [i]could[/i] have been scarier, this doesn't mean that it [i]should[/i] have been scarier or that it wasn't scary enough (for me). No, no, it is good as it is, and as already mentioned, I found the atmosphere very well done. And besides the cosmic/psychological horror aspect, this game addresses a whole range of (other) potential forms of anxiety and phobia: nyctophobia (darkness), claustrophobia (tight spaces), thalassophobia (deep water), acrophobia (heights), megalophobia (large objects), to name just a few.

What I particularly liked about the monsters was their distinct personalities, not in terms of their behavior, but rather in what they said. And the fact that they sometimes addressed you by name, etc. This, in turn, contributed greatly to the atmosphere in a positive way. "You are in the ♥♥♥♥ now, McLeary!" Awesome! I also liked how the monsters apparently didn't quite understand that they were monsters now, and sometimes even asked for help or something similar, but at the same time wanted to get at you hard. I enjoyed the encounter with Muir the most, I think.

The personality aspect works well not least because of the character "development" at the beginning of the game. The characters only exchange a few words, but these small specific stagings have already been sufficient to build personality and make the whole setting more authentic again.

The graphics are really great, as already said. Sometimes I just stood there and watched the gas flare from a distance. The churning waters beneath the platform were always worth some looks as well. And I also want to mention the visuals when ascending Leg B. Furthermore, great attention to detail has been put into the game in general. It starts with small things like tarpaulins flapping in the wind or notes attached with duct tape here and there, and it ultimately ends with a completely modeled oil rig, packed with "lore", that feels totally authentic and functional.

The story is perfectly fine, even if there's no real attempt at a "resolution" or anything like that. Perhaps we shouldn't really talk about a supposed "story" or "plot" here, but rather about a "narrative", and this is very well done, I think.

The game guides the player through the world in a completely linear fashion, but due to the modeling and narrative, it doesn't really feel that "static" at all. Certain places, which are visited several times over the course of the game and which are designed differently each time, depending on what has happened there in the meantime or what is happening with the oil rig in general, make it appear even more "dynamic" and reactive to actions.

The controls and gameplay are pleasant. You always know what you are up to and what to do, and you receive hints regarding which buttons to use, etc. Running sometimes feels a bit slow, but that's a deliberate stylistic choice, no question.

The game isn't too long, but it's perfectly fine and also suitable for this specific (type of) game, definitely better than "artificially" lengthening it. It took me about 8 hours, not least because I really explored and admired everything in as much detail as possible.

What else is there to say except that I absolutely recommend this game, [i]Still Wakes the Deep[/i].

222733893.review:
Mixed bag for me. The voice acting and story I really enjoyed, they know how to pull at the heartstrings that's for sure.

Unfortunately gameplay doesn't match for me, some aspects like needing to jump from one spot to another or shuffling along a rail can be incredibly tedious. Some parts need to be perfectly executed and timed or you'll end up doing it over and over and over. 

It also felt like there was an underlying mystery from reading a note or two late in the game that never amounted to anything, it would have been a nice monotony break. The game kinda never really went anywhere. 

People turned into monsters, but why? What was the thing and where did it come from? Why couldn't we send a mayday call out, how come there was nothing to do other than avoid them and flip some switches. It just didn't feel like anything happened, rushing around to accomplish nothing really. 

It could have been a lot more than it was, I feel like it was a well made polished game that looked and sounded very good. But that's where it ends. A disappointment for me.

Plus side it ran on the deck well, in that I could play it with no glitches, I think it maxed at around 30fps though so some parts looked a bit grainy. I'm no tech expert so that's about as detailed as I can be unfortunately!

Isn't scary as I think it's trying to be. never felt a real sense of danger in the game. when I died it was mostly because I forgot to f`ing hold the button to climb.
On that note, controls might be a bit awkward but nothing too horrible for this type of game.

In terms of story. It's decent. nothing stands out. 
it's all rather predictable and mundane. there are flares of decent story telling among the predictable bs.
if you explore enough you'll get some idea about a bit of "modern" writing put into this game for no reason at all. 

I felt the writing and story was all over the place with no clear narrative about anything. it's like they couldn't figure out what they want to do here except in very broad very obvious and mundane sense.

Overall, not a bad game but nothing brilliant. if you've got about 4h and want a very vague adventure with great sounds and music then get it on sale. otherwise, steer clear.

DLC - The DLC is about 2h-2.5h. Enjoyed it a lot more.

What I like about both games is the sense of mystery and the unique settings.
What I don't like as much is how everything felt forced and nothing about it felt natural in terms of the story telling and why thing occur the way they did. There's a lack of coherence and consistency. 

Both games feel like they had a the start of a cool idea but it never grew past that and that's the game you're getting.

Get it on sale for sure. it's not worth the full price in my opinion but it's worth playing.

222087644.review:
A 4 Hour Interactive Horror movie ghost train ride. It has very light puzzle, platform, QTE and stealth mechanics, so light that it would be challenging to refer to it as a game.

It also has an element of a ‘Kitchen Sink’ Family drama running in parallel with the unknowable eldritch horror and industrial accident story. Has no business working as well as it does.

The setting of an Oil rig is the vibe of Alien Isolation, but on a far stricter game play path. It captures the ambiance without having any of the mechanics.

So, a walking (Screaming in Terror) simulator.

Visually it is good and the 1st person perspective is excellent for immersion.
Voice acting and writing is excellent. But just be aware, it is a short linear story with no real replay value.

Recommend to buy on sale.

222085607.review: The Chinese Room has really stepped their Game up since my last encounter with them many years ago with Machine for Pigs. However, in some regards they are much the same. Just like last time, the monsters rarely if ever pose a real threat, when they do death isn't especially punishing due to frequent checkpoints, the puzzles aren't really puzzles, and the mechanics are very simplistic to the point that one could argue this still fits their reputation of being a walking sim dev. However, I enjoyed the atmosphere, art direction, writing, voice acting, narrative, and general vibe and aesthetic so much I don't really care. I really enjoyed my time with this game and its DLC and I can see myself returning to it some day, perhaps with friends or during the Halloween season.

221711073.review:
Haud yer wheesht!

Still Wakes the Deep is a cosmic horror masterpiece set in December 1975, where you play as Caz, an oil rig worker who’s handy with the leccy.

I went into it blind when it hit Game Pass and bought it on Steam the minute I finished it. Some games are good while you’re playing them, then evaporate the second the credits roll. This was not one of them. It stuck with me, so I went back through it and ended up writing this.

The setting is incredible. The rig feels lived-in, coldly industrial, and instantly hostile once everything goes tits up. The Scottish setting, dialect, and slang add so much to the experience, and the whole thing feels grounded and convincing. The performances are some of the best I’ve heard in a game. The writing is excellent, the acting lifts it even higher, and the crew feel like real people with real baggage. The game does a remarkable job of making you care.

As horror, it is not just jump-scare haunted-house stuff. It leans into thalassophobia, fear of heights, drowning, body horror, darkness, and that horrible feeling of being trapped somewhere you cannot escape. The audiovisual design is phenomenal and ramps up the tension beautifully. It is smart cosmic horror too, the kind that gets further under your skin the more you think about it. If you like The Thing, there is a good chance you will appreciate the body horror here. Whatever they drilled up feels like a worthy addition to the pantheon of body-horror and cosmic-horror monsters.

What also struck me is how well the game works as working-class horror. The cosmic horror is terrifying enough on its own, but it is wrapped around a world where ordinary workers are already being asked to risk too much. The crew are the ones paying the price, while the people in authority feel distant, insulated, or ready to save themselves first. That gives the whole thing an extra layer of subtext. You can absolutely read it as an allegory for corporate greed and unsafe, inhumane working conditions.

That comes through in the smaller details too. You are constantly surrounded by signs of a workplace being held together by strain, improvisation, compromise, and neglect. Budget pressure, workers grumbling, suspect safety measures, worn gear, reused parts, makeshift fixes, all of it sells the sense that this place was dangerous long before anything eldritch showed up. Then when things do go south, the failure of critical safety measures only makes it worse. I work in a factory, so that side of it resonated.

Despite being tagged as a walking simulator, it has plenty of gameplay. Some of The Chinese Room’s earlier work lost me because I prefer a bit more interaction than just walking. But Still Wakes the Deep has enough gameplay to avoid that issue. There is stealth, traversal, panic, fail states, and just enough friction to keep it engaging.

Visually, the game is stunning. Everything feels meticulously detailed. The attention to detail is staggering, from the signage and hallucinations to the flickering flashlights and the rainbow discolouration on the oil-contaminated water. The character models and animations might be the weakest part, but I still think they are excellent, especially once the body-horror monstrosities start showing up.

The sound design is impeccable. The team truly pulled off something special here, and the art direction and sound design are top-notch across the board.

The pacing is fantastic too. It never felt like it was stalling, padding, or wasting my time. The characters act like actual human beings, making sensible choices and doing their best in an awful situation, which already puts it above a lot of horror stories.

I genuinely hope The Chinese Room makes another game like this, because they absolutely nailed it here.

Still Wakes the Deep is one of the best narrative horror games I have played.

Absolute belter. 

Recommended.

221708494.review: It’s the perfect game for anyone who enjoys cosmic horror. It’s a great blend of Annihilation and The Thing. The game focuses on realism and atmosphere, meaning there are very few “game” elements; however, it’s still plenty of fun. What really drives the game isn’t the “game” elements, but rather the story and the dialogue, which are great — though I understand that, for many people, this isn’t the kind of game they might enjoy. My advice is: if you don't like the game's vibe within the first half hour, you can ask for a refund. For me, the game felt just the right length — the story isn't rushed, but it might be worth waiting for a sale to buy it.

221653940.review: Walking simulator meets Alien Isolation on an oil rig where the Scottish accents are strong enough to put Trainspotting in the shade. Set in the 1970s there's a real retro feel to this: boxy televisions and telephones that are attached to walls and a feeling of a world on the cusp of a thousand tech breakthroughs.  While we wait for that though we can walk around a crippled oil rig trying desperately to save ourselves and our crewmates from an alien (?) lifeform which has apparently lain dormant under the sea until its awoken by drilling.  After that it gradually takes over the oil rig, mutates the crew and generally makes life hell for apparently the only crew member who is still alive and hasn't gone completely mad or mutated into something out of The Thing.  The gameplay is pretty linear, there's no collectables to speak of but it has a atmosphere of long accumulated decline, fear, tension and is an absolute banger.  God knows if anyone outside of the UK will understand the accents or some of the reference but igve this a go and you won't be dissappointed.

221496265.review:
As a fan of the writing in Amnesia: A Machine for Pigs, I was excited to get to play through another game by The Chinese Room. While I was very impressed with the game's presentation (which was honestly good enough to recommend picking this up when on sale), I was ultimately disappointed with the game's plot and narrative. 

The environmental work is seriously a triumph. It's very clear A LOT of work was put into researching these industrial behemoths, and it pays off. Every pipe, tank, electrical conduit, scaffold is very intentionally laid out in a way that is very immersive. The graphics are really impressive, especially when it comes to faces and lighting. The actor performances (while sometimes being limited by the scrip to a lot swearing) really shine and feel very realistic. I seriously cannot praise the people behind bringing the world alive enough. 

Then there's the plot. Without spoiling anything, the game spends the majority of it's runtime, dangling a carrot, hinting at a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that never comes. Reality distorts, memories play out in a sequence that feels like you are meant to piece something together, some revelation that this incident is meant to expose. But it never comes. The game just ends. The narrative get's reduced to simply "Love is important because sometimes ♥♥♥♥ goes down and that's all that makes it matter". It's really boring and compared with the finale of Amnesia: A Machine for Pigs, it just feels like a let down. 

The game is worth experiencing just to appreciate the work that went into it, but don't hold your breath expecting a big twist in the end.

221440382.review:
Not recommended at full price as only the budget justifies that amount, not what the game offers. On a decent sale, it might be worth it if you want a short film-like experience set on an oil rig that feels authentic.

Amazing world design, atmosphere and voice acting are debased in service of bland gameplay (half walking sim, half "run away from the spookies"), a lazy and mid attempt at imitating [i]The Thing[/i] but without anything that makes that film phenomenal, and what [i]has to be[/i] the most unoriginal and cliché story possible to tell in this setting.
It feels as if the developers were really invested in telling a pedestrian story of an ordinary working guy with family issues, but for some reason decided to add cosmic horror trappings (that go nowhere) on it as an afterthought. There's nothing stimulating or original about the horror elements of this game at all.

Had less money been wasted on hi-fi visuals and there was instead a brave attempt at making something like an immersive sim with risks taken in the story and concepts, I have no doubt that this would have been a classic for the ages, rather than a baseline competent riff on [i]The Thing[/i].

221377327.review:
6/10

Unfortunately, this game was very underwhelming. I had heard good things about it but got ultimately disappointed when I played it. 

Bad things:

-Absolute abuse of yellow paint/cloth/anything. This game has a terrible level design: while environments are beautiful and very detailed the placement of elements of interest and the way they are highlighted is terrible. Even when I set the yellow items to be reduced in the settings, I still found A LOT of them, I can't imagine how many there would have been if I didn't use that setting. Not only it is packed full of them (yellow paint, yellow clothes, yellow lockers, yellow ladders, yellow metal bars on walls, yellow switchesm yellow valves and so much more) but they were placed very poorly. There were lots in areas where there was absolutely no need for them and the few areas where a little hint would have helped had none. This is some terrible level design and I belive there are so many different ways to make an element stand out without completely ruining the game's aesthetics like this. 

-The game gives you no peace. A very effective formula that has been followed by countless horror games is to alternate between scary parts and calmer, safer parts. If the game keeps scaring the player without a break, the player will become desensitized or even annoyed and will enjoy the game less. Having a moment to breathe, like a Resident Evil save room for example, helps create a healthy break and make the player feel safe but still give them anxiety cause they know they are gonna have to go out there again sooner or later, and this allows them to be scared through the whole game. Still Wakes The Deep doesn't do this: the game almost never gives you a break and the few moments where you get one are scarce and very brief, giving you no time to actually breathe process what's happening. This game puts you into a constant rush. I know there couldn't be many breaks for some plot reasons but I would have preferred if they squeezed some through the experience.

-The plot is ok, and I can't say anything else about it. It's just "ok". It is pretty simple, doesn't feel very deep or convoluted and the psychological elements are very scarce and spread thin through the game.

-The soundtrack feels very generic and forgettable. 

Good things:

-Amazing designs for monsters. The monsters are very unique and I've never seen anything like them in horror games. The closest thing I can think of are the necromorphs from Dead Space. I really liked their look and the unique way the have to move around. The flashy creature came from the sea also looks amazing and very original.

-Great voice acting. The actors are very good at their job and can deliver conversations that feel realistic while also conveying emotions with their performance.

-Good graphics and animations. It has a few small problems with optimization but the game looks very good except some minor details. 

-Very good chase sequences. Chase sequences against some monsters can really keep you on your toes and you'll need to think fast.

Conclusions:

Still Wakes The Deep had a very big potential but I'm not gonna hate it since it's still very ,very good for an indie game. I wouldn't recommend it to horror enthusiasts like me, since they may end up disappointed, but it is still a nice horror experience to try at least once.

221141935.review:
Strong start, OK middle, tedious end.

A beautifully realised world, with the look and feel of this aged oil rig lovingly detailed. Voice acting was also movie quality - always great hearing accents and dialects from outside the USA. The opening encounters were truly frightening and tension inducing.... but....

As the encounters continue, and with death, the tension lessens, and often leads to frustration when paths are unclear or when the run mechanic fails. There are some mechanics that don't feel like they were finished, like the heaters, and the mechanisms of the oil rig that missed opportunity for any complexity. None of the characters are particularly fleshed out, so the attempts for emotional pull alongside string swells fell flat. I let them run just to squeeze out what content I could.

Before the end, I am getting tired of running around with the constant "we have to do this or it's all over" which even the characters within the game are commenting on. I've heard criticism of the game for it's short run-time, but damn, if it was more of the same, that is a mercy.

I wanted to love this game, and I did for a moment, but it did not last.

220868262.review:
I enjoyed my time with Still Wakes the Deep and definitely do recommend it. The phenomenal Scottish voice acting and well-written characters hard carry the game. Still, I wish there was more interpersonal dialogue and character development. [spoiler]Especially given the short runtime and helter-skelter pacing of most of the game, I would have loved to have seen a much longer introduction before the drilling incident, during which we could have learned more about the characters we'd encounter later. That would have added emotional weight to the narrative and further sold the illusion of being on a working oil rig. Giving more time for the suspense to build around the drilling incident would have been welcome too; there must have only been 5-10 minutes between the game starting, "Oh yeah, there's a drilling issue, but we're still on schedule", "I DON'T CARE, DRILL ANYWAY!", and all hell breaking loose.[/spoiler]

The visuals and sound were incredible too and did help draw me into the game world. However, I'd still describe it as more video game-y than immersive, for a number of reasons: bizarrely slow sprinting, swimming, and horizontal climbing speeds, loading doors, expletive-filled staggers after very short drops and outright death from ~10' drops, way-too-high levels of downward momentum when jumping or falling into water, doors that magically unlock after you do the key interaction in a given room, an inability to jump on most knee-high objects (except for the handful the game intends for you to jump on), sometimes being cold enough to require a heater after going in the water and other times completely ignoring it, monster AI issues (e.g. usually not being able to hit you a couple feet through a doorway despite having clear line of sight), sections where you successfully stealth past a patrolling monster and it magically changes its patrol pattern right after you pass, deaths by falling a couple of times playing a water sound effect when I had actually smacked into a platform, etc.

The gameplay mostly consists of movement (walking, climbing, basic platforming, a couple of chase sequences) and basic interactions (e.g. open door, turn valve, pull lever, push button, answer phone) punctuated by simple stealth sections (throw object to distract the single monster, then crouch walk past). I think it was fine to focus more on the narrative and audiovisual quality over gameplay, but it would have been nice to have a little more depth. For example, some environmental puzzles would have made sense and there could have been another mechanism for holding off the baddies (e.g. a flare gun to burn them and make them flee).

Other miscellaneous gripes include that there aren't many options to lower graphical settings, which is likely to cause issues for players on lower-end hardware even with the ridiculous amount of upscaling technologies available. Toggle sprint by default on controller was a bizarre decision too, as I thought the sprint just didn't really work for a while until I dug into the settings. Finally, the lunge while swimming didn't work consistently for me; it almost seemed like the tooltips weren't synced up chronologically with the inputs the game expected.

Still Wakes the Deep clearly had some budget behind it, so while I understand the high price tag, $35 for ~4,5 hours of gameplay still feels a bit pricy for what you're getting. It's a smash buy for me on sale, though!

220520839.review:
[h1]A Tense, Emotion‑Fueled Horror Journey With Remarkable Acting[/h1]

[b]Still Wakes the Deep[/b] is an atmospheric first‑person horror experience that blends claustrophobic survival with one of the most genuinely compelling cast performances you’ll find in a game of this type. The setting — a 1970s oil rig off the Scottish coast beset by a creeping otherworldly nightmare — is beautifully realised, but it’s the acting and narrative delivery that truly elevate this adventure from a simple walking sim to something memorable. 

What sets this game apart is the authentic, gritty voice‑acting from an all‑Scottish cast. Every character sounds like a real person with a history, personality, and emotional weight behind their words — from gritty humour and salty banter to moments of heartbreak and sheer terror. The dialogue, rife with regional slang and delivered with intense conviction, pulls you into the world and makes you care about the crew you’re desperately trying to protect. 

The performances aren’t just surface‑level flavour either — they carry the emotional core of the story. There are moments of genuine vulnerability, frustration, and fear that hit hard because the actors sell them so convincingly. Whether it’s Caz’s weary determination or the anguish in a terrified crew‑mate’s voice, these scenes add real depth to what might otherwise be a straightforward horror escape tale. 

Mechanically, the game leans into exploration, climbing, and stealth as you navigate the collapsing rig and avoid horrors you can’t fight. There’s no combat, which puts the spotlight squarely on vulnerability, pacing, and tension. While the core gameplay is often simple and linear, this design choice reinforces the narrative tone and keeps you focused on the characters and their plight. 

Visually and audibly, the world is beautifully realised with atmospheric sound design that helps sell every creak of metal and howl of wind — but it’s the voice work and writing that truly make you feel like you’re living this nightmare, not just watching it. 

[b]Verdict:[/b] A gripping, cinematic horror experience anchored by outstanding performances and immersive storytelling.

[h2]10 / 10 — Highly Recommended[/h2]

220276252.review:
### Strengths
Visuals
VFX
Voice Acting
Set Pieces
Ambience

### Weaknesses
Gameplay (Walking simulator)
Story
	No Real Explanation at what's going on
	Not enough time to get invested in the characters
Enemy Encounters are way too easy and have little to no tension
Some QOL stuff.
UE5

Still Wakes The Deep is a first-person horror game reminiscent of SOMA. You are playing as Caz, a electrician on an oil rig who is hiding from police because he put some guy in a hospital for flirting with his wife. Suddenly the rig starts falling apart and you discover that the people aboard the rig are turning into monsters. It seems like something is growing and using the rig as its incubator? You must try to escape before its too late...

This game has great atmosphere, it really sucks you into the world. That is thanks to the great visuals, sound design, and a solid cast of voice actors. The set pieces of this game are visually stunning and I wish there was more of them, their use of color and sound is top tier.

The problems start to arise in the gameplay section of the game, it's mainly a walking simulator from one place to the next. There are no puzzles, and the enemy encounters are so easy that they fail to provide any tension. The game really feels like its on rails, delivering you from one set piece to the next, which are great, but the content between the set pieces is a bit lacking. The story is also not great. There is little to no explanation of what is happening with this eldritch being, and the characters almost don't care about it they seem more concerned with the rig falling apart than [spoiler] an eldritch being growing and turning everyone aboard into monsters[/spoiler]. Continuing with characters, [spoiler]the game does a bad job of making you care for them, you spend little to no time learning anything about them before they die almost immediately after you meet them, the only exception being Roy who seems like a good guy, but the game spends longer telling you that Roy is a good guy rather than showing you. Because we don't connect well to the characters the deaths fall very flat, instead of being this emotional moment.[/spoiler] There is also some QOL stuff, like keys not pressing, sprinting being weirdly delayed, hiding important progression stuff out of view, just minor nitpicks that annoy you when they happen. Another issue is that its an UE5 game, meaning you get constant frame drops, blurriness and ♥♥♥♥ performance. 

Overall I still really enjoyed this game because I am a sucker for good atmosphere, and can forgive a weaker narrative and characters if the game shows me something outstanding and immersive, which it does. If you're someone who doesn't get as much out of a strong atmosphere and visually impressive moments, you probably won't enjoy this game, the gameplay is very bland, the enemy encounters provide no tension because of how easy/stupid they are. Not every game needs to be a narrative masterpiece, sometimes doing a few things well goes a long way. I would rate this game 6.5 oil rigs / 10. The game never overstays its welcome, I do wish there was more time given to the characters, that alone would have made the game a lot better.

220006163.review:
I am usually not the emotional type when it comes to games, yet this was such a phenomenal experience that I couldn't pass by without giving it a very glowing and positive review. 

For starters, let me say- in a very short amount of time I have developed a massive liking towards the characters presented. It was easy to see who we were dealing with and what type of narrative they go by. From the get-go you're hit with a wave of mixed situations that trigger a lot of various emotions due to the phenomenal voice acting, which was both very immersive and incredibly impressive. I found myself in awe at certain moments because of the amazing delivery of lines, it felt genuine and emotional. The gameplay itself to me had no boring moments, there was a nice level of balance between intense and calming that it made the experience rather memorable and very interesting. One of the things I applaud this game for is the claustrophobic feeling that it gave me, mixed with the dread of the open sea and its various depths. It was such a striking contrast one from the other that it stuck with me like glue, I can't quite explain the genuine discomfort I felt at the clash of these sensations together. Mixed with the dread of being hopeful for a second only to drown with every false attempt. In all honesty- I was immersed to the story on a whole different level then I have been in a very long time. The ending to the game only struck a bigger emotional cord for me, which was the icing on the cake of the entire experience and a fascinating cherry on top. In terms of story telling, to try and not spoil things- I like how we (also the characters in game) are the first ones to experience the phenomena happening on the rig, so the moments of characters questioning things they saw and such also felt realistic. There were no prior documents or information about what was going on, yet alone knowledge of what they hit, so this part to me felt incredibly realistic. 

On a more technical note, it was a little difficult to adjust the graphics on the game. It did have moments without any type of lag, only for my PC to experience a little stroke from the overload which would happen pretty suddenly. It did take away from the gameplay momentarily, yet I honestly didn't mind it as much because the story was rich like the environment. The soundtrack of the game is a solid 10 because of the built up of suspense and emotion that I found myself having the volume always on a 100, since I appreciated the details and various delivery lines. 

All in all, Still Wakes The Deep is one of those games I would recommend to so many people to experience rich story telling, with a good amount of suspense and mystery. Adding in memorable characters and a narrative that sticks. I can see this game having a continuation of itself, which I wouldn't mind due to how impressed I am with the effects of the thing they discovered. Yet also, in the same breath? I wouldn't mind leaving it off on a mystery, something people can make theories about.

Phenomenal game, a 10/10 recommendation.

Now, for the first time these developers tried to make a game with real gameplay and they failed.
Outdated mechanics, quick time events, hide and seek game with Outlast vibes and puzzles that a 5-year-old would solve.
You will be walking,swimming, flipping switches, turning valves and climbing ladders for most of the game. Then there is your occasional-scripted run from the monster section. The game play is boring and repetitive.
Unreal Engine doesn't help here. Good thing we have yellow paint because it would be almost impossible to navigate without that solution. What is the point of photo realistic graphics when you have to put yellow signs all over the place. 
I don't know if they wanted to make a horror game or not ? If they did, they failed because the horror elements are weak. 
I will stop here. I could write more negative things about this game but this is enough.
The voice acting is ok, the sound design is solid and the OST is generic.
I bought this game on sale and had high expectations. I got nothing from this game and a refund would be nice if you ask me. The developers set the bar high so they have to deliver. In this case they failed.
Overpriced game, at full price you would have to pay 35€ +  11€ for the DLC that is important to the whole story. 
If this is your first game from these developers than buy it on sale. If you played their games before than skip this one because it is a real mess. I can't recommend this game, of course.

What a great premise for a horror walking sim. Not a haunted house, or a space station, but an oil rig. I played this for the first time in "silver screen" mode and it was truly immersive and comfortably scary. I would describe the experience as a combination of The Thing (1982), Color out of Space, and Annihilation (2018). 

I haven't played many non VR games as immersive this. You will feel completely helpless and simultaneously so resourceful, thanks to the smooth animations and simple, responsive controls and context-specific actions. The oil rig design and feeling of remoteness on the ocean is superb. The voice acting and sound design is excellent. Even the occasional, unexpected death was a lot of fun. I'm probably 90% finished, so wish me luck. Time to dive back in and see this through. Easy 9/10. Maybe higher once I see the end and play the DLC. Looking forward to replaying this some time, in the base game color mode.
