218753659.review:
The game is great because it takes us into an environment completely different from what we’re used to. We explore an oil rig from top to bottom, visit different workstations, and meet many characters who add some lore to the plot.

Overall, it’s a solid horror game, as it keeps the tension and psychological fear consistently high. If you don’t know anything about oil rigs, don’t worry—the game will teach you.

Atmosphere, graphics, stability, gameplay, voice acting, plot, sound—everything is top-notch.
I highly recommend it.

Don’t worry, it’s relatively short, but very satisfying.
The DLC is just as good and doesn’t fall behind.

218752882.review:
A very competent, walking-sim horror game. Everything in this game is done pretty well, nothing in particular is going to redefine the genre, but what is here is enjoyable. The graphics are pleasant to look at, though if I am honest for a game with this limited gameplay, I would have enjoyed it more if, graphically, it had a [i]bit[/i] more detail. I know that’s easy enough to armchair develop, but with this game's reliance on pure atmosphere, it is just so close to looking incredible and just falls a touch short. The story sort of falls the same way for me: it is an enjoyable Lovecraftian tale, but it plays out mostly as you would expect. Nothing wrong with that, the characters are well written and the story is more than enough to keep you going, but again - not going to blow you away. It’s definitely got “The Thing” vibes.

Where the game does stand out is its absolute genius location: the oil rig. What a terrific environment to set this game in. It’s something that I have never seen done before, and the cold, dark, endless ocean really adds to the atmosphere that they are going for. It was a constant joy to explore. I also thought the enemy design was pretty neat, and the tentacles that take over the station looked great. I appreciated that they actually had some colour/oil-reflections to them, it really gave the game more of an identity.

218751895.review:
If you play the first, you have to play the 2nd part. Both stories are incredible, kept me on the edge of my seat the whole time. Story is emotional and rather freaky / terrifying. I wont give away anything, but it leads you....holds your hand for a moment... then turns you loose into the chaos. Best played on a BIG SCREEN...with headphones + loud bass (or stereo)...all lights must be OFF. Lock in. 

If you have the capability to immerse yourself, it can be ...terrifying.
The 2nd story was very calustrophobic, (but thats why you came anyways) to a terrifying degree. It creates urgency and anxiety (in a tasteful way) in very cramped, and almost hopeless atmosphere. 

The graphics are excellent. I played it in 4k on an OLED. High polygon textures and environments. More immersive feel than....most shooters...the first person camera feels more realistic in how you look around in the 2nd part. 

People that complain about the length of time.....are dorks. This is a very well done game, with highly detailed environments, I thought of it kind of like an extremely scary and haunting interactive movie......unusually immersive. 

Good God, a VR version ......would be utterly horrifying in the best way. 

Stop looking at all the pictures and spoiling it for yourself, wondering......Instead...: throw yourself in there not knowing what to expect...thats half the magic anyways. 

Im serious about the bass-y headphones / playing in the dark.
This...is the only way.

218699977.review:
Methodology and TL;DR: 

My recommendation is always based on the MSRP at the time I write the review; right now, that's $44.99 CDN for the main game, and $16.99 CDN for the DLC. 

I do not recommend the main game or DLC at full price. I *would* recommend the main game on sale at 50% off, and the DLC at 75% off or better. At those discounts, I'd say you have a solid 7/10 experience for what you're paying, based off my own subjective opinion.

As a Horror Experience:

I'd say about two thirds the experience was just wandering the rig, mildly unsettled by the gory aftermath of the Event. Things are gross, but you can generally tell when you're actually in danger, and when you're not, and that drops the level of fear significantly. There are a few (very easy) quicktime events as the rig falls apart, but you very quickly learn to just hold all the QTE buttons when doing certain things and you'll probably be fine. 

The remainder is mostly a decent level of tension when you're being stalked by monsters, with tiny spikes of actual fear when you've been spotted and are racing for a hiding spot. However, those fear spikes drop fast when you realize the monsters are really, really easily distracted, and if you get to a hiding spot, they don't seem to have a means to get you even if you're sitting right in front of the opening.

Specific Call Outs:

Pros:
- Beautiful graphics - I recommend not doing your first run with the black and white mode active, the colour palette is excellent. Also, personal opinion, but if you're not doing the black and white mode, turn off motion blur and film grain - both are distractingly strong.

- Excellent voice acting by a mostly Scottish team. If you aren't familiar with IRL Scottish slang, you'll want subtitles on.

- Immaculate cosmic horror 'vibe' - the devs say they were inspired by "The Thing", and it shows in the monster designs.

- The setting itself is interesting and really sells the feeling of isolation.

Cons:

- Very short - Main story lasts 5-6 hrs tops.

- There isn't enough time to really come to care about your fellow rig workers before things go to ♥♥♥♥, so when they die, it feels hollow. I would've liked maybe another hour of 'face time' with them, maybe going through one 'workday' as Macleary before he gets called to Rennick's office, all while the drill is struggling with the thing it's stuck on, foreshadowing the Event.

- Gameplay is very rudimentary - it's a walking simulator with a few very simple mechanics stapled on. It's possible to softlock yourself if you do something the enemy AI doesn't expect.

- The devs handhold you by having the playable character comment on almost every horrifying thing they witness. I had Caz say "Oh my god" or similar to something I wasn't even facing several times over the run, forcing me to spin around looking for whatever it was he was seeing with the eyes on the back of his head. This is immersion breaking - IMO, the best horror games have the playable character shut up between cutscenes, and the player is the one who reacts to what's going on around them. That heightens immersion, vs being constantly told 'There's something scary here, now look around until you find it.'

- This is just a nitpick, but the game is a little oversensitive to you falling; the playable character freaks right out as if they were falling to their death when hopping down from fairly low heights, and sometimes when leaping a gap that there's little question you should make, which again, kills the tension because you laugh at them for overreacting.

- Another nitpick; Having to turn subtitles on to understand the slang isn't ideal given it also captures noises like your player character's [panicked breathing]. If you have names turned on, it also names the monsters, spoiling who it is that's died before you see them. Wishlisting, I would've liked a version of subtitles that was 'slang only', and they only pop up when slang is spoken - OR - they record a line with the slang, and a line without, and you choose the audio you want to hear when you start the game.

The DLC:

Specific to the DLC (I'm not writing a separate review), it strips back the game even further into a walking (Swimming) simulator with a single monster sequence at the end that's easier than any of the ones that came before. And aside from the monster sequence, it really wasn't scary. It got such a low score because it's being charged at the price of a full experience, and it's really not; it ends just as it's getting good. My partner looked at me as the credits rolled and asked "That's it?".

218667580.review:
I'd been looking forward to playing through this game for awhile and finally got around to it.
The introduction was going good, until the obvious political jabs and references began wafting in like a bad fart while you're eating. Sort of kills the immersion when people do this in video games. Can't just leave the real world behind for a little while, nope. Gotta subject players to YOUR obsession with real world politics, day in and day out. Not all of us want to eat, breathe, and ♥♥♥♥♥ current politics ok??

I might have even overlooked that after awhile if the mechanics and gameplay didn't become repetitive and predictable after such a short amount of time. The game looks great, plays smooth, but after awhile it becomes clear that all you're doing is playing cat and mouse in between climbing around and throwing switches. 
The ending is what truly ruined it for me. Too much like the average horror flick where I'm just cutting my eyes side to side and wondering why the hell I just invested hours of my lifespan on that.
It's not scary so much as gripping, and even then only for the first hour or two until you realize you're untouchable as long as you're hiding in the conveniently-provided HUGE crawl spaces. I can throw things, shine my flashlight right in the monster's eyeballs like those two doofus kids in the car scene in the first Jurassic Park, and the monster just kinda cusses me out before growing bored and squelching off to resume its pathing sequence.
Have no fear as long as there is a crawl space or locker near.
Don't get me started on the preachy, self righteous insufferable messaging that the devs don't even try to be subtle about. The "prick" on the rig (aka the villain) is "anti-immigration", wants to make Britain great again,(yeah I'm dead serious, that's actually in the game) while all the "good guys" are pro-union and come to understand on an instinctive level at some point that drilling for oil is bad bad bad! The monsters came because you wouldn't leave that beautiful sea alone!
The dlc chapter, while genuinely more fun and eerie than the base game, was entirely too short and the ending is where the bulk of the preaching came in, while the player is still left with more questions than answers as the credits begin to roll.

I'm annoyed that I fell for it. What a waste of money. I'll stick to satisfying my curiosity by watching Markiplier or Hollow play these narrative-driven games. Too pretentious and unimaginative for my blood.

218491207.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is what happens when someone says, “Let’s set a horror game on an oil rig in the middle of the ocean,” and everyone else replies, “Cool. Add emotional trauma and absolutely no escape.”

You play as a very normal guy having a very bad day at work. One minute you’re dealing with coworkers and workplace drama, the next minute the North Sea decides to personally ruin your life. HR is not equipped for this.

The oil rig feels terrifyingly real tight corridors, metal staircases, angry weather, and the constant reminder that you are surrounded by nothing but water. If you ever wanted to experience claustrophobia and thalassophobia at the same time, congratulations.

The horror doesn’t rely on cheap jump scares (okay, maybe a few). Instead, it slowly builds dread until you’re whispering, “I don’t like this,” to an empty room. The creature design? Let’s just say whatever is happening out there should have stayed very, very deep.

There’s no combat. You are not a hero. You are a man sprinting, climbing, and hiding like your life depends on it because it absolutely does. Every chase feels like cardio sponsored by panic.

The voice acting is phenomenal. The characters sound so real that when things go wrong, it hits hard. You start the game thinking, “Spooky oil rig adventure,” and end it needing a moment.

9/10 Made me afraid of the ocean, oil rigs, and going to work.

218487085.review:
Quite the surprise! Very good writing. I never played a game where the characters curse so much, it's actually hilarious. Story is good, atmosphere is superb, graphics are very pretty at times. They cut corners, there are only a few locations and some backtracking, but it works well with the scenario of an oil rig. The scares work really well, as the monsters are super scary.
Gameplay is simple and consists of
- traversing
- hiding and distracting
- opening valves, pulling levers
- cut scenes

With about 4 hours of playtime, it's rather short, but it's actually quite perfect this way. Even though it doesn't offer too much replay value, I think I will replay it at some point just to take in the atmosphere again. Thumbs up!

It might was my problem not knowing I am buying a walking simulator and not a horror-like game, but I am disappointed at what game gives you. First half an hour of the game was the scariest part because you expect Lovecraft's narrative where you don't know what you see or what you are against, but no. Not only that, all of the segments with monsters are just "hold run button to run straight" or the easiest possible hide'n'seek with monsters being stuck sometimes so you can just walk.
Story is alright. Again, was expecting so much more, but it's just there. Game just left me emotionless to what's happening there.
I see a lot of comments to reviews like "so what if it's a walking sim" - the problem is being not an interesting walking sim, but literally just walking and parkour which just takes your time (but sometimes with really good views). Usually walking sims give you thoughts to think or hide the threat until the end  or gives you anything that holds you until the end, while in this game all you can question is if the character will survive.

Somehow I liked DLC more than the main game. Yes, it's illegally short. I finished it in 1,5 hrs (main game around 3,5 hrs), but at least it's scarier and I actually had some fear where I had to tuck myself while, for example, running from smth.

5/10. I really appreciate graphics, sounds (forgot to mention. Sound design is amazing in this game) and voice actors. But if you want walking sim that is good in everything, just get Soma.

217543978.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is one of those games that reminds you why video games are such a powerful medium. It doesn’t rely on spectacle, constant action, or cheap scares. Instead, it slowly pulls you in, makes you feel uncomfortable, isolated, and emotionally invested, and then refuses to let go long after it’s finished.

From the moment you step onto the oil rig, the atmosphere is suffocating in the best possible way. The setting feels real — the rusted metal, the narrow corridors, the relentless wind, and the endless black ocean surrounding you. You never feel safe, and that sense of isolation becomes a character in itself. The sound design is incredible; every creak, groan, and distant noise adds to the tension. Even moments of silence feel heavy, like something is always waiting just out of sight.

What truly sets this game apart is how human it feels. You don’t play as a hero or someone trained for what’s happening — you play as a regular person, and that makes every situation more terrifying. The writing and dialogue feel natural and grounded, especially under pressure. The characters sound like real people trying to cope with fear, confusion, and desperation, and the voice acting across the board is phenomenal. The authenticity of the performances makes the emotional moments hit even harder.

The horror itself is handled with restraint and confidence. Still Wakes the Deep understands that fear comes from tension and uncertainty, not constant jump scares. When the game wants to scare you, it earns it. You’re left uneasy far more often than outright terrified, which makes the moments of true horror far more impactful. Being mostly defenseless forces you to think, listen, and survive rather than fight, and that vulnerability is what makes the experience so intense.

The story is quietly powerful and surprisingly emotional. Without spoiling anything, it explores themes of guilt, responsibility, survival, and what it means to keep going when everything feels lost. It doesn’t overexplain or hold your hand — it trusts the player to absorb the world, the characters, and the weight of what’s happening. By the end, you’re not just finishing a game; you’re reflecting on it.

The pacing is perfect. The game never drags and never feels rushed. It tells the story it wants to tell and ends when it should, leaving a lasting impression instead of overstaying its welcome. That focus makes the experience feel deliberate and meaningful.

Still Wakes the Deep isn’t just a horror game — it’s an experience. It’s proof that games don’t need massive worlds or endless mechanics to be memorable. All they need is strong storytelling, atmosphere, and respect for the player’s emotions.

This is the kind of game you think about after the credits roll. The kind that stays with you. And because of that, it’s absolutely worth experiencing at least once.

217476067.review:
[h3]"The Sea is Full of Strange and Beautiful Things..."[/h3]
An incredible cast and stunning visuals really pull you into the atmosphere of this doomed oil rig, down into the Scottish North Sea. This isolated and distant locale allows our protagonist, Caz, to flee responsibility for a drunken bar fight back home, abandoning his family. It's only later we find out how much he betrays himself in this decision, mirroring his own fathers' abandonment. This theme of absent fathers reccurs with [i]Siren's Rest[/i], set a decade later, running towards the danger instead of away from it, in an attempt to find closure.

While the environments of the DLC are far less impressive than the main game, there's more opportunity to explore and interact with the environment, which had previously felt linear and restricting. Moreover, the story benefits from a reduced run time, becoming more cohesive, where the main story drags quite significantly and becomes tedious in the middle. These two stories form a really compelling arc, but I can't recommend the experience at full price. Luckily the complete collection frequently goes on a 60% sale, which is a reasonable deal for the experience.

The writing, characters, and pace of the game is just fantastic, and the atmospheric horror does a great job building up tension without having to resort to jump scares. It's a survival horror without the means for you to protect yourself via combat, but the game does a great job not making surviving feel overbearing and helpless. The ending was impactful, and overall the game was a fantastic sleeper. Highly recommend.

217448671.review:
Still Wakes the Deep was a ride.

As (extremely) scottish electrician Caz, I snuck, ran, sidled, climbed, swam, fixed, scooched, and leapt for my life aboard a deep sea oil rig. There's a nice story wrapped around the main mechanic of Survive Current Events, which gives everything a little extra depth.

It reminded me a little of Alien: Isolation, both in the way the story unfolded and in the little touches of intention that were given to a lot of the actions you perform - for example, pushing up or down on the stick to push or pull a lever instead of just pushing X to thing, or performing a series of movements to activate a more complicated mechanism. Probably the similarities between an offshore oil rig and a space station don't hurt either. 

The narrative is very linear. You have one objective, you need to get somewhere and/or do something in order to accomplish it, and there's basically one way to do so - either by finding the right way to traverse the environment, sneaking past someone, activating a mechanism, etc. This isn't a [i]bad[/i] thing, as there's still challenge in the finding/sneaking/activating as well as in discovering what is it you need to do in the first place, but it can be a little frustrating when you know what to do but are having issues with the execution. 

The game looks and sounds great, and the environment is very stylized and consistent. The horror aspects were fun and well done, and I feel like there was a good balance between tense hiding/sneaking, all-out running away, and environmental stuff like darkness, claustrophobia, and drowning. The pacing was also well-managed, so I didn't get tension exhaustion or adrenaline overload. It also ended while I was still enjoying it, which is an underrated quality in a game.

217129945.review:
Weirdly a game that I really enjoy for the story, but the gameplay is nothing special. 

I did just complete a second playthrough (picked up the DLC in the Winter Sale and thought I would revisit the main game. 

The best elements of this game is the writing and environmental design. You really get the sense of the Lovecraftian nightmare taking over the rig. 

Amusingly I found a couple of the monster sections I could actually just walk through without using the hiding areas or destractions. I only noticed that on the second playthrough as I was trying not to sprint for more than 10 mins in the entire run.

217056981.review:
Honestly, I went into this game with zero expectations. I thought it would be some average, forgettable experience. My plan was to launch it, play for five minutes, and delete it like I do with most games.

Instead, this game completely blew me away.

First of all — the visuals. The game looks absolutely next-gen. The atmosphere, lighting, and overall presentation are insanely immersive. From the very first moments, I was impressed by how technological and cinematic everything feels.

The setting is also fantastic. A guy going to work on an offshore oil rig in the middle of the ocean, and then everything goes to hell — it’s a perfect horror setup, and the story really pulled me in. The narrative and atmosphere kept me hooked the whole time.

What I also really loved is the variety of gameplay mechanics. The game constantly mixes things up: sometimes you’re being chased and have to run, sometimes you’re solving puzzles, sometimes you’re dealing with completely different situations. The pacing and balance are excellent — you’re never stuck doing the same thing over and over again, and it never gets boring.

I honestly expected to quit after a few minutes. Instead, I ended up playing all night and couldn’t stop until I finished the entire game. Even after that, I couldn’t fall asleep for hours — that’s how much it stuck with me.

Even though I originally played a pirated version, the game impressed me so much that I decided to support the developers and bought the game along with the DLC. And I’m really glad I did.

The DLC is great because it doesn’t just repeat the same ideas — it introduces new mechanics, like underwater sections and quests, which feel fresh and creative. I really respect developers who don’t just reuse the same formulas and actually try to innovate.

For me, this is easily one of the best horror games of the last 10 years.
15/10 — absolutely unforgettable experience.

216998670.review:
Still Wakes the Deep feels like a masterclass in modern Lovecraftian horror, trading jump scares for an oppressive, slow-burn dread that steadily tightens its grip. The game excels at tension-building, letting atmosphere, sound, and isolation do the heavy lifting as unease seeps into every moment. Its depiction of the entity is both beautiful and unsettling—otherworldly in a way that inspires awe, terror, and dread at the same time. The result is a haunting experience that lingers long after you’ve put the controller down.

One of the most welcoming aspects for me in Still Wakes the Deep is that it feels far more like an interactive movie than a traditional game—and that’s very much to its benefit. Player agency is intentionally limited, with simple mechanics and guided sequences that keep the focus squarely on narrative, pacing, and atmosphere. Instead of puzzles or combat driving the experience, it’s the cinematic presentation, exceptional voice acting, and carefully staged moments of tension that pull you forward. This approach allows the slow-burn dread to unfold with precision, making each scene feel deliberate and emotionally charged, as if you’re moving through a meticulously crafted horror film rather than “playing” a game. 

I fully enjoyed my experience and hope you all do to. Thank you developers for bringing this gift of a game to life.

I completed the game in around 5.7 hours.

If you like cosmic horror I strongly recommend you get this game.

In terms of gameplay, this is basically a "walking simulator". There are platforming, stealth parts, puzzles and QTEs, but they are there as an excuse to be a video game than they actually matter. This is NOT Alien Isolation.  
No real exploration as every path but you have to go is conveniently locked or blocked, and you can literally follow the yellow brick road, I mean yellow markers. No item collecting, no upgrades, no skill trees. No replay value whatsoever, either.

Having said that, I still loved the game. It feels ironically refreshing among so many games these days putting in open world and all the fancy modern gameplay elements almost from obligation.

216641028.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is a game that could have come out 15 years ago, and that's not necessarily a bad thing. That time was, after all, the golden age of Horror/Walking Sim games. While the term "Walking Simulator" was meant as a negative initially, I don't think there's anything wrong with the type of game, and to those that say "they aren't games", label them what you want, they can be great experiences nonetheless. 

Still Wakes the Deep is no exception. It doesn't try to be anything else. There's no inventory management, no weapons, no fear mechanic or survival system. All you do is walk, and occasionally run in panic, through essentially a very linear haunted house type experience; except its an oil rig rather than a house. 

It isn't meant to challenge, its meant to take you through a spooky narrative, and it does very well at that. The voice acting is excellent, and you can tell it was written by true Scots. It doesn't have that awkward writing that results from Americans trying to be authentically [insert culture here] and missing the mark. 

The story isn't all that unique, but its done well. A Scottish North Sea oil company took some inspiration from the Dwarves of Moria and delved too greedily and too deeply, and woke up something horrible, only this time its in the sea and has a distinctly eldritch flavour. There's lots of interpersonal drama going on too, but unfortunately its a ironically a bit shallow. The very beginning of the game doesn't allow for enough time to get to know the cast of characters before all hell breaks loose, so when it does its harder to see them as fully fleshed out characters. I also think there could have been more sections that allowed for lore to be drip-fed, but in this game you have about 10 minutes of that at the beginning and then its just the horror ride until the end. A few extra instances where you could read journals, or listen to tapes or whatever like in games like Soma, Amnesia, etc would help flesh the game out more. 

The game is also gorgeous and atmospheric. It has probably the best use of film grain I've seen in a video game for a while, and the sound design is exceptional. There's not much else to say about that, it looks and sounds great. 

Overall, I'd recommend this game for anyone who enjoys horror. Its not going to be anyone's game of the year, but its a fun horror experience that you can finish in a weekend. Get a hot drink, get a blanket, turn of the lights and have a good time with it.

I'm surprised that in 2024, people still can't build a game with a believable narrative or engaging gameplay. This "media product" has a very atmospheric setup and fantastic voice acting (I love Scottish accents—check out Kevin Patterson on YT). However, it all quickly dissipates once the game truly begins. What a joke! My suspension of disbelief is gone. These aren't real people; they're just voice files and 3D models. People don't act like that in dangerous situations. I've only played for a bit and already lost interest. Fatalism devolves into absurdity. Instead of 'I cannot save him because the world is falling apart,' we feel 'I cannot save him because he lacks a walking animation.
I agree with other reviews: this game is just a bunch of small locations with nice graphics and tedious climbing and crawling... The anomaly itself doesn’t make sense; it just uses cliché body horror with colorful auras. Where is the actual game? How can you leave Roy MacNair (the cook) alone when there's alien flesh destroying everything around the corner? Oil rigs are made of steel, by the way, yet this alien "doo-doo" thing just rips everything apart. And you just leave a living human behind? What kind of narrative is that? It's up to us to offer some critique, or "walking simulators" like this will continue to appear. Damn, I must be too old for this crap.

215915347.review:
Still Wakes the Deep left me conflicted in a very specific way. I usually appreciate shorter more focused experiences, especially when a game ends and leaves me wishing there was more. Here though, that feeling came from relief rather than longing.

I think I might just be burnt out on horror walking simulators, but this one pushed me over the edge. The complete lack of traditional gameplay made the entire experience feel like a chore. At a certain point I genuinely started asking myself why I was playing this at all when I could have just watched it. I felt the spirit of Gabe Newell gently place a hand on my shoulder and ask, “Now, why is this fun?” And the only answer the hamster wheel in my head could come up with was that it isn’t.

The game is absolutely infected with the yellow paint trend showing you exactly where to go at all times. Any sense of discovery or tension is smothered by the constant hand holding. You are never allowed to engage with the world in a meaningful way, only move through it exactly as instructed. I felt as if I was an actor just playing my role in these scenes. 

The voice acting is good, genuinely so. The interestingly preformed Scottish voice acting performances carry a lot of weight and help sell the atmosphere. The story is also fine, serviceable even. But based on the marketing, I was led to believe this would be something so amazing I would just have to get a “Still Wakes the Deep” tattoo on my head. I wanted this to be far more affecting, something that would stay with me long after the credits rolled, but instead, once the novelty wore off all that remained was the realization that I was not enjoying actually playing the game.

That said, I cannot in good faith give this a negative review. Because of steam’s binary negative or positive review system, my review will be published as positive. I want to be fully transparent in saying my experience was barely positive, very middle of the road. There is absolutely a place for a game like Still Wakes the Deep, and I can easily see many people loving it, especially those who value narrative and presentation above interactivity or maybe for somebody that just isn’t experienced with games. As a seasoned GAMER with a hard R, it just wasn’t for me. My praise begins and ends with the performances and the story. Beyond that, I found zero enjoyment in the act of playing it. SCOTTLAND FOREVA

215897612.review:
It is a demonstrable fact of cultural production that the horror genre, in its reflection of societal anxieties, has long found fertile ground in the boundless and abyssal sea. Yet, for the discerning materialist, the bulk of this “nautical horror” remains mired in bourgeois supernaturalism or mere monster-mongering, with few works achieving a dialectical dread.  The literary foundation for nautical horror, it can be argued, is found in three pivotal works: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner (1798), The City in the Sea (1845), and Moby ♥♥♥♥ (1851).  However, nautical horror would not be mastered until the early 20th century with H.P. Lovecraft’s tales of oceanic atrophy: Dagon (1919), the seminal The Call of Cthulhu (1928), and The Shadow over Innsmouth (1936).  Michael Crichton’s Sphere (1987), which translated the unknowable into a psychological artifact of human folly, deserves an honourable mention.

Moreover, the cinematic translation of this dread followed, with notable eruptions in the waning years of the 20th century and 21st: Leviathan (1989), Below (2002), and Underwater (2021).  Each, in its way, grapples with the terror of isolated infrastructure under pressure, both physical and psychic.  However, a more potent and historically specific iteration of this isolation - the offshore oil rig as a monument to extractive capital - remained curiously underexplored.  A presentiment of its potential was glimpsed in the procedural paranoia of The X-Files episode “Vienen” (2001), which demonstrated how the rig’s fusion of industrial alienation and sheer remoteness could produce a perfect petri dish for horror.  (A high I have been chasing and Still Wakes the Deep delivered!)

It is against this backdrop that the recent interactive work, Still Wakes the Deep (2024), arrives not as a mere entertainment, but as a brilliantly crafted synthesis.  Here, at last, is the full realisation of that long-yearned-for setting: the oil rig as a total system - a clanking, groaning apparatus of profit - becoming the locus of a profound and intimate catastrophe.  The game succeeds precisely because its horror is not merely appended but emerges dialectically from the environment and the social relations therein.  Its narrative force is amplified by a mode of dialogue which rejects theatrical soliloquy in favour of a grounded, realistic pattern of thought, thereby binding the player’s experience to the palpable desperation of the crew, odds and absurdities notwithstanding.

The subsequent exploration of the wreckage of the Beira in the game’s follow-up - Siren's Rest (2025) only reinforces the historical-materialist appeal: we are compelled to survey the ruins of industrial overreach, and unique blend of investigation impinged upon mystery.  This is the direction in which the genre must develop - not toward mere spectacle and cheap jump scares, but toward a concrete horror born of specific, real-world locales of capital and isolation.  For those who seek a narrative that understands terror as a product of environment and circumstance, rather than a generic imposition, Still Wakes the Deep stands as a necessary recommendation.  This demonstrates that the gravest evils are not remnants of mythic histories but are produced by contemporary institutions - not the spectres of superstition but the deliberate, systematic mechanisms of the bourgeois order.

215747184.review:
Still Wakes the Deep can barely be called a game, as its more some linear progession with light switch throwing and monster avoiding sprinkled in to pad out its pacing. However, though the "game" part is lacking, the story and atmosphere make up for it. 

You play as Caz (he's good with the 'leccy) and he's stuck on a sinking oil rig while a cosmic horror does it's Thing and turns his crewmates into fleshpiles, abominations toward man, and something inbetween. Caz is also apparently a skilled mountaineer descended from Tarzan, coz he clambers over ruined wreckage and leaps across gaping chasms in a way that would make Lara Croft ask to buy him a pint. With his trusty lockpick (i.e. a screwdriver that doubles as a prybar), you'll pilot him around the rig in a desperate bid to survive and get back to shore, to see his Sarah and his weans.

If you have trouble with a Scottish brogue, I suggest you play with subtitles, though the subtitles are also phonetically pronouced so you may have just as much trouble reading the words as you do trying to listen to them. However, the acting here is flawless, everyone acts like actual human beings from the time period (even the monsters at times), and the story is well paced and presented. The game also looks majestic at times, and the premise of being stuck on a oil rig atop a Hellmouth is a good spin on a somewhat dated cliche.

If you've got an afternoon to kill and want to experience a story instead of just sitting back and watching The Thing again for the billionith time, give Still Wakes the Deep a shot, I think it'll be worth your time. The DLC, Siren's Rest, is just a little more of the same with a few different quirks, and I feel is a good experience too, if a bit short. Wait for a sale before you purchase though.

215649799.review:
Technically, Still Wakes the Deep is an absolute powerhouse – atmospheric, impressive in sound design, and visually stunning. From a gameplay perspective, however, it sometimes feels like a workplace safety video produced by a hyperactive walkie-talkie.

The premise: 1975, an oil rig off the coast of Scotland, everything goes wrong – and right at the moment of his dismissal, the screw-up suddenly becomes the socialist-inspired “Hero of Labour.” Socialist? Of course! One person works while everyone else stands around getting in the way and barking orders. A for “instructions.” Or A for “another call giving instructions.” Both are painfully accurate.

Because the crew always knows exactly where our hero is. Surveillance by telepathy? GPS from the future? Who knows – the main thing is that the radio must ring every five minutes. “Do this,” “go there,” “jump over that,” “dive under this.” The protagonist obeys like a caffeine-addicted lab rat. Instruction – execution – next call – next instruction. Occasionally he’s chased by a monster just so things don’t get too cozy, but even that barely disrupts the oil rig’s bureaucratic workflow.

And the player keeps asking: Why? Why am I doing this? Why don’t I just build a raft out of coffee cups and leave? And above all: why doesn’t the hero use his own brain for once? Well – that wouldn’t fit the role of the “Hero of Labour,” would it? Independent thinking was clearly not included in the script. Not until the bitter end.

Speaking of the end: of course, it’s… another instruction. What else?

At times I felt like only one task was missing: “Bake me a cake, it is essential for our survival.” It would have blended seamlessly into the workflow.

Conclusion: Technically brilliant, visually gorgeous – but narratively an 80s B-movie that is best enjoyed with a molecule consisting of a carbon chain and an OH group. In other words: beer. One or several. There are far better genre entries, S.O.M.A. for example.

Side note: The Scottish accent is wonderfully executed. However, some cutscenes drag on a bit too long and drip with sentimental cheese. Less would have been more.

Wow. I did not think possible for such a remote, industrial thing like an oil rig could be a phenomenal setting for a horror game but man, I was wrong. For me, this game became an instant classic masterpiece of the horror genre like Layers of Fear.

The story in short: you are a worker on a northern sea drilling rig who gets fired - but before you could leave, something happens: the rig drills into something unknown and all hell breaks loose. In the ensuing chaos, the entire structure slowly begins to collapse into the sea, while unknown, eldritch monsters start hunting the survivors — it draws heavily on the horror movie The Thing, so I won't say more, lest I spoil the story. The graphics are top-notch, the rig structure is probably as close to the reality as it is possible to get and The Chinese Room takes care of the story and the emotional factor so it is very easy to immerse yourself in the events. The only downside I found is the small control related bugs here and there but nothing very serious. For me, it's a 9.5/10, a must have for all horror fans.

215287112.review:
I just finished this in just over 4 hours. If you’re looking for a massive open-world grind, this isn't it—it’s a tight, linear horror story that knows exactly what it wants to be.

The Good:
The Setting: An oil rig in the middle of nowhere is the perfect spot for a horror game. It feels heavy, rusted, and claustrophobic.

The Tech: Running on Unreal Engine 5, the game looks incredible. If you crank it to max settings, be prepared to hear your fans screaming—it’ll stress pretty much any system, but the payoff is some of the best lighting and environments I’ve seen lately.

The Voice Acting: The Scottish/English cast is top-tier. It feels authentic and gritty. Side note: Keep the subtitles on. The accents are thick, and unless you’re from the area, you’re going to miss half the dialogue without them.

The Not-so-Good:
It doesn’t reinvent the wheel. If you’ve played other survival horror games, you’ll recognize the "hide and move" rhythm immediately. It’s a bit of a "walking sim" with horror elements, but the location makes it worth the trip.

Verdict: It’s a short, polished, and intense experience. Definitely recommend it if you want a unique story and have the hardware to see it at its best.

Briefly: it's like a game - "Alien: Isolation".

I am a big fan of games where you feel isolated in some place (space, ocean/sea, etc.) where you realize that there's a tiny chance to be rescued by someone or something.

It's a great game with a great story and VERY cool visual effects, detailed monsters and graphics in general.
You fight not only for your life, but also for the lives of your colleagues, for the lives of your relatives and for the life of the oil rig.

The only small minus is that it's kinda short (for me), the whole gameplay is about 3-5 hours and the DLC additionally 1.5-2.5 hours. But maybe it's cool because it's dynamic and the plot moves pretty fast.

The thing that I mostly liked: everything you do - you see and feel the result because every task is to support the rig not to be drown fast and hold for the chance of being rescued. 
AND!
You feel that you dont know how and have nothing to defeat the monsters. It's very cool because it makes you fear that something will happen right in a moment but you are just an electrician with a simple screwdriver in a hands.

215229792.review:
As a die-hard fan of SOMA, I found this to be rather good overall and would recommend it; especially if you're a fan of atmospheric horror games like SOMA or The Chinese Room's other offerings, or love underwater adventure games. It did fall flat in some places, but I would recommend it generally.

[b]The good:[/b]

The visuals are incredible, I was constantly spamming the screenshot button, even if I did have to turn on *all* the AMD FSR superscaling AI nonsense, but I guess that's modern gaming for you.

The voice acting is top notch, among the best I've heard in any game I've ever played. Subtitles recommended if you're not Sco'ish. The audio design in general, for the creatures, environments, music, atmosphere - it's absolutely top notch. Really good work to both of those teams.

Gameplay is okay: the stealth sections are fun and cool, the parkour puzzles are a bit meh and can be tedious. Chase sequences are fun and generally quite scary.

The game has excellent accessibility options as well, the UI is really nice and polished. The game also didn't crash in my playthrough.

Your main character, Caz, is a good fella and all round pretty relatable and funny guy. Love him.

[b]The bad:[/b]

To be honest, I was expecting a lot more story and lore-wise. Clearly, this game uses very Lovecraftian visuals, but we never truly get any information as to what's possessing the rig. Generally, the point of an atmospheric horror game/walking sim is that you want to [i]say[/i] something; make a statement, or even just bring you into a world of lore. SOMA does this really really well, SWTD does not at all. For most of the game, you are basically an errand boy running around and fixing things on your ♥♥♥♥♥♥ oil rig. It gets tedious. Plot wise, it's really just pretty lackluster. There's no development, movement, or characteristaion. I was pretty unhappy about this. I might be stupid, but in one section, Caz [spoiler]is told to go fix a radio to contact a rescue ship[/spoiler] which everyone then promptly seems to forget about.

Most of the parkour puzzles are fun, but there is this one incredibly ♥♥♥♥♥♥ one that involves a sprint-jump on a floating platform that is nearly impossible without rebinding the sprint controls to hold instead of toggle. You'll know it when you die about 20 times.

[b]Overall:[/b]

Overall, whilst the game is a bit of a plot disappointment, it was a fun 4-5 hours and I would suggest picking it up on sale.

215024513.review:
This game has a great execution: Gorgeous environments and fun characters while being reasonably scary when it wants to be. It's technically proficient too. Especially the water rendering.

The most interesting aspect is clearly the setting: Almost all the action takes place on an isolated oil rig a few decades back during a freezing storm in the North Sea. Very fresh. Because of this all the interactable media and literature dotted around the rig (post cards, ledgers, menus, rotas, newspapers etc) are full of interest and can be often nostalgic. [spoiler]The appearance of a copy of William Shaws "The Trawlerman"[/spoiler] is easily my favourite beat in the entire story!

The major standout for most will probably be the dialogue, The game is loaded with strong Scottish accents, colloquialisms and rig jargon that treats you like an adult makes no attempt to hold your hand or compromise on who these people are and how they communicate. If you're not from this area of the UK or this line of work (which let's face it will be most of us!) it's going to be really fun to live in such a different environment for a while.

I wouldn't say it's very interesting for big beats. This is bog standard body horror mixed with metaphysical landscapes as the perceptions of our main character become unreliable. I.E. the same story you've probably played a thousand times before. Yawn.

And though I complimented the horror elements as "reasonably scary" just now I'd say in truth it's lacking in that regard.

I don't think Still Wakes the Deep pushes the envelope in many meaningful ways, and I don't think it'll be remembered by many down the line. But overall a good gift for someone and not necessarily a bad time!

214912879.review:
God, I hate when I leave negative reviews on relatively decent games, where the team does the best they could. But sometimes someone's best just isn't good enough. 

Still Wakes the Deep is technically a horror walking simulator. You are Kaz, a poor soul on an oiling rig, when you hit a Lovecraftian deity and all hell breaks loose. It is an interesting concept and the designers did a hell of a job providing this kind of stunning artwork. Apart from the art direction and the Voiceovers, the rest of the game fails worse than the rig itself. 

Firstly, the game is animation based and not system based. There is no health, no stamina, no anything. The whole game is walk through the level and interact with things, grab, climb etc. The greatest example are the heaters. In some levels you are going underwater and when you emerge, you feel cold so you need to find the heater to get some warmth. But there is no such survival system that tracks your temperature. It's just a gimmick, where you have to press a button to interact to give you the illusion of a proper lived world. 

The second is the story. I'm sure many found it passable and it kinda is, but it also has a lot of flaws. For 90% of the time you just go around in circles pulling levers and fixing things in order to escape. There is no narrative just "oh, this is broken, you have to fix it, so we can escape". Literaly, the game is mostly this, but decides to take a U-turn literaly 10 minutes before the end. 

The characters are memorable, but only in the later half of the game, where you actually trying together to survive. And the story... is not that good. While other horror walking simulators (e.g SOMA go play that one) has an interesting story, with deep themes and clever dialogue. Now it's just "Move forward and try to survive". There is nothing more to that, and the flashbacks of your wife, remain squandered potential. 

Eventually it is a mediocre game that had some ideas but eventually fails to deliver to its potential. In retrospect, the fact that the devs Chinese Room also tried to salvage the VtM Bloodlines 2, paints a clearer picture of a developer studio that wants to punch above its weight, but they just can't get there. 

A pity.

214908687.review:
Decided to dive in on this title for the crisp graphics and strong art/design direction. I wasn't disappointed in that respect. This "game" is an art piece worthy of around 6 hours of your time. Impactful, memorable, allbeit rushed and predictable. 

I really felt we could've used more story and connection with the various characters from the start. We're kinda just thrown into this chaos after an extremely brief intro. The ending felt both rushed and exhausting to get to. The pacing certainly could've been finessed further for a more complete experience. If we had gotten to know the crew more with collectibles or deeper interaction beforehand the weight of the tragedy would've felt heavier. As others have pointed out, this is a guided interactive movie with zero puzzles outside of a avoid-the-baddie gameplay loop. If this were embraced with more to discover/read I think it would be fine, but as it was it felt quite hollow. With that said, I am fully impressed by the title's visuals and believable banter... (could we chill with the f-bombs and constant blasphemies, though?) That's exactly why I wish the experience was deeper and more interactive. Puzzles, collectibles, conversation choices. Something to keep the player more engaged and immersed in the well-crafted world. 

Perhaps I'm biased, w/ Scottish blood myself, but we need more "games" like this exploring unique, realistic environments from remote, less-mainstream facets of European cultures with existential crises.

6.4/10

I may update this review after I go through the DLC.

Highly recommend when on sale like I got it.

214646635.review:
Devs should have described this "game" like this: "Hey, look at this oil rig that we've crafted in UE5. Come, let us show you around".
That is all to it.
This is the "game".

Q: Would I recommend?
A: If you like pretty-looking walking simulators that pretend to be a "game", or in this case, a "horror game", then yes. Though, wait until heavy discount.

[+]
1) Good directional sound design
I wish Total Chaos had a sound design like this. It would instantly be better...
2) Loading screens in UE5
No stutters, but for the game like this - it's immersion breaking, that's why the same comment goes to [-] section below.
3) Nicely crafted oil rig with good textures
4) Immersive environment and atmosphere
Mostly thanks to UE5 features.

[-]
1) Unoptimized
As expected from UE5 slop. I could barely get native 4K 60 FPS.
No stutters, though, thanks to the constant loading screens.
It is clearly designed with upscaling in mind... not using that, though.
Optimize the game, and you won't need to rely or even think about implementing upscaling!
Besides, if you are creating a game, make it playable, as all games should be (50-60 FPS is not playable at all nowadays).
2) Most Options cannot be changed to lower than High
3) Style over substance
Just a pretty-looking UE5 game
4) Monster design/textures are awful
Speaking of style over substance; clearly, devs were designing an oil rig walking simulator,
but than decided to add a monster,
which textures and design are horrible, undercooked, styleless trash.
There are some well-made "eaten" people, but "tentacles" or whatever it is, is just awful.
A monster, or a big part of it, is a glowing colorful ribbon-like tentacle...
No style, no depth, no gruesomeness, no nothing.
That's the best monster you could do in UE5?!
5) No in-game FPS limiter
6) No explanation on how to adjust the brightness
Very user-friendly.
7) Some buttons in Options do not work (Back button, for example)
8) Subtitles do not match the dialog
9) Abrupt (jumpy) camerawork during cutscenes
It even "teleports" sometimes.
10) Light-dimming on sudden movements
I think it's UE5 "feature". Global Illumination, maybe.
Personally, it's just annoying.
11) One of characters (Roy) hasn't moved a pixel from one scene to another
Which was quite hilarious.
12) Loading screens
In game like this, loading screens just break the immersion.
I think this choice was made to counter UE5 stutter (which it did), but it hurt the gameplay.
13) Buggy
Some bugs: cannot interact with things (doors, etc.), crashes sometimes.
14) Counterintuitive passages
Some passages (holes, cracks, etc.) are so small,
that I just skipped them, because I thought the character wouldn't be able to fit... but he did...
15) Plot-demanding closed doors
I doesn't make sense to have a locked doors on an oil rig. And I don't mean doors locked with a key, like private cabins, I mean passage doors with an actual lock on them... Nonsense.
Pure plot convenience.
16) Some objects height is off
Some objects are placed so low, that the character shouldn't be able to pass under without crouching... but he does.
17) Lame, boring story

Conclusion: 5/10

I really loved this game. The level design is amazing and the graphics are pretty nice overall. It’s very good, very scary, and honestly feels kind of special. Personally, I’ve always loved games about the sea and anything related to oil rigs. I like learning about them, watching videos, and just the whole vibe. Very mixed feelings about this.
Is it beautiful? Yes, absolutely. I'd love to get lost on this rig for a bit.
Is it well written? Yes, the dialogue flows nicely and has some character.
Did I regret playing this? No.
The story of what happened on this rig isn't bad either.

It's a pretty walking simulator with some body horror thrown in, but really the horror never landed for me. We are stumbling forward with a Lara Croft level plot armor through very impressive setpieces and it seems like the "thing" has a psychological compontent that influences... the result. But I never felt that was explored in an interesting way. Not at all for the side characters and barely for the main one. There's a huge disconnect between the characters regrets and what's going on on the rig.

Gameplay is forgettable, its some climbing and crawling in a set corridor, maybe wait for a monster here and there. Any interaction just exists to string you along.
To be a must play I think it either needs to explore its characters more in a non linear non force fed way or have way more engaging systems.

214422600.review:
This is a qualified recommendation. The game itself is decent with some good artistic vision and some pretty good animation. I did enjoy playing it for the most part and I could tell a lot of thought went into the game and its story. However, the controls are a bit sluggish and the physics combined with them can be a bit buggy. I had to restart from a checkpoint at least once because the game got stuck on a prompt that was no longer available and sometimes finding the sweet spot for a prompt to come up took some wiggling.

There is a point toward the end of the game where it shows you a standard prompt... but the game also forces you to fail the prompt. There is no indication of this, though, and I was restarting over and over thinking I was up against a bug. This combined with some confusing signposting in a game that has yellow paint (that you can turn off if you so choose) made navigation a bit frustrating. When you have to run from monsters but you get stuck in a corner not knowing where you're allowed to go, it gets a bit frustrating.

Story was decent and if you have a passing familiarity with Lovecraftian stories then you know exactly what's going on and what's going to happen. There is some play for emotions which goes over much, much better than in those stories but you're not going to be shocked by any of it. It did keep me playing, though, so I guess it works!

The game is roughly 4-6 hours depending on how many times you die and that's honestly long enough for a walking sim. I enjoyed it, mostly, but it had a few too many technical flaws for me to really recommend it to anyone but fans of the walking sim or Lovecraft-style story fans who are tired of bad third person shooters.

214183716.review:
This is a game where you won't be able to defend yourself, no weapons, you will need to avoid mutated human enemies. I want to say that there are three chase events. Games like this usually make me anxious, but avoiding enemies isn't tedious and you can often distract the enemies to distance them from you. For the chase events, just keep moving forward and you will succeed. If you die, you will restart before the chase begins, so it's not tedious if you struggle.

The game generally performed well, I did hit an enemy killed me, then couldn't get past an area in the room where I died (kept dying automatically), but exiting to the main menu and then reloading fixed the issue. The game auto saves often, so you don't typically lose much progress if you hit an issue.

I completed the game in less than 7 hours. There isn't much exploring, you're kept on a tight path.

214179092.review:
Захватывающая атмосфера и выживание на краю света

Я провел в этой игре всего 4 часа, но впечатлений — на целый сезон хоррор-сериала.

Что понравилось:

Невероятная, давящая атмосфера. Разрабам удалось создать ощущение полной изоляции, холода и надвигающегося ужаса на нефтяной вышке. Звуковой дизайн и визуальные эффекты работают на все 100%.

Сильная нарративная составляющая. История затягивает с первых минут. Очень человечная драма на фоне сверхъестественного кошмара.

Геймплей, сосредоточенный на immersion. Здесь нет боя в привычном понимании — только бегство, скрытность и решение environmental-головоломок. Это заставляет чувствовать себя по-настоящему беззащитным, что идеально вписывается в концепцию хоррора.

Что стоит знать: Это не экшн, а скорее "walking simulator" с элементами стелса и выживания. Если вы ждете динамичных схваток, их тут не будет. Иногда механика может показаться немного однообразной.

Вердикт: Безусловно рекомендую любителям атмосферных, нарративных хорроров в духе Amnesia или SOMA. Это напряженное, короткое и очень качественное приключение, которое оставляет след.

214172470.review:
Love psyhcological horror games. The clausterphobic parts with water, especially while being in danger, were stressful in a good type of way that works to the benefit of the developers trying to make the player feel unwell. 

9/10. I do think that the laundry room AI needed to be tuned better and for it to be more obvious at the conclusion of that bit that you did not fail the stealth check but rather it was scripted to auto-find you as you reached the exit. The speed of the AI when that happens, within feet of the exit, made me think I was doing it wrong. Over and over. 

I really liked the Muir section. I actually think the writers/devs forgot about him, because hes the only one we don't encounter again later. We have a first confrontation section with each named enemy and then a final confrontation where we outsmart or outrun them, resulting in their defeat. Muir only has the introduction and then is never seen again. 

If you could flesh this concept out into small-medium island with several points of interest nearby (oil rig/derrick, abandon commercial fishing vessels, large cargo ship, cruise ship, etc.) It could feel very sandboxy. 

The passage of time can be used as a scarrier thing for players as well. It happens rather linearly throughout the game but I recall at least once wondering "if I stay here will it eventually turn night and make this way harder?" 
Flashlight not working when pointed at the eldritch anomalies makes me wonder why that is but I suppose it is very lovecraftian for something to be an unknown evil beyond human comprahension, even if that is just to wonder why a flash light dims when pointed at them. The environments lights don't seem to do that when they're around. Adding that as a thing would also be really cool.

I had fun with it. Only took around 6 hours though.

214109855.review:
This is an immersive walking simulator with a very detailed and interesting setting. If that is all you are looking for, you will be satisfied.

Problem is the story and pacing. This game presents a lot of characters to you, without really giving you a reason to care about them, except maybe the wife of the main character. I mean, sure they are your coworkers and good friends, but in a game like this, that does not cut it when they... ahem.. SPOILERS THEY DIE.

Let's take SOMA for example. Catherine is the only "human" left, which makes us care about her when trying to navigate the horrors in that game. In Still Wakes the Deep, everyone is actually useless. Sure, the game tells us they are helping, but doesn't show it. 

This game is also weird because the dialogue is played 100% straight, but everything that happens in the game is extremely cheesy and borders on action schlock at some points. Just a very strange mixture of vibes. Your character also may be the slowest sprinter I've ever seen, which is weird because of how much action is in this game (for a walking sim).

This game is quite short, and I think the game would have benefitted immensely if they leaned into fleshing out characters and relationships more. The ending was certainly a choice, but I wouldn't have minded if character deaths had more impact.

Anyway, long rant for a solid 6.5/10. Definitely worth it if you're fiending for a highly immersive walking sim that looks amazing and has good dialogue.

214107115.review: Contrived, painfully yellow-coded, extremely gamey environment that conveys absolutely no sense of immersion

214105755.review:
Was enjoying it up until the Muir encounter.  Be aware, this encounter this encounter can be buggy AF.  Reaching the first crate (where there are collapsible wooden boxes on the other end) , you're supposed to be able to reach the 2nd crate where Muir will toss it around and cause the crate to open on the other end.  If you have a throwable upon entering the first crate, you can easily make it to the second crate, *HOWEVER*, Muir doesn't interact with the 2nd crate and you become blocked.  

If you die (which you need to) it will place you back at the first crate.  Which is fine, except Muir seems to bug out.  IF its bugged you won't be able to interact with the doors on the crate you're in (that way you'll know if its bugged).  To get around this, hit ESC and do a forced "Restart at checkpoint" and it will go back to normal.

This Muir encounter is really, really buggy.

Overall, this game misses the mark by a wide margin....until the very end.  The ending drops a ton of emotion and depth.  But the rest of the game?  No emotion, no depth, just literally get from point-A, to point-B to point-C without any meaning, any connection, very little revalence to the rest of the crew.  This could have been avoided if there were pickups (letters, notes etc) or interactions with the other crew members.  It's shallow (very shallow) until the end.  And, while the ending is is good and emotional and tough to deal with, all the activity leading up to it feels like lost opportunity.

There has been no game I've played recently where I just wanted it to be over so I could move on to something else. 

Worth playing for a discounted/heavily discounted price just for the ending but man, its a slog.

214103416.review:
The whole point of the so-called "walking simulator" genre, of which The Chinese Room virtually defined unilaterally, is to make you feel something. The genius of 'Dear Esther' was that it redefined the purpose of this medium we call video games. No point system, no achievements; only poetry. 'Amnesia' was similarly ground-breaking in that the horror you felt came mostly from your own mind's interpolation; what the player believed was happening, not what the developer was spoon-feeding. These golden years have sadly passed, as made apparent by 'Still Wakes The Deep."

There are many things done right in this title-- most of which can be seen in its trailer. The setting and atmosphere are fantastic. Wood paneling, frigid isolation, conduits, rivets and steel penetrating the ocean. Only the most masculine of men could survive such a place in 1975, sans sea monster. I know I couldn't. 

The music, sound design, voice acting and artwork are all top tier as well. The experience is extremely immersive, as one would expect. But, as with other forms of post-gamergate media, the writing has been ideologically sanitized for the "modern audience." 

This is not to say that I'm opposed to games that have a political message. For example, Kojima is a brilliant artist whose politics I largely disagree with and are woven throughout his ludography. No, I'm talking about writing that acts as an eraser. Writing that opposes even a semblance of merit or unequal outcomes... The proverbial HR department of the games industry. Do you suppose there was an HR department on an oil rig off the coast of Scotland in 1975? 

This is where the immersion ends. 

If I may expound: It's hard to believe that an androgynous, overweight female (surely a they/them half a century before ideologues placed pronouns in their online bios) technician could handle roving 20th century Scottish roughnecks. And perhaps this is where one of the writers would retort, "Actually, we did our research and discovered that a woman did work..." but this is precisely my point. Why are you working so hard to wedge your round peg into a square hole? It's okay for things to exist outside of your paradigm. What value does this add to the story?

Other signs of sanitization for modern audiences: 
- Ethnic diversity during a time when Scotland was so ethnically homogenous their government didn't even collect that data.
- Not a single poster of a naked women. (Must I say it again? It's an oil rig in 1975)
- The protagonist grumbling about a nationalist crewman
- DEI department in credits

And then there's the story. It did start out quite strong. I felt empathy for the protagonist, remained curious about his background and enjoyed learning about his relationships with other workers. The pacing leading up to the horror elements of the game was also excellent. 

Perhaps my prior criticisms don't resonate with you. Great! This could be a game you'd enjoy. In fact, that's the mentality I had-- After all, I can ignore a certain amount of post gamergate drivel, if the rest of the game is good enough. The problem with 'Still Wakes The Deep,' is that it isn't. 

Outside of the earie bowels of the oil platform and the relentless waves beneath it, the horror elements are not that horrifying. What's worse is that the game seems to want you to believe it's on to something profound as it progresses, but ultimately fails to deliver; having to fall back on existing tropes. This all could have been redeemed by a provocative ending or twist, but this too never comes to fruition.

The Chinese Room started out with a small team of less than four people. Today... Well, the credits rolled for what seemed like ten minutes as I sat, calculating the value of my time. I don't know if its possible for developers to make great works of art as they scale. This game serves as evidence that it isn't.

214083867.review:
I really tried to like this game. Unfortunately, I just can’t.

As a huge fan of the Amnesia series (yes, except A Machine for Pigs) and SOMA, I was really looking forward to this. Sadly, I can hardly find any positives here.

The first 30 minutes are genuinely great. The oil rig feels alive, the atmosphere works, and you can really soak it in. And that’s it. The best part of the game is already over before anything truly strange even begins. The Lovecraftian premise - “what happens if we dig too deep” — sounds promising, but it never really delivers.

Negatives:

Logic leaves the game early.
At the beginning, you survive an absolutely unsurvivable event… only to die later from fall damage after jumping off a bed. The game constantly pushes you into invisible walls with lazy messages like “it’s not time to go there yet”. No physical barriers, no environmental storytelling - just invisible walls.
Elsewhere, you pull a lever and suddenly a blocked path is magically cleared, even though no one was around and nothing visibly changed. You can break one fence, but an identical-looking fence right next to it “won’t budge”. Consistency is nonexistent.

The horror is gone.
The main antagonist is fine - Lovecraftian, abstract, acceptable. But the actual creature that hunts you? Not a single goosebump during my entire playthrough.

Gameplay never shows up.
Houston, we have a problem.
Turn a valve.
Climb a ladder.
Turn another valve.
Hide from the monster.
Repeat.

For about four hours straight. Occasionally you meet a friendly NPC, only for them to behave in the most unreasonable way possible. I pushed myself to finish the game out of curiosity, but the ending was just… boring.

Lack of detail and exploration.
You are stuck in a single corridor. If you reach a junction and choose the wrong path, you’ll hit a dead end within seconds. There is absolutely nothing to explore. No environmental details, almost no lore, no meaningful notes - just a few tables with messages like “if you don’t pull this lever, you die.”
Such a massive waste of potential.

One last thing:
The game has an achievement for throwing an object into water - unlocked by 5,7% of players.
How hard is it to throw an object into water… on a foo-king oil rig? That says it all.

214069518.review:
The voice acting is incredible and really helps sell the story and tension. The atmosphere on the oil rig is very good — it feels oppressive, creepy, and unsettling the whole way through. The enemy designs are also really well done, disturbing without being overused and fitting the tone perfectly.

The main downside is that it’s a bit short, so I’d definitely recommend picking it up on sale rather than full price. That said, the game has an ending you won’t expect, and it left a strong impression once it was over.

Overall, a solid, memorable horror experience that’s worth playing, especially if you enjoy story-driven games.

Recommended.

SWTD is a perfect little gem of a game, although using the term 'game' to describe it might be a bit of a stretch.

Interactive story might be a better fit; for the most part, you're moving around.  Sometimes, you're hiding.  And occasionally, you're doing generously-timed quicktime events.  

The story is classic horror, so don't bank on the narrative surprising you much, if you're a fan of the genre and its conventions, but SWTD does it justice, across the boards.

Everything is beautifully depicted, visually and aurally; the setting, the characters, the 'monster.'  This is not the kind of game I'm drawn to, and honestly, I kind of hate hide mechanics, but having just finished it, I'm sad to see it go.  It's a one-playthrough kind of experience, but I wholeheartedly recommend it, and if you're trying to get someone into horror into games, this might be a good starting point.  

Also, there's unintentionally hilarity in the Scottish dialogue and how absolutely different the subtitles are to cater (presumably) to non-Scots.

213973421.review: This is my first time ever writing a review on Steam, or for any video game for that matter. I LOVE this game! While I agree that it is not for everybody, it is definitely for a small minority of gamers that look forward to short, linear, story-driven video games (me). I cannot for the life of me get into tedious and grindy open-world games that require hours, days, and possibly even years to progress. I hate having to do outside research on gameplay mechanics. I do not want to make a video game my next JOB and I do not want to commit an insane amount of time to it. This game was the perfect length for me, only taking about 7 hours to beat the main campaign. The graphics were some of the best graphics I have seen to this day. The voice acting was absolutely phenomenal! The story was very intriguing, although it clearly took inspiration from Dead Space. Oddly enough, it actually scared me at certain points and kept me at the edge of my seat - something that most horror movies, books, and games just don't do for me anymore. This is my first time playing a "walking simulator," but I enjoyed it quite a lot, and it may have opened a door to a new genre of games that I will probably enjoy! I think the only con I have for this game is the sprint mechanic... It does not respond fast enough and was a bit hard to use when it came to certain parkour aspects of the game. Other than that, loved the game and I look forward to playing the DLC!

213771271.review:
I'm not a massive fan of The Chinese Room, I find their games to be lacking quite a bit and what games that are celebrated I find to be quite overrated. Dear Ester in 2008 was interesting and, for a mod, really looked incredible but it lacked everywhere else for me to get drawn int it. Amensia Machine for Pigs was pretty bad and not what people were expecting and 2025's VTMB2 was just terrible - but it was in development hell for some time and they were brought in quite late to the party.

This game changed my opinion for the most part. There's no pandering, it's still quite grounded and depicts real life people of the era, scottish men who are genuinely scottish not mismatching an entire culture with a "inclusive" spin on it to cater to every background known to man.

The voice acting is some of the best I've come across to date. They really nailed it. Genuinely thought I would find it annoying listening to very thick Scottish accents for seven hours but it was so well done and the writing was incredibly well written I enjoyed every second of it.

The story is pretty good, nothing spectacular but you get the gyst of it. It doesn't smack you over the head about what happened or why the main character is there but you can come to your own conclusion as to why he's there. They also stick the landing with the story, no more long drawn out twist that "it was all in your head" or anything of the like. It's just a standard "This is a horror story, this is happening" and it's not something made up in the MC's mind or a metaphore for something about some modern dayism.

I was AFK for about an hour or two, played the DLC(Which was pretty decent tbh although it's getting some flak for a reason I don't quite understand) and probably only have about 7-8 hours played including the DLC. It was really good for what it was. If you're in the mood for a single afternoon playthrough this is your game.

I'll also be honest: This isn't scary. You've seen all this before and the majority of scares are scripted to the point as long as you run forward you'll very rarely fail. It's also incredibly linear but that's not an issue with a walking simulator in my opinion. You're really there for the story and pacing and it does both incredibly well.

213760192.review:
I first took note of Still Wakes the Deep shortly before its release. The setting and general atmosphere looked very promising, but due to a lot of less-than-favorable reviews, I held off on playing it until now. After playing it, I definitely understand the criticism but have to say that I personally wasn't bothered by any of the game's perceived shortcomings. 

Yes, the game is very short, but the story doesn't feel like it was left unfinished. Sure, they could have drawn it out over another 3-4 hours, but in my eyes that would have only harmed the pacing of the game.

Yes, the game is incredibly linear to the point that you can really only go maybe a few meters off route before you hit a dead end, but I personally didn't mind that. Abstaining from optional quests or a myriad of collectibles allows the player to get fully immersed in the story and its very well-written and voiced characters.

And yes, this is basically a walking simulator. While you have a couple of stealth or chase sequences, the rest of the gameplay comes down to what are essentially quick-time events. Again, as someone who likes walking simulators, this didn't bother me in the slightest, but I know it will bother others.

To mention some positives, the game's setting, atmosphere, and visual style are all immaculate. Characters are voiced brilliantly, and the story manages to be engaging at all times.

So in conclusion, Still Wakes the Deep is a bit of an acquired taste. As long as you are fine with the previously mentioned characteristics, I can not recommend this game enough. However, if it feels like you wouldn't enjoy what I described above, then I would either pass on this or keep my expectations low.

[h2]BASE GAME[/h2]
played through still wakes the deep with a buddy on our horror game rotation. being in or around open ocean is probably my #1 nightmare scenario, and it delivered in spades. the writing and voice acting are immaculate, the vibe is unmatched, and i am so far outside of my lane i was consistently worried for my sanity

213511712.review:
Atmospheric horror game built in UE5 with raytraced shadows/reflections is predictably a very bad idea.
The amount of visual "distractions" thanks to this stupid engine is frankly remarkable : I cannot walk 5 meters without noticing another boiling reflection or pixelated shadow and you of course cannot turn it off. Keep in mind that I am playing in ultra quality with DLAA so this is as good as it gets apparently..

There's no distinctive visual style : this literally looks like any other UE5 "game" except with too much grain other post processing effects.
What is the point of having a photorealistic render if you're then just going to slap badly raytraced reflections/shadows and ugly post processing effects on top of it ?
Did you not notice your game looked like an impressionist painting at any point during development or do you simply not care ??

On top of it all, some absolute genius decided to add a camera effect whose only purpose is to create a giant jagged line on your screen (as far as I can tell at least). I genuinely though it was a DLAA/DLSS bug at first but after some testing I'm now almost sure its a "stylistic choice".
What is the point of having anti aliasing and then reintroduce jaggies artificially ??!
Something is wrong with these people, I swear to god.

This is a technical disaster but apparently no one cares about that anymore.
If you can deliver something that looks like that and then find enough mouth breathers to give you a very positive rating on Steam anyway, why would you even try ?
I can hardly blame them at this point, this is our collective fault as an audience for consistently rewarding mediocrity.
