213312054.review:
Interesting premise with poor execution. The game is let down by repetitive, stale gameplay, uninspiring and unchallenging QTE sequences, and the occasional cheap death. 

I was drawn to this game because of its cool setting, and great voice acting, but ultimately the game's challenges arise more from navigating clunky platforming and general level design than relying on quick reflexes as you'd expect from most action-based horror games. None of the action scenes had my adrenaline up as with other games of the genre, and the progression through various chapters felt samey and linear.

I was already getting bored after 3 hours because the entire rig looks the same, the gameplay is just a series of 'go here and pull a lever' tasks, but decided to give up after reaching the tedious pontoon section after drowning when I couldn't find an opening amid the turgid grey-brown set pieces.

If you're a big fan of holding a button to slowly go down a ladder, and sometimes holding another button to fulfil a QTE prompt, in between bouts of some shoddily designed platforming sections where your main task will be finding out which piece of nondescript jagged metal rail you need to reach, this may be the game for you. If however you're a seasoned survival horror fan, you sadly may find this a rather dull affair with lots of well-trodden ground.

There's a lot to like about this game. The characters are well fleshed out and distinct, it looks good in many places, it has a compelling premise and setting, the voice acting is excellent and it's well written. This is all let down by sluggish and dated gameplay and uninspired level design, which is a real shame. I was disappointed - really wanted to like this one but I just didn't.

213110332.review: There is a lot to like in this original yet predictable game. The oil rig is rendered in excellent detail you get a sense of the harsh conditions and camaraderie that needed to exist amongst the workforce to cope with the isolation and the risk. There's a lot of social commentary about class, exploitation and masculinity that accurately reflects Scottish social structure in the 70s and 80s. The voice work is very good and distracts from the limitations of the game which are the linear and repetitive game play. Its quite clunky in places and quite slow but the atmosphere is well created and sustained. You can see very clear influences from Dead Space, Alien Isolation and The last Of Us.  The main character of Caz has a huge amount of empathy and sadness about the fate of his workmates and the realisation of how disposable and irrelevant they are to the management of the oil rig. There are still relevant political themes and social commentary here but also more fantasy based existential dread as people repeatedly face the risk of death against the elements. Frustratingly I wish it had been slicker and more polished, with more combat and less protracted cutaways after each death. The ending was quite downbeat and again this felt earned due to the excellent voice work-which has gone on to win awards. I hope they remake this game in a few years as the oil rig setting and premise has a lot of untapped potential.

213100707.review: Genuinely one of the best games ever made. Every time, without fail, this game makes me cry. If you ever want a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ beautiful story that gives you enough nerves to make you nearly gag, this is it. Please give it a shot or at least watch someone play it if you haven't.

Still Wakes the Deep instantly became one of my new favourite horror games. The atmosphere is absolutely incredible—being trapped on an oil rig in the middle of the ocean creates this constant feeling of isolation and tension that the game nails perfectly.

What I loved most is how easy the game is to follow. You don’t have to overthink puzzles or mechanics; you can just sit back, relax, and immerse yourself in a genuinely good horror experience. It’s smooth, cinematic, and keeps you engaged from start to finish.

The Siren’s Rest DLC is a great addition too. It keeps the same vibe and expands the story in a way that feels natural and satisfying. Both the main game and DLC deliver memorable moments, amazing atmosphere, and that perfect mix of calm and dread.

If you enjoy atmospheric horror that’s easy to follow but incredibly immersive, I highly recommend Still Wakes the Deep and Siren’s Rest.

It looks pretty good on the whole, the setting is unique and it has pretty consistently good voice acting. Going in blind, I didn't really expect it to take the direction it did, but I wasn't put off by that and enjoyed it's inspiration from a certain 1982 movie.

On the downside, it really is very close to just being a walking sim, with very repetitive traversal around the rig, too much yellow paint and too many on screen prompts.

On balance, I would say it's worth playing with a big discount but gets a 5/10 from me.

10/10 award for best screwdriver animation though.

212965611.review:
Bought this game at a discount along with the DLC. Beautifully heartbreaking horror game. 

Visuals are stunning and overwhelming, especially near the end of the main story. I noticed that I would stand around and just look at the rig, the violent waves of the ocean, the monstrosities that surround the place and be amazed. The DLC looks just as good, with really clever way of flipping their assets (like, literally flipping structures from the rig itself all over the place lmao). The use of lighting or lack of it under the sea was executed well, trenches and wreckages look intimidating and eery. Bottom line is, the ocean is still terrifying, I don't know what else to tell you lmao

Sound design is great, and barely anything sounded out of place. There's always something big crashing against another, The use of echoes with voices, infastructures and throwable objects in hollow or large areas are satisfying to hear. Equipment and machinery around the rig are loud like they're supposed to be and the monsters are audibly terrifying to listen to. I love how larger-than-life everything sounded, and it really makes you feel small with all the big gears turning and unfolding around you while you anxiously make your way through the rig and the ocean trying to find some sensible, audible answers. Soundtrack isn't too exciting, but very clear cut for the horror genre.

Gameplay is probably the weakest link. There's simple platforming, but there's the occassional QTE's that actually made me lock in during tense, life-or-death moments in-game. Exploration is linear, but I love looking at the little details of every crew's cabin and every corner of the rig. DLC exploration is much more linear, but feels more daunting (its the ocean lmao)

FInally, the story. Again, its simple. But the voice acting is phenomenal. Everyone sounds like actual people who've lived these lives. There's years of wear-and-tear in their voices, and fit the setting so well. Story can be boiled down to a mix of The Thing and practically any Alien entry or sci-fi/cosmic, body horror movie. However, to me, the characters are where the game shines. Crew member curse at each other like there's no tomorrow. But when things do go wrong, those who remain come together to form an impossible solution to a hopeless dilemma.  There's some emotional cutscenes that made me keep delving deeper into the horrors and made me genuinely curious as to how it will end. DLC was short but sweet, and my words above can be applied here, too.

TLDR; A short, simple horror game with great visuals, pleasing and nerve-wrecking sound design and fantastic voice acting for a simple but effective, emotional story. Buying it at a discount definitely made me enjoy it more, but I think its definitely a gem of a horror game.

212617147.review:
[h2]A good idea that deserved to either be a limited series, or a survival horror sandbox - but not this janky, mismatched, extremely short yet shockingly repetitive mess.[/h2]

[b]Still Wakes The Deep[/b] had an awesome pre-release minigame named [b]Oil Strike '75[/b] that I didn't even know existed until the credits rolled on the finished game, including mention of it.

Oil Strike '75, a retro minigame collection and teletext emulator full of secrets, presented as a sort of self-contained ARG, is a [i]significantly[/i] better game than Still Wakes The Deep, and I wish it had been promoting a better game. I recommend you go and play it, it's intriguing, funny, and slightly spooky. I do not recommend SWTD, and I'll explain why as best I can below, without spoilers except those you choose to click to reveal:

SWTD begins as a compellingly authentic presentation of a grimy, damp, poorly managed North Sea oil drilling rig in the mid 1970s. It manages to artfully convey the real mood of the working class 1975, eschewing Disco for poverty and political unrest, little hints of the normalised tensions of the mid-late Cold War visible in the interactions between the rig's crew - before things go wrong.

And ♥♥♥♥ do they go wrong, but not at all the way I hoped. As soon as the game begins to hint at Bad Things and impending horror you're already seeing troubling signs in terms of design and a lack of immersion - even with 'reduced' environmental prompts turned on, bright yellow paint marks ledges, ladders, grilles and vents everywhere. After a pleasantly interactive first ten minutes with light-switches, personal letters and other environmental details, at a certain very early point these cut off entirely, replaced exclusively with a small set of props that can only be thrown. What could that mean. 

You begin to notice that there are unexplained crouch-height crawlspaces absolutely everywhere in any large, obviously set-piece room, and that your sprint, if you look down at your feet, doesn't synchronise with your character animations at all, your feet sliding wildly as your character suddenly gasps and chokes and squeaks with exertion while the 'sprint' is more like an asthmatic's power-walk.

Horrors ensue, and the dark promise comes to pass; you're in a stealth maze/locker hide/throw distraction object Dead By Daylight-like, an Alien Isolation Lite. You are in Quick Time Event hell.

The entire game is between 3 and 6 hours long depending upon how curious you are about environmental exploration in the first area, before all depth, detail and choices cease to exist, and you will spend 90% of the latter span either: 
[list]
[*] locked into static dialogue sequences that not only prevent you moving but even control your mouse, wrestling it away constantly to stare in an arbitrary direction that rarely if ever contains anything of interest
[*] jogging or walking very slowly through large, murky rooms full of accidental environmental storytelling -the environment is full of yellow-painted ledges, Half-Life-sized megavents that simply create a five metre loop back into the same room for AI dodging - and the story they tell is that you'll be consistently [spoiler]ambushed by a single monster in each set-piece room after you complete an objective and have to vent-wiggle/distraction-throw/just sprint back through it[/spoiler], a story that only gets worse with each telling.
[*] very slowly climbing, ledge-shimmying, ledge-clambering, and otherwise following prompts to grip, climb, grip, over and over again around the inner or outer surfaces of what could have been a fascinating, atmospheric oil rig but was turned into an amateurish copy of an Assassin's Creedesque climbing frame. You will predict every single ledge having another curiously timed distant explosion that causes you to fall off, forcing you to QTE again to catch the ledge - but you can simply hold these buttons down permanently so you never have to worry, and just keep moving through the ranting and panting and swearing of our poor player-character.
[/list]

There's nothing else. You will QTE, QTE, and QTE. You will loop tiny vent sequences while creatures shout a scant few repeated phrases at you. You will sprint when the stealth sequence is done, with no tension, as the monster follows you at a slow, scripted pace that can only catch you if you simply stop moving. You will climb, holy ♥♥♥♥ you will climb and climb some more. You will fall off repeatedly and catch yourself in QTE after QTE.

The monster concepts are great, though the execution is poor - they are essentially [spoiler]a piece of meat that floats around with very jiggly physics, within the illusion that the tendrils they dynamically spit in all directions are pulling them along or holding them aloft. There's some variation - one smaller example crawls along the floor a bit more in tighter environments - while one early example that is actually the most intimidating, Muir, has knobbly legs and a more convincingly aggressive mode of locomotion and speech. [/spoiler]

[spoiler]Another is just a head that could have been deeply scary in its surrealism but that moves so slowly and in such a scripted manner it feels more comical than frightening - and more like a weak attempts to mimic certain aesthetics from the upcoming game ILL or the warped giantism of GTFO or Attack on Titan.[/spoiler]

Ultimately each of the monsters [spoiler]has a blatantly obvious scripted zone in which it can move and is either a strictly scripted pursuit over a short set of corridors until you reach a door or tight gap, or it occupies a small, obvious maze section within a single large room and you bypass it by throwing any of the large number of hammers, spanners, helmets, aerosols or cups scattered all over the floor, then crawling into a couple of vents. No tension, no challenge, and sadly the visual effects fail very quickly as the creatures have little weight or presence, constantly floating through walls, swinging their jiggling limbs through the floors and ceilings, or entirely walking through walls when they glitch, obviously just... a balloon with jiggling physics and a few recorded lines, never feeling like a threatening AI presence.[/spoiler]

Between the godawful QTE-ridden climbing and button-pushing sequences that feel like filler even in a game only a few hours long, and the forced, scripted stealth maze-vs-corridor sprint in lieu of either genuine stealth-based gameplay with real AI agents, or any form of combat or resisting the antagonistic forces, it feels like good narrative design and creative direction were absolutely ♥♥♥♥♥♥ over by designers who couldn't decide whether to copy Alien Isolation, , Dead By Daylight or Outlast - or continue the bland horror structure of the Amnesia series they previously worked on - and ultimately created an ultra-lightweight, janky, poorly animated mess, so simple and repetitive that any fear or tension the game could have generated is outweighed by the mild irritation and growing boredom of repeating very mundane tasks very slowly, with occasional checkpoint restarts if you miss a jump or fail to pay attention to a QTE prompt.

[b]Still Wakes The Deep[/b] deserved to be a better game. Real care and imagination went into the concept, while the gameplay design was not only completely unoriginal but wholly embraced the worst qualities of multiple genres, while being too simply for them to have a reason to be there.

TL;DR - A compelling setting and concept ruined by awful false stealth gameplay and filler QTE platforming, weakly gluing together perhaps one hour worth of narrative, without any exploration freedom, without any choices, and without any thinking or decision-making whatsoever. What could have been a good horror film wasted on being a very poorly made, shallow patchwork of a very short game that can barely be described as an interactive experience, and certainly neither a fun nor a frightening one.

211790109.review:
Usually, after I 100% a game, I don’t really ever feel like going back to it, but Still Wakes the Deep is different to me. In the visuals department, I could not be stopped from taking, in all likelihood, far too many screenshots. The monsters are disgustingly awesome looking, and the rig itself carries a terrifying air about it, even before it's engulfed by a storm of red and purple. I really appreciate how the other characters, specifically Brody and Finley, are just as active as you, helping to reduce the feeling of main character syndrome that most games struggle from, and I enjoy how cohesive the rig feels when passing through the same section several times in varying states of decay. I think the achievements are easy enough, but still fun to get, and overall, I would always be down to replay it. 

As for the DLC, while it is objectively less engaging than the main game, I actually find that the route they went with the character’s story is quite good, and I think anyone who enjoyed the main game, even just to a minor extent, should play the game. Just keep your expectations in check and enjoy it for the diving game it tries to be.

Also, remember to keep in mind that this is a story-based game. If you are interested in those truly heartfelt, thought-provoking stories to experience through the gaming medium, this is for you. That being said, the game does not lack interaction with the world or challenge from its gameplay, only any real choice on the player’s part.

211716297.review:
It´s kinda...boring crap. Yes, the voice acting is great, and the setting is fantastic.

SPOILERS

I don´t get all the glowing reviews praising its story and characters however. You don´t get enough time to bond with the side characters. So whenever Caz, the protagonist, sobs over a dead crewmate "Aaak nooo! Wee li´ll Stanny, who I spoke to for 2 minutes in the prologue, turned into flesh soup! Tis nae fair!", it feels forced and, ultimately, unimportant.

Even when your fat, diabetic best mate Roy dies, you get this by the numbers, underwhelming emotional moment. This happens at least 5, 6 times throughout the game. It´s neither good writing nor good character development. 

Now the story, or lack thereof: Your missus is sick of your shenanigans, you f off to an oil rig to dodge an assault conviction, and after 20 minutes of ´gameplay´ the rig is already overrun by a -mostly- visually underwhelming eldritch entity. Par for some "aaack, by me bagpipes, dis is true horror!" remarks from the protagonist, you get no further clues nor lore nor exploration of whatever entity is eating this massive oil rig. 

Also, dear developers: nothing breaks immersion as fast as having to see the ´PRESS R2 TO CLIMB, YOU DOWN SYNDROMED PERSON´-prompt over. And over. And over again. You have an option to turn of -most- of the gameplay hints...except the one you see the most.


The movie "The Thing" explored the paranoia of a stranded crew in the middle of nowhere eerily well, and showed the true horror of the alien entity in all of it´s disgusting glory. 

This game ain´t no The Thing, and any comparison is an insult to the 1982 bonafide horror classic.

211009508.review:
This game is a compelling experience, one that held me completely captive from start to finish—I played the entire game in a single session. Its greatest strength lies in its stunningly realized atmosphere and its central premise: the potent fear of being trapped and isolated in the vast, unforgiving ocean. While the game may not rely on jump scares for its horror, the psychological tension derived from this concept of inescapable confinement is fantastic.

The Missed Immersion:

Unfortunately, a key element of the setting feels underdeveloped. Given that the game is set specifically in the 1970s, it fails to fully leverage this time period. To create a deeper, richer sense of time and place, I would have loved to see more immersive, period-specific artifacts, such as:

In-game documents and newspapers mentioning contemporary events.

Radio broadcasts featuring 70s news reports.

Fully embracing the era would make the environment more immersive.

210412254.review:
Holy hell, if there weren't subtitles in this game, I wouldn't understand half of what these people would be saying.

Joking aside, the game is much better than I thought it would be. It doesn't quite reach the heights of games like SOMA or other horror classics, but the writing for its length is excellent, the characters are well acted and the Oil rig is a fantastic setting for a horror game. I feel like aquatic horror is a bit under-utilized in the genre.

The games audio and visuals are one of the best parts of the experience. They really help to establish this atmosphere of being stuck in the middle of the ocean with no rescue in sight. Even at start of the game where things are still fine, the game still has this claustrophobic feeling with its long, tight corridors and cramped rooms. The rig is obviously on its last legs or at least in dire need of a fix up. All the sound effects, the creaking of metal, distant screams, the waves and water flooding the rig, its all very well done. The voice acting is also excellent, all the characters feel alive and like real people you'd meet working a 9-5 at a job site. 

While I found the characters great for the most part, I feel that the game didn't give me time to really get to know them much. I feel the s##t hits the fan a bit too fast to really establish things like character relations and then I'm expected to feel for characters I barely know. I still feel for them a little, but I feel like the more emotional moments would've hit a bit better if I was given more time with them.
The gameplay is alright, I wasn't expecting much either way. It's your standard stealth-horror romp a.la Amnesia minus the physics based puzzles. I did like the light parkour/climbing elements while travelling through the rig since it makes sense due to the place falling apart. It doesn't feel tacked on like in some games. 

I do feel that the game does suffer a little from its shorter length, which is a bit of stable of horror games. I got the game for cheap, for about 9€. However, I feel that the asking price of 35€ for about 5 hours of content is a bit much. If you're someone who plays games with a kind of price-to-hours ratio in mind, the price is a bit hard to justify. But if you're someone who likes games like SOMA or games that I would classify as "workplace-horror" I'd say this is a worthwhile pick.

209602850.review:
This game is interesting. The graphics and environment are gorgeous and always having a fondness for the ocean and the dark mythos inspired by it this game was already hitting my pleasure points. I love how the subtitles translate Scottish slang for the average viewer, just a nice touch. And the story for this game is spectacular and haunting. Walking through the rig reminded me of my time working on ships and piers and reminded me of my love for the sea. 
It's a great game that I cannot recommend it in good faith.
This unfortunately has all the aspects of a game you'd expect from The Chinese Room. 
The gamer is incredibly linear and given the setting you are quite literally on rails the entire time. Never do any of the puzzles feel like puzzles they're just tasks you encounter at every set point. You interact, press the appropriate key, and that is the full depth of mechanics. There's no gathering of tools that you specifically need for the level and while there are interactables you never actually need them. 
The monsters are viscerally terrifying and twisted looking and yet I didn't actually get to experience that horror. Monster sections were usually brief and on rails just like the story. Early in the game you're taught how to hide in lockers and you never really need it at any point in the game, it was another useless mechanic. First monster "encounter" I didn't even encounter it, I thought I was stealthily avoiding it then realized halfway through I can just proceed through the stage with no danger At no point was genuinely terrified because the game doesn't allow for buildup. There's no tension, not even in later monster encounters which are dangerous... but not in a way that's scary... 
Unlike their previous game "A Machine for Pigs" where the lack of tension came from how little danger you were ever actually in, Still Wakes the Deep lacks tension from how laughably easy it is to be killed in almost every monster encounter (like quick time in Resident Evil 6 level of easy). Never did any of my deaths feel like they were caused by my actions as a player, often times what killed me was that I didn't start running 0.1 seconds sooner than I did and the monster got me... which didn't add any tension to the monster encounters it only annoyed me. Especially the game's "stealth" sections which are barely stealth at all. 

This game can best be summarized as "It's by The Chinese Room" and that should tell you everything.

208509771.review:
"Och aye Loch Ness widdly-peep fanny!"

Fleeing crimes he's committed, Still Wakes the Deep finds the player character taking on a job on an oil rig in the 1970's in the hopes of buying himself some time to sort himself out before facing justice. Alas, justice decides to follow him, he gets fired from his job and then the world ends over the course of about four hours. What a thursday!

The game looks and sounds quite nice, especially with some very finely cast voice acting, but due to Unreal Engine shenanigans it was a pain to get running properly. Mirrors don't work, shadows look awful and the framerate tanks at seemingly random times just to make sure any immersion is regularly shattered for the player. Still, the visual designs do earn a special mention, with the oil rig looking and feeling exceedingly detailed and accurate, while the various monsters that spew out to infest it and hound the player look appropriately macabre and twisted.

What the game lacks, then, is a game. There are almost no real mechanics to speak of, no real challenge to deal with and a player with even the most basic understanding of videogames will breeze through the game without any challenge. Monsters are rarely present and easily distracted and an inordinate amount of playtime is dedicated to walking, climbing, holding down buttons and repeating interactions that do not challenge or even have a way to fail them.

As for the story, the game mostly comprises of smaller character stories that begin and then unceremoniously end. Towards the end of the game people are killed off at such a rapidfire pace that it becomes a chore to sit through the (certainly well written and acted) "forgive me for I couldn't save you" -speeches. Especially since the game is so short that we really don't have the time to bond or begin to care for any of the characters in a way that would justify these scenes for the player. As for the main story, the game is purposefully vague and leaves much unexplained and open to interpretation. 

Was it all a dream?

Probably, actually. That'd make the most sense.

Being so short and not really fun to play, I can't really recommend the game. You'd get just as much out of the experience by watching someone's longplay video of Still Wakes the Deep, and save yourself a bit of money doing so.

Playtime: 4+ hours (A single playthrough)

Ratings (1-10)
Visual: 8
Audio: 10
Story: 4
Gameplay: 3
Overall: 4

208424918.review:
[h1] A smashin' braw an' weel-made video gemme [/h1]

Jokes aside, I enjoyed my time with Still Wakes The Deep, thanks to its refreshing setting, stunning use of colors and a versatile cast of characters spewing jargon that certainly doesn't sound English to me but brought a smile to my lips every time I matched a slang word with its more familiar counterpart. I was especially fond of the substance that crept up the walls, slowly transforming the tasteless industrial landscape into a vibrant artwork of mystery and eeriness.

While I appreciate the developers' attention to detail, genius monster design and atmosphere-building soundscape, I do have a few complaints about the gameplay itself. The mechanics are way too unresponsive and clunky for such a polished game, considering the acrobatics the protagonist needed to perform to reach certain areas. I found myself dying by falling or drowning many more times than getting killed by one of the creatures just because I didn't press a few buttons at the right time. What's more, there was a slight lack of fear factor because the encounters followed the same formula from start to finish, and the game basically held your hand all the time - which in my opinion is positive when taking into account the maze-like, claustrophobic, dark bowels of the oil rig.

All in all, Still Wakes The Deep is a solid horror game that goes beyond what Chinese Room has developed thus far by introducing quick-time events, new movements and button prompts to spice up the gameplay and showing a clearly dedicated teamwork behind the lore, characters and environmental design. If the aforementioned flaws didn't put you off, then you should definitely see the unique horrors of Beira D for yourself!

208243502.review:
Still Wakes the Deep might be on my top 5 list of Horror games

The visuals are amazing, I mean actually amazing. They really draw in the atmosphere and immersed me into Oil Rig setting. Triple A fidelity but with actual performance.

The Voice Acting is like the actual cake itself though. By god is the talent in this game just ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ amazing, my ears have been hard tuned to understand Scottish just so I could really appreciate the dialogue and acting.

The animations are phenomenal. Clearly time was put into them, nothing ever looked half assed. Not even animations as simple as pressing a button.

The story is rather very linear but I feel like that's a good thing. A narrative focused title thrives on focusing on the narrative. It did a great job at feeling like a story and I leave it at that.

The horror can be few and far between for some, It's nothing that will have you scared ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ but the game really has some ass clenching moments.

==

Now for a couple negatives:

Main game is only about 4 hours long, so the pricing to say the least is.... Not very reflective of that. I picked it up for real cheap on sale, I think anyone else should as well.

The game wasn't just a tad longer 

The game is mostly a walking and oil rig repair sim. I never minded that. But I know some do and will.

It uses the very generic 'alien goo/substance turns people into monsters' trope, but with no story behind it throughout the whole game... There's no story development around the alien-like substance. 208199936.review:
What an experience, my dudes... All I could think of while playing it is how much of a banger a film adaptation of this game would be (in the right hands, of course). However, putting that aside and bringing focus to the story, I must say that, indeed, during its second half, it slows down to a certain degree that bothered me very much. Just like in "[i]Alien: Isolation[/i]", the game worries too much about adding obstacles over obstacles and by that I mean that at every turn there is a new problem that needs fixing in the rig and, gosh, does it get tiresome. I feel like if we had less to salvage after dealing with the generators, the game would feel more concise, since all the running and activating and handling of the rig in this specific part feels pointless other than to serve the purpose of delaying their inevitable fate (and testing the player’s patience). And I must confess, the technicality of the latter parts took me out of the experience a tiny bit. Aside from Roy's friendship with Caz, I feel like nothing else (character-wise and story-wise) was developed as well as in the first half of the game. This bothered me quite a lot, because from the swimming section onwards the game loses [b]A LOT[/b] of momentum and steam. It stagnates. I was thoroughly [b]EXHILARATED[/b] during the first hours, and then in comes the padding. However, up to that point: top-notch gaming experience, extremely balanced and satisfying. "[i]Still Wakes the Deep[/i]" has everything that makes me tick (the vibes, the theme, the approach, the setting, the inspiration, etc.), but, unfortunately, I can't rate it as a 10/10 because of this pacing issue that absolutely [b]HAUNTS[/b] the mid to end game. Other than that, I do plan on replaying it just so I can appreciate it more and maybe wrap my head around these flaws (I don't know, maybe the fact that I finished it in one sitting has something to do with this bad feeling). But anyways, [b]WHAT A STORY[/b].

EDIT: The game definitely grew on me in the last couple of days. I take back some of the things I said as it now stands as one of my favorite games ever. All I needed to do was to let it sink in for a while inside my head. Glad I did. No replaying was necessary.

208160708.review:
This game consists of walking with occasional quick time and not so quick time events. If you don't like that gameplay, don't bother.

That said, I really enjoyed this game. The atmosphere is intense, the voice acting is very immersive, the characters are relatable, the creature design is bizarre, and the visuals are breathtaking, with one particular scene underneath the rig making me pause and admire what I was witnessing. The subtitles having translations for the slang being used was pretty funny.

While most of the level design is good, I was sometimes wondering how my character would physically be able to fit in some of these vents, or do some of the athletic feats required during this game. Not a big deal, just a bit immersion breaking. I also wish the paths you could take weren't so linear; maybe if you went down the wrong hallway, you got attacked by something and had to hide, just to add a bit more suspense throughout.

Would recommend for horror fans, with the caveat in the opening of the review. Also it is a short game at 4.5 hours, maybe 5 if I missed something.

208116426.review:
I can't remember the price I paid for this, but think 15-20 feels right.

This is a great story told on a railroad. Maybe the game is upfront about that but I avoided looking into it once I decided I wanted to play it, so that was news to me. I was expecting to have some choices. 
I played on Story mode and I think that really helped show me that you can't really do anything wrong in this game. There aren't any punishments. You die - you start at the last checkpoint, which in my experience was always practically right where I was already. 

I really loved the setting and atmosphere, 100% my favourite part. I took many opportunities to stand by windows and watch the wind and rain rip sideways outside, hearing the rushing and pattering of sheets against the glass. I also found myself tucking away in seacans or even short tunnels in just the right spot where the wind cuts out and suddenly you feel very hidden and dry and safe. They really nailed this part for me.

Monster design is top notch, what the ♥♥♥♥.
I wish we got more interactions with them though, less chase and more trying to reach them.

The most annoying thing about this game was all the locked doors. That sounds dumb because it is. Locked doors aren't a problem, but why are they locked? To keep you out? that's fine. Are the locked as a skill check? That's fine. Are they locked to guide you on a path and prevent you from getting lost? That's also fine! 
What's not fine is a door being locked in order to funnel you towards a trigger which makes you 180 and have nowhere to go except through the locked door which is now magically unlocked. This happens a lot and there is no explanation for why these doors unlock themselves, or why they were locked in the first place. 
After realising this was more or less a movie though, it stopped bothering me. Instead of the door being locked on the first pass, oops, I found out it was locked but the character actually canonically did not do that and I should experience the story without that little bit. Oh wow! after suddenly having to run that door he forgot to check opened on the first try! What luck!

I don't know how enjoyable this game would be on standard mode. I'm not always an easy/story mode guy, but I'm really glad I didn't ♥♥♥♥ around with getting in the way of watching this movie unfold for myself. (I realise I'm saying "movie" a lot but there are no real cutscenes aside from some phone calls or what are essentially epileptic flashbacks.)

I 100% expected this game to be MUCH longer, but again, maybe the game is upfront about it's length an I was just assuming and wrong. But I did feel misled by watching the steam progress bar for the game as I played. The base game includes all the achievements for the DLC as well, so you an imagine my surprise when credits rolled and I've achieved 40% 
There are a few for the base game I missed, sure, and I wasn't expected to 100% it on my playthrough, but I absolutely expected to at least get the majority of achievements by completing the frickin game. 

Do recommend, might only recommend on sale. It's not very long, is great quality, will be more of a winner if you like stormy ambience. 

Might be apt to include that I am not made uncomfortable by raging waters, open oceans, underwater machinery, etc etc. I just thoroughly enjoyed the setting, others might find that terrifying.

207998145.review:
Sometimes I regret that Steam only has a Yes or No review system. This is one of those times.

So, I'll start with all the things I liked about this game.

The game looks incredible and the monster design is really inspired. The voice acting is really good and I found it refreshing to have a game with so many scottish actors. The setting is incredibly effective as a horror setting, quite able to induce a fear of isolation, the dark and the water.

That's about it. I found the gameplay quite shallow. The enemy encounter were of two varieties: scripted chase scene where you pretty much hold forward and vault when necessary and very easy hiding sequences. The "puzzles" were also extremely easy, if we can even call them that. This, however, is quite common in horror games like this (outlast and amnesia have pretty much the same gameplay, although I find in those games the enemies are more of a menace).

The story was also quite shallow, and I find that much more disapointing. In the first 10 minutes of the game you find out all your character is: A man with a loving wife and daughters who got in a fight with a guy and begrudgingly had to ♥♥♥♥ off to an oil rig with his best friend while thing "calmed down" at home. That's it. He does not change from that, we don't find any depth about his life or relationships. The monsters in this game seem to have some hallucinatory properties. You think the story would use this to show the depths of the main characters mind. But no, all hallucinations or flashbacks show the same: You're a man with a loving wife and daughters who got in a fight with a guy and begrudgingly had to ♥♥♥♥ off to an oil rig with his best friend.
And forget about the rest of the characters getting any plot relevance or depth. You meet most of the workers in the beginning 10 minutes and later find half of them already dead. Every dead body that I find, the main character has to say his name out load to remind me he has a connection with them. And the ones still alive only exist to say one thing: "♥♥♥♥'s ♥♥♥♥♥♥ man. Here's what you gotta do now."
Okay, so what about this game's themes? I... don't really know. I mean, there's the obvious envorinmentalism, and some anticorpo messaging with how ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ♥♥♥♥ the oil rig is maintained. And that's it. Maybe I'm just dumb, but I can't tell you any other themes or concepts this games explores.

All in all, I would say: "If you can get it for $12 or less, it's an interesting couple hours experience (I beat the main game in 4 hours). Not for more than that."

I still have the DLC to play. Maybe I'll play that and see if it's any better.

207895439.review:
[h2] Summary [/h2]

Still Wakes the Deep is a beautiful story about reconciling with your choices, to stop running and face the music. At least that's how I interpreted it. Coupled with some fantastic graphical fidelity and art, it paints a bizarre yet almost beautiful eldritch horror that draws you toward it and corrupts whatever gets too close. Sadly, despite it's tight character drama and stellar art direction, the horror's are very much a set-dressing with nothing to tie it to anything and the scant gameplay, suffocating linearity and shallow interactivity makes this a great one-time experience with not enough to come back to.

I'll continue by addressing both my main gripes in greater detail. [b] Minor spoilers [/b]

[h3] The lack of Horror [/h3]

The horror is presented as unknown and not understood. It goes unexplained throughout the entire game, all characters are  stuck in flight-mode or in denial to even properly question what's going on. These are all good things at least initially but as the story went on the phenomenon's meaning or intent is never theorized upon and we are given nothing which has the opposite effect and it becomes trivial. When it dawns on them that they are stuck with the drill-goop not a single one has any thought or idea about it or it's implications, the drama comes first. At that point you could've just made it a super-hurricane that brought a pride of lions onboard and it would've changed nothing. I'm not saying I want answers, I want the world and character's to try and get answers but ultimately fail. If the player doesn't have to think about it, then they won't.

[h3] The lack of Gameplay [/h3]

I know that primarily The Chinese Room are storytellers first and any function in their games are purely there to facilitate that the story moves forward and that you are immersed, it's their niché, I get it. What saddens me is all the gameplay potential this had with just a bit more complex puzzles and looser reigns on the player. If they had gone with the approach that the rig is openly traversed with clues and key items scattered about coupled with having to learn the mechanisms it would have felt much more rewarding and less like the "are you awake?"-busy work they came up with. It would have also had us spend more time avoiding monsters and made their appearances that much more iconic. You can even keep the idea of the rig constantly changing by having the areas we go back to, to solve puzzles change at certain checkpoints and/or having the player rethink on how they're gonna get the neccessary items for the puzzle. This is not outside the scope or their capabilities, you just leave the player to their devices to solve problems while avoiding dangers without all the yellow paint and QTEs.

[h3] Conclusion [/h3]

What I am asking is perhaps not me understanding what The Chinese Room is all about. It's still a touching story with plenty of spectacle and a whole slew of hard work and talent has been put into SWTD. But what areas they have chosen to neglect puts their asking price into question and what playing actually gives the player that watching a no commentary Let's Play for free doesn't. Buy this on sale to experience it, I'm sure you'll like what's there and most likely will remember it fondly; because you for sure will not replay it to prove your memory wrong.

207851859.review: I cannot recommend this game enough 9.7/10 had a few bugs with the QTE but the story absolutely makes up for it.

207850978.review:
Criticizing a Chinese Room game for not being a game is like complaining about the cutscenes in Metal Gear Solid. So I won’t go there. I will say that there is a comfort in the utter linearity of Still Wakes the Deep; to know that no matter which path you try to take, no matter what problem faces you down, all can be overcome in the most mindless way possible: by pressing forward and mashing the keyboard whenever there's a prompt. 

If there is a fire, an extinguisher will always be one foot away. If you find a path, it is never a dead end.

This almost-railroad straightforwardness makes Still Wakes trivially easy, but this is a strength. It’s fine. I'm not being ironic. They went way overboard with the yellow paint--I turned it off and it still was too much--but the “game” is there to give your brain something to do. It's okay for it to be easy, if the real draw is the story.

Unfortunately the story does not pull its weight. This game’s plot is five or six hours of chores, all of which always comes to nothing for some random stroke of misfortune outside of your control. That guy you rescued? He actually dies anyway. That boat you recovered? It randomly gets destroyed. The power you restored? lol didn't matter.

Oh. I guess the whole game is pointless, then.

In this way the player can keep busy while the plot never advances. This is one of the most frustrating and tedious tools in narrative design. If I complete my objective, I should succeed; I shouldn't lose for some stupid reason just to keep the game from ending. Meanwhile the busywork treadmill is all just so you have something to do while you wait for the characters to come to the obvious conclusion that you yourself came to the second you realized this game was set on an oil rig being taken over by hideous mutant monsters. 

The characters themselves are competently written and fantastically acted, but there are ten times too many, and none really receives enough space to be developed to the extent the story seems to want. Caz will mention someone's name like you are supposed to know or care who it is. Uh, I don't. Just another mutilated corpse. 

It isn’t all terrible aside from this. But for a horror game, it simply isn’t scary. The monsters are way too big and goofy to be anything but comical. At one point a character dies and is instantly resurrected as a giant carnivorous tentacular head. I literally burst into laughter when he stumbled out to eat me. I mean it’s so ridiculous. Without any survival mechanics or combat there’s nothing to build tension, so you might be startled a bit when the enemies catch you the first time, but after that it's just annoying. They’re not nearly subtle enough to seem creepy or real. They’re just ridiculous. Which is fine, but we’ve all seen The Thing and played Dead Space and The Last of Us and Alien: Isolation, and we’re all tired of gory goop growing on the walls and weird flesh monsters that Once Were You saying Weird Things like they did in Prey. You can't do the same things these games did, and then remove the gameplay. What is it that you have to offer?

Well, there is some pretentious nonsense thrown in, like a nice sheet of rice paper around your video game meal, so you might be fooled into thinking there's some kind of Actual Meaning beneath the walking simulator exterior. But basically gory goop monsters growing from the walls is all they've got. 

Still Wakes is fine, I guess, and playable, but it’s not worth your time if you want a The Thing-inspired horror experience. It's too maudlin to be frightening and has no real atmosphere. I kept forgetting it was set in the 70s. There are many better options.  

Also, representation does not matter. I have to live with type 1 diabetes every second of every day. I don’t want to see it in video games. If you are going to put it in then you could at least figure out how it works first. It’s not that rare. I don’t think I’m asking too much here. But that is too much to ask of the Chinese Room.

207850829.review:
I keep tricking myself into getting The Chinese Room games. I know I dislike the melodramatic storytelling and its singular focus on quiet misery, and it's consumptive philosophy of life (i.e. there's a flash of beauty and cosmic appreciation before everything you've ever been gets snuffed out, which plays out as "dying is kind of lit, actually. you should try it") but by gum: the graphics! Boy do they know how to gussy up a game world and make it feel rich in environmental details.

And gussy they did. You've never been on a 70s oil rig off the coast of Scotland, but now you can get a guided tour around one that is probably kind of accurate while also dodging spookums and jumping across ledges. Yes, Roomheads, TCR has seen fit to add some mechanics to their walking sim. They aren't even half-bad! There's jumping and grabbing onto ledges, there's prying open stuff and crawling through it, swimming, scampering across rickety catwalks. It's a real adventure camp out there.

What actually bugged me is that after a ton of adventuring and jumping and climbing and so on--some real fun survival-against-all-odds stuff--the game brick walls with a "you know you can't actually hope to survive this, right? Here, press E to heroically blow up everything and yourself." And then it has a sappy coda. 

Aarrrgh! I've been had! Yet again! What the hell! It's a VIDEOGAME, bruv! ♥♥♥♥! I know I couldn't actually do any of this in real life, that's why I'm [i]playing a videogame![/i] If I wanted an emotional tale about dying, I'd sit in a chair and read a book!

God. I just hope I remember this the next time they put a game out.

Oh wow, Bloodlines 2 finally dropped. Hmm...

207822443.review:
I wanted to like this game and for the most part I did, but overall it's just a massive let down for what the full price is (which I thankfully didn't pay).

First off, you can look at my total time played and infer from that how much you're going to get out of this game. There are no secret endings or really any hidden secrets to find, so once you play through the game that's really it. Replaying the game won't reveal anything else new. 

The game play itself is a classic Chinese Room walking simulator with a good narrative. You play as Caz, a Scottish electrician who has taken up a job on an oil rig to run away from legal troubles on shore. Throughout the game you get glimpses into what happened by way of flashbacks of your wife berating you for beating up someone who insulted her and sending them to the hospital. Running off to the oil rig doesn't seem to have helped the situation or Caz's relationship with his wife, as the police send the rig a request to ship you back home to face an investigation into the assault. Before Caz has a chance to take a helicopter back to shore there is an explosion on the rig and then everything goes to hell. An eldritch abomination of unknown origins and design erupts from the rig, corrupting and changing anyone or anything it touches. It's up to you to try and save the crew and get out alive.

This is a fantastic setup for the game and you can spend more time before the explosion by speaking to the other crew members and exploring their cabins to get an idea of what things are like on the rig. Caz is clearly a popular and well liked member of the team and has won over most people in the short time he's been there. He's a natural charmer and is a well written and believable character. So are most of the other NPC's you run into on the rig. 

Graphically speaking, this game is gorgeous. There is an insane amount of detail in everything on the rig. You really feel like you are crawling through the guts of an enormous oil rig that is falling apart. Probably one of the best looking games  I've played in recent memory. That being said, there are performance issues. I had to tweak the settings multiple times to get the game in a stable place with the FPS. There was also this awful stuttering that happened when turning that took me a while to figure out how to fix. The lighting in certain areas can be awful to the point where you have to just hope you are heading in the right direction because you can't see even with a flashlight.

The mechanics of the game are pretty simple. You spend most of the game walking around the ship, but can break locks and vents to access other areas of the rig. Caz is slow as sh*t. He comes with a "sprint" function, but I swear he doesn't move any faster than his regular walking speed. There are other times you need to interact with machines on the rig in order to progress the story. These are quick time events that don't require much thought. There are a few enemies in the game that will chase and kill you given the chance. They are the remains of the crew who have turned into these blobs of flesh with tentacles that reach out to touch and move around. They can talk to you, but it's always some inane or aggressive ramblings. You can hide from them and throw objects to distract, but other than that you just need to avoid them or it's game over.

As I progressed through the game I couldn't help say to myself several times "This is like SOMA, but worse" and now having completed the game I am vindicated with that feeling. Let me give some examples of how they are similar:

-Game set in the ocean
-Horror game
-Main antagonist is a corrupting force that changes everything it touches
-Enemies are former crew members (or robots) that have been corrupted by the main antagonist
-You can only hide, sneak past, or run past these enemies
-You are required to perform a series of mechanical tasks while avoiding enemies in order to progress
-You can't look at enemies with your flashlight, as doing so with cause the flashlight to turn off
-Being near the corruption reduces your visibility and creates effects on screen showing you are too close to the corruption
-Death screen is filled with random images flashing before your eyes

I could go on, but you get the picture and I'd just be nit picking if I kept going. 

Where SOMA succeeds where Still Wakes the Deep fails is with the length of the game, story, and the horror element. I never once was scared playing Still Wakes the Deep. The changed crew of the rig are grotesque and unnerving, but never downright scary. They are also sometimes comical with their dialogue they shout at you. Encounters are rare and with a plethora of hiding spots or easy to follow escape routes while being chased, you are never really in any true danger.

While SOMA isn't the longest game, it's at least a 10 hour playthrough. More than twice Still Wakes the Deep. There is so much more to explore and many different areas you have to traverse through in SOMA. In Still Wakes the Deep you are stuck on the oil rig the entire time and while it changes drastically as the game progresses, you are still on the oil rig. There was so much wasted potential here with the rig. It is a massive complex that you criss cross frequently, but the game is so annoyingly linear that there is no opportunity for barely any exploration. If the game allowed multiple different paths to areas and opened up the rig more rather than blocking everything off with shipping containers or broken pipes, it would be an amazing change. That's not to say SOMA is open world or anything like that, it isn't, but it isn't as linear or restricting as Still Wakes the Deep.

The story for Still Wakes the Deep ends up falling flat on its face by the end. With how short the game is you never really see any of the relationships Caz has with his fellow crew members grow or be expanded upon. You are treated to constant flashbacks of your wife yelling at you throughout the game, which became very grating very quickly, much like in real life. By the end I couldn't help but feel bad for Caz having to be married to his wife Suze. After the explosion on the rig the other NPC's are just vehicles for Caz's next objective. There isn't any real conclusions with them besides watching them die one by one. 

The NPC's also treat the corruption on board like it's not really there. It's rarely mentioned by the NPC's and at the beginning it's almost like no one wants to discuss it's presence. I hate when games or shows or movies do this with the horror elephant in the room. They just ignore it or speak around it during dialogue and only address it when it's actively tearing them apart. There is a flesh tentacle tearing through the wall here, can we please talk about this? Nothing is explained with the eldritch abomination that is attacking the rig, which is in a way a good thing as it leaves a lot to the imagination, but the game explains literally nothing about it. Why is it here? What does it want? Where did it come from? Nothing. Not a thing. I don't require an NPC to give them the entire life story of this creature or thing or whatever it is, but I would like a crumb of information on it.

Overall, 5/10 game. Still Wakes the Deep is way too short of a game with zero replayability for $35 USD.

This game, overall, is good. HOWEVER - going into it - keep in mind that this is more of a walking sim than anything. 

Some things it did really well:
Voice acting - 11/10
Graphics/Immersion - 10/10
Story - 8/10

Overall: 7.5/10

You are only in danger 4-5x throughout the game from actual monsters, otherwise it's a lot of rootin' around in the map to try to get to x objective or go here or whatever with literally no danger, but cool aesthetics. People say it is like Alien Isolation - it's not. You are not hunted at all throughout these travel points and honestly it's just kind of filler. 

At first these travels feel unique and exciting, but then you quickly learn that it's all pretty much the same with some pretty simple puzzles. Of course - you have a "mission" as to why you're moving around the rig, but it does get old.

Overall, the game is good, I would recommend it on sale. The voice acting is legit some of the best I've ever heard, the environment and graphics are unique, and the story is interesting enough.

207689989.review:
There aren't many games that i want to continue after i leave a ''not-recommended'' review but this one ain't one of them.

It started off with an interesting story for me. The setting was quite right and it actually got me hooked. If there wasn't this ''very-big'' problem i would have already finished it and left a ''recommended'' review. Unfortunately, here i am with the ''not-recommended'' one. 

Game is a technical mess, and i mean this in every way. Played on 4 different devices and all of them had the same issues.

FPS stutters all the time, even if you have a very high-end rig. 

First machine i tried this game on had a RTX 3060, performance was horrible, couldn't even get more than 40 FPS. Second machine had RTX 5070, FPS was way more higher but the constant stutter persisted. 
Third machine was XBOX Series X, same issues.
And the last pc that i'm currently using has RTX 5080 + AMD RYZEN 9950X3D, still the same issues.

My rigs, for some reason, can't beat this game. It acts like a game which uses so high-quality elements that even the latest rigs can only get 60 FPS.

Well believe me this issue wouldn't actually make this game ''unplayable'' by itself. Yes, the constant stutters do make you angry after some time but still i would keep going if it was the only problem. So what exactly is the problem ?

The way developers choose to design this game is the exact problem. 

Movement, interactions, exploring, everything you'll be doing in short, feels so sluggish. Like i don't know how to explain this but game looks like it's about to break anytime. Voice acting, story telling and all those things feel very very professional but the way you move makes it look like an indie game that has been published by a random game company people never heard of. 

I really don't know how the quality management let this one go through but nah dude, i bet i could make better mechanics  than this with 0 knowledge if you gave me a random game engine.

All respect to work but i can't play this game with these mechanics.

207634989.review: I really enjoyed this game and would definitely recommend it, but it is certainly a Chinese Room game. The gameplay is very minimal and this is very much a linear horror experience. Even the stealth sections where you are given some autonomy are extremely simple and just boil down to take cover, wait, move forward, and repeat until you make it past the monster. If you are coming into this looking for something like The Dark Descent, you will be disappointed. If you are able to look past the gameplay however, then I believe you will be extremely pleased with this game as it pretty much excels in every other aspect. First and foremost, the very best part of this game is the voice acting, hands down. This might be some of the best voice acting I've heard in recent memory. I don't know if it's because the accents are playing some sort of trickery, but I swear, it's to the point where it doesn't even sound like voice acting. It's more like they took recordings of actual people talking without their knowledge, just absolutely superb. And this really makes you care about the characters because they truly seem like real, likeable people. The dialogue is also great, very natural and realistic with how our main character reacts to some of the things he's told to do. The setting is the next best thing this game has going for it, and this one is really important because in Chinese Room fashion, you're gonna be doing a lot of walking, so it's very important that you're doing that walking in an interesting place. This game has a setting I have yet to see in any horror media, an oil rig, and it honestly makes for a perfect setting. Completely isolated from the outside world with nothing but endless sea and sky in all directions. Sometimes, it almost seems as if the whole sky is ensnaring this specific oil rig, creating a barrier that you cannot escape from. Honestly, this game is just haunting beautiful and I sometimes would just stand there and take in the sights. Screenshots were definitely taken, enough said. The story in my opinion, isn't at the level of other things I've mentioned so far, but it is still enjoyable and well written, my main gripe is just that there isn't enough of it. To me, I would not consider being told to do one thing, doing it, being told to do another thing, doing it, and so on "story." Sure, these actions have meaning within the story and work to progress the plot, but they don't hold any actual significance when it comes to the story itself. What I'm trying to say is that a task like "restart the generators" could be replaced with "fix the oberstein transistors" or whatever and the story wouldn't change. Basically, this whole schpiel is me saying I think you have to do too many miscellaneous tasks throught the story, but when you aren't doing that, the story is good, done. Highly recommend 👍.

Ця гра стала для мене сюрпризом. Очікував коротку трешову хорор історію, але в підсумку отримав свій топ-1 в жанрі "біжи і ховайся". 
Якщо без спойлерів, то гра пропонує  круту атмосферу з гарною графікої і з шедевральним саунд дизайном. Сподобалось як задизайнена нафтова платформа, що є досить незвичним сетінгом для ігр.


Історія. 
Що мені найбільше тут подобається, це те, що тут взагалі нічого не пояснюється. Все що відбуваєтсья в грі, це саме те що відбувалося б якби щось подібне сталося в нашому світі.
[spoiler]  Уявіть що ви працюєте на платформі, буріння йде шкереберть, і якась хрінь з нальотами Лавкрафта починаю розповзатись по всій платформі. Вашим пріоритетом була б евакуація, але шансів на то немає, і платформа починає тонути. Ви хочете цьому запобігти з вижившими колегами. Протягом цього всього ви усвідомлюєте що виходу немає, нічого немає сенсу, і єдиний варіант - померти забравши цю хрінь з собою. Ви помираєте і ніколи не виясняєте що відбулось. Кінець історії[/spoiler]

Мінуси.
Геймплей. Просто йдеш, лазиш і ховаєшся, все. Дуже примітивно, але через тривалість гри не вспіває надоїсти.
А ще. Єдина річ, яка завадиле мені повністю розтворюється в протагоністі, це бажання дізнатися більше про екіпаж. В грі особлива увага приділяється відносинам Каза з його колегами, але як гравці, на початку гри ми маємо лише одну розмову з кожним з них (більшість опціональні). По ходу розповіді, я, чесно кажучи, поняття не мав, ким була більшість з них. В найкращому випадку я пам`ятав двох-трьох по іменах чи обличчях. Я думаю, що якби нам дали чуть більше часу на початку для знайомства з екіпажем, то і те що відбувалося б пізніше, виглядало б більш драматичним. 

Підсумок.
Це гра в жанрі хорор, яка взагалі не страшна (для мене персонально), але надзвичайно атмосферна.
Це гра, "людяність" персонажів якої ти відчуваєш  і віриш в них.
Це гра, де страждання і відчай головного героя окутують тебе через екран.
Рекомендую

207316862.review:
A relatively interesting experience that doesn't overstay its welcome. For many people it will be far too short. It also has zero replayability. It's very much an on-rail horror experience with a few tense scenes of hide-in-seek like you've seen them all before back in the early 2010s with Penumbra, Amnesia and the other horror-hide simulator.

The visuals, sounds, and level design are superb. In most spots its quite clear where you need to go. So long as you toss items around and move silently, you can get out of most horror encounters relatively unscathed and unstressed.

I think the character interactions and narrative beats could've been better. The game rarely strays from the loop: Pick up phone/talk to npc > Get told what to do > Do it > Scary part > Repeat until the game ends. There's very little room for experimentation or just actually discovering, you're kind of an eternal victim that has things happen to them with very little agency.

207287755.review: A fantastic horror game. It is of the minimalist style of horror game where there isn't much game mechanics beyond hide and run to avoid monsters with some light platforming, but the game's design, atmosphere, and story are all tuned perfectly for creating a strong sense of dread and tension. I'm a big fan of cosmic horror and the story really delivers on the overwhelming, out of your depth nature of the "evil" while keeping the focus on the characters struggling to manage the situation as it spirals further and further out of control. The voice acting is some of the best and if you're having trouble with the dialect there are a few different options to help make it more accessible. Definitely give it a look if you enjoy games that are more about the experience than skill and challenge.

207280207.review:
The writing, voice acting, and atmosphere of this game are really, REALLY good. The characters speak how people actually speak. They act how people might actually act during a disaster. You will actually care about the main character, whose backstory feels raw and real and is delivered expertly. You will feel the emotional impact of the other characters, whether negative or positive.

Where this game falls short is in the shallow gameplay. There are some basic hide-and-lure mechanics and some nice platforming, but nothing more than that. I wouldn't call this game a walking simulator, but it isn't too far from it either. I also wasn't super impressed with the performance, but it was serviceable at least.

Think of this game like you're playing through a great horror movie. If you love a good horror story, and the oil rig setting appeals to you, BUY IT.

206995929.review:
tldr; If a story based horror game is your thing then I highly recommend it.

Great short story of one day aboard an oil rig that doesn't go to plan.
I played the game once through on a handheld.

The game is best played early on by taking your time and examining your surroundings - especially if you want all the achievements (I ended with about half of them).
While the game is a fairly unique setting, you can expect gameplay at times with similarities to the walking sim mystery of Firewatch combined with the heavy hide and seek atmosphere of Alien: Isolation with light environment puzzles.
It is extremely linear but packs a lot of story into it's short playtime and outside of the setting I felt the voice acting really shined. While heavy subject matter at times there are definitely a few wry laughs alongside your weary, witty Scotsman player character.

Playing with yellow clue markings to guide your character are optional but recommended.
You can expect to have a few moments where platforming or underwater based elements take a couple of tries to get right and there are occasional quick time events which - while eventually easy to predict - are perhaps slightly overdone. That said, there are a decent amount of controller settings to ease your woes.

If you don't enjoy watery environments or closed spaces perhaps look elsewhere.
Speaking of water - the graphics really sell the location and the water is very well done from a relatively small team. Play it.
ps. Beware the man on the horse.

206791182.review:
I recommend this game not for being a horror or delivering on its gameplay, I recommend it for its grounded portrayal of Scot's aboard a rig, how rich the environments and characters were, and how immersed I was because of this. 
Dialect was used in proper contexts, there were real place names down to the streets in Glasgow, real cultural and political overtones. Walk into Addairs cabin, and it provides context to other rig workers contempt towards him and excellently sets up that cafeteria conversation soon after. I loved that the Scottish lingo was not just saying "aye" like any other Scottish portrayal and Gaelic mode seems to have been a love letter to Scottish culture as with the rest of the game. 

Unfortunately its horror elements just weren't that scary, and stealth horror sections were undercooked and were too repetitive. I think I would have preferred the real horror being trapped on an oil rig in confined spaces with the threat of drowning being the true fear. This game would have benefited from removing all linearity and opening up the rig for you to explore, memorising the maps (that are ultimately pointless) and making navigation increasingly difficult through its winding pipes and walkways. Give a set of objectives such as 'Reignite the flare-off tower' 're-level the ballast tanks' and gate them off with things you naturally discover like keys, codes and items such as a flare, or a swimsuit. The point A to B gameplay removed tense moments and any sense of discovery, like squeezing into a confined high pressure water pipe with a sluice gate at the end as you know "thats where I know I need to go next, there isn't a failure state or risk of getting lost as its linear", I couldn't help but feel that this should have been one of the most scary elements in the game, it was close but just wasn't ultimately.

206572057.review:
Overall (7.5/10)

A visually powerful and immersive horror story that shines through its atmosphere, tension, and one deeply human character arc, even though the broader narrative lands with less impact.

Story (7/10)

The story is intriguing but ultimately ambiguous, with an ending that does not fully match the emotional peak reached through one standout character’s arc. That character moment delivers genuine depth and was the highlight of the experience, even as the overall conclusion doesn't land the same.

Pacing (8/10)

The pacing is tight and well-structured. Every sequence maintains tension and momentum without unnecessary exploration or filler.

Gameplay (7/10)

Gameplay functions as a vehicle for immersion rather than a core mechanic. The movement, interaction, and environmental navigation work well for what the experience is designed to deliver, sustaining tension and engagement without breaking the cinematic flow.

Cinematics (9/10)

Visually striking and sonically rich. The lighting, sound design, and confined environments create an exceptional sense of claustrophobia and tension that feels film-quality.

206565781.review:
It's alright I guess. 

Only problem is that all of the jump scares and creepy moments are very predictable. You can tell exactly when you are about to be in danger and when the game is going to throw a quick time event. The controls are also very awkward such as not being able to toggle or hold your crouch while holding an item. 

The game is kind of boring and drags on at some parts with little to no satisfaction at the end of the story because nothing is explained. It's one of those games that wants to try and mystify you by leaving a lot of the story up to interpretation but it wouldn't hurt to give us something to grasp on to. There's a reason there are sooo many theory videos explaining the lore and actual events of the game but every single one is different. 

The lore expansion of the DLC did nothing to help the case. While the DLC gave you a little bit more freedom to wander around and explore, you were still locked in to following the pre-designated path. Plus the swimming controls were kinda not good. 

Walking simulators work when you are given much more freedom to look around and explore. In addition there should be things to explore off the beaten path that add to the gameplay. A good example is the Painscreek killings where you have a purpose to walk forwards that isn't story driven but player driven. 

Overall the story is alright, as other reviews had mentioned my main complaints are the abrupt ending, lack of details surrounding the story, and an unsatisfying end. 

For the positives, it's a 6/10 for me. The voice acting is absolutely fantastic and adds a lot of dept to the story. The game is also graphically beautiful. That paired with the sound design are able to make you feel as if you are truly isolated out at sea. Monster designs are alright, they look kind of meh up close and get repetitive but to be fair, you're not spending your time in the game staring at the monsters lol. 

I don't really recommend this game to others just because it's one of those games that you don't feel satisfied after completing it. It's also a very short experience for the price tag that it's asking for. It's also very much a walking simulator, there is little to no input on the player's part and no choice involved either. These types of games are much better classified as visual novels which if that's what it was going for then I'd be able to recommend it. But it's not.
