206319282.review:
I dont know why my hours played says 2.3 when I played for about 5, thats how long the game will mostly take you, but as far as a review goes, take this as a thumbs middle? Buy on sale at best, leave to rot in your wishlist at worst. Let me explain.

I am a perennial Dan Pinchbeck hater, I started the anti-fan club when I played The Chinese Room's first game Dear Esther and heard him say that he intentionally wrote the story to be difficult to understand because the writing didn't matter, the feeling did.

Thats something that someone who has no idea how to write says when they want to sound smart to a group of people who do not know how to write.

Sadly for him, I do know how to write and I will 100% call out that bullsh*t. 

Thankfully, after years now, The Chinese Room seems to be mostly independent of Pinchbeck's influence. This game did not escape him entirely, though.

The concept, and from what it seems, a lot of the writing direction in Still Wakes The Deep was Pinchbeck's doing until he left the studio before the game launched. I find this funny, because you can almost tell exactly where other people got to be creative against his usual slop. 

There's some really cool moments in this game.

I feel like they went super hard on making the oil rig feel like a (mostly) believable space, almost to the game's detriment at times. The rig is massive and a good 80% of the game is spent climbing and navigating the rig with no horror threat beyond creepy noises. That other 20% of the game is fantastic, where Caz is having, genuinely, well written conversations at the start of the game or facing the unknowable horrors that are overtaking Beira D. 

Thats where I get mixed feelings on this game, though. At 35 dollars you expect a certain amount of polish, which is there, but you also expect there to be substance. I don't know if I entirely agree on the substance being here, because by the end of the game I felt as though the vast majority of my time was spent parkouring and the eldritch horror part of the game was just lacking. 

The visuals were a strong suit, especially for those few parts where you see whats overtaking the rig in all its glory, but again, only a few parts of the game really show this off. Oddly enough I feel as though they didnt lean hard enough into the eldritch part of this beyond the monster design. 

Now we come to the part that really makes me torn. The story. 

I do not, for the life of me, understand where they were going with parts of this. Spoilers ahead.

[spoiler] Caz Mcclearry assaulted and hospitalized a man that hit on his wife Suze before the game started, and he's hiding out on the oil rig thanks to being gifted a job by Suze's brother Roy. There's a touching story there, underneath some weird sh*t, but I have a really hard time just buying this as the impetus for us being on the rig? I know why they did it, they needed some sort of guilt for the eldritch being to flash Caz back into when things get really trippy, so we see Suze yelling at him over his actions and Caz playing off the severity of what went down. That being said, I can't shake the feeling that the writing was put on this narrow path in order to get the idea Pinchbeck had for the ending down. A lot of it feels like it only serves the purpose of being an excuse to pad the runtime, or to keep us climbing up and down the rig, and not to aid us in understanding characters or wider themes. Every time there was a flashback I kept going "Why are we still on the beating guy up plot point, I get it already" because much like Caz beat up the guy that hit on his wife, we just keep getting beaten over the head with that plot point right up until the credits roll. 

The reason I take issue with this is because if the writing was more focused, there is a really touching story to be told about faith in our loved ones, having to create space between us in order to do the right thing, distance making the heart grow fonder, and most importantly, guilt. That is the story that was almost told in this game, but it never quite got over the line for me. There was a few too many *stressed scottish noises* moments in between major plot points, but I do see the vision that was there and hopefully in their next game The Chinese Room can really nail down the pacing and the plot.

There was also one thing that really troubled me which was the feeling that there wasn't a final proof done on the script. At one point one character dies from not having his insulin, except 2 rooms before that we find his insulin, and instead of immediately trying to give him a dose as he might be in diabetic shock, Caz just kinda sits there and cries. It cheapened the death for me because I kept thinking, "Dude at least try to give him the insulin". There was a lot of little stuff like that where I kept getting the vibe that things were a bit rushed in development on this game. [/spoiler]

For the non spoiler version of that paragraph, the game struggles at times with writing even when it shows flashes of brilliance, and its something that I think makes this game worth playing if you get it for 6 bucks like I did, but not entirely worth it at full price. 

Its a better movie than it is a game, but its much longer than a typical movie, which begs the question that only you as a consumer can really answer, why play this when there's other stuff out there like it?

This feels very much to me like its trying to be in the vein of what The Chinese Room originally got themselves into hot water with, which is Frictional Games style horror. I would push you in the direction of SOMA rather than this, but there are worse ways to spend your time and money than on an oil rig off the Scottish coast. 

Seeing as how this is at the very least, a financial success, I hope that the pain points in this game are remedied in whatever this studio works on next. I'm definitely in the minority with issues here, as well, as the game has a 90% positive rating. At the very least, now they're free of Pinchbeck. Looking forward to whatever comes next. 

Signed,
Pinchbeck's #1 Hater

206263171.review:
Absolutely beautiful game. I finished the base game in about 4 hrs in a single sitting, so it won't take very long. 
The story is great, the monsters are well-designed and it is satisfying to sneak by them, the characters feel unique and each tell their own story and the atmosphere is just so captivating and ominous.
The game is not really hard, there are some weird points in which I had to spend 5 minutes max to look around and see what I have to do, but overall it is intuitive. You will eventually figure out what to do if you are stuck.
The fact that "you do everything" in interactions such as gas valves and keys is pretty cool and innovative, even though it can get a bit annoying sometimes but not very. It would like to see it implemented in other games, but with moderation, as it can get annoying very quickly if overused.
The only problems (which are veeeeeeery small and barely noticeable) are that some hitboxes for some machinery are bigger that normal and you can't throw objects over them, which can be frustrating when attempting to distract the current enemy to a more distant location and that a random broken pipe had no textures inside and you could see through it. That's it. The rest is bug-free. I loved every second of this game and I will buy the DLC to continue waking stuff in the deep.

A cool game about avoiding grotesque horrors that wander the halls of the oil rig this game takes place and finding out more about this disaster and its consequences, with an unexpected but beautiful ending.
If you are into not very long horror games that are polished and offer all of the features above... BUY THIS. It is worth the money. Do I need to say more?

EDIT: If you are an achievement hunter, you will spend quite some time getting all of them. There are 59 total and I have earned 23 by simply playing the game. The only ones I can recall not being progression related are Social Butterfly, Walking Simulator and that one to [spoiler]hit a monster with an object[/spoiler] but I might forget some others (I cannot close this window). The rest of them are either DLC-related or weirdly specific and time consuming. I don't even understand what the last achievement is even about! Good luck, I guess..!

206165180.review:
This marks the last time i'm playing an UE5 slop.

I'll start with optimization. I' playing this game on steam deck which has reasonably strong hardware. I don't expect it to run game on ultra, but the game runs badly on low with upscaler enabled, manages to look horrible on every visual setting and doesn't shy away from dipping down into cinematic 15 fps.

Level design is horrible. Without the yellow paint it is usually not evident where to go. But funny thing is that even with the yellow paint it is not always so. Reason being fov is set to low 80s or 70s and is not changeable which is not accounted by the level design which often leads to important elements being outside the screen after animation play. This is systematic problem.

Controls are unresponsive. Not only during those cinematic fps dips but in general. Character has a lot of weight to hims and it seems that he reacts to button prompts with a slight 1 second delay. Game asks you to do some platforming and in one place it really wrenches your gut. Those platforms are really tight like in some mario game and your character isn't listening like mario does.

Stealth segments... exist. They're not hard, they're not scary. They're boring. It's a telling when you have an urge of skipping meat of the game. There are also chase but since monsters in the game don't look scary (you're literally being haunted by big head of your boss, in 90s that was humor content) you don't really feel threatened. Those chase segments suffer from level design and control issues too.

The last is story. I don't think I'm finishing this, boys. The story is so much "I don't care" content that playing the game is a chore.

The horrors of the game are underwhelming, body horror always makes me feel uncomfy and gets me. But for some reason this time I wasn't phased, it didn't feel unique or original. It is very The Thing which isn't bad but its not done in a new way.

205758467.review:
Pro's
- Excellent Art Design and Environmental Design
- Fantastic Soundtrack
- A cast of fairly good voice actors with some very impactful deliveries where it counts (most of the time) 

The game is fairly short and inoffensive with some

Con's
- Gameplay
This game is 90% walking around an unsettling environment without any danger aside from failing QTE's as whenever there is a monster encounter it is VERY heavily telegraphed and follows one of two methods for the entire game. 1. Run away in a scripted chase until a cutscene starts or 2. Hide in hiding spots that make you invincible, throw distraction item, run to the next one, repeat. There is only one monster encounter I feel used this effectively (the monster on the deck) and the rest are painfully boring and lacking any real suspense due to the next reason

- Monster Design
The Monsters in this game, aside from the 2nd one again, are horribly uninspired flesh blob tentacle monsters which fall into the category of silly and unthreating or "What am I even supposed to be looking at" but not in a good creepy horror way, more of a toddler's sketch kind of way. During a certain chase scene in Admin I had to pause the game because I was laughing too hard at the design of the monster, it really is that silly at times.

- Themes
This game is totally lacking in any serious overall themes to its story beyond "Scary stuff happen", you are given absolutely nothing to go off of as to the what, why, and how of what is going on beyond that it is happening and that makes the game play like a series of side-quests with no really overarching story beyond "trying to survive". The psychological horror aspect is incredibly weak and is left mostly to a few hallucinations in the latter half that sum up to "I miss my wife, I wish I didn't make her want to divorce me, I love my kids" 

- The Characters
I will challenge anyone who played this game to tell me about the characters in any depth, because outside of the main characters tragic backstory, the greedy mean foreman, and the best friend. Every character in this game acts almost identically with very little individual personality. This problem is remedied in the DLC but that carries over all of the other problems of the game and adds new ones.

- The Dialogue 
Sometimes you will wish the M/C just shut the ♥♥♥♥ up, specifically during every monster encounter the M/C will narrate everything that is happening as if he's an anime side character with the same few lines of dialogue that even in the short 5-6 encounters you will have across the game you WILL get sick of.

- Lack of Context/Explanation
Good Horror leaves the best parts to the imagination, unfortunately this game leaves Everything up to it. It sets up terrifying ideas and concepts, hallucinations caused by the unnamed force/entity that is causing the games events, the monsters supposedly holding on to larger parts of their former selves, seeming time shenanigans (DLC only) and so on. So many things happen because it is "Scary" or looks cool and it at a point starts to feel like its happening because "its eldritch your not supposed to know and that's scary right?" But when nothing has context and everything is samey fleshblobs and pained screaming nothing really stands out.

205748713.review:
It's "The Thing" on an oil rig with a Scottish crew except this thing does not bother to try and hide itself (so the premise of the story is good but certainly not original). The voice acting and amount of detail put into the environments are really what carry this game over to the positive recommendation side for me. Overall though, there is a lot more they could have done.

-It's extremely linear: borderline walking simulator status (funny enough, this is actually the name of one of the achievements).
-There's no need to use the "hide in lockers" feature since there's plenty of hiding/crawl spaces.
-The "throw an object to distract an enemy" mechanic is not executed well since half the time they run straight at you instead of where you actually threw the item.
-A lot of the things you have to interact with have nominal input schemes that make them feel trivial rather than satisfying. 

So essentially, it is a horror walking simulator with a few stealth scenarios. That said, there are several areas in the game that you feel pulled into--like they captured the atmospheric feel perfectly--so it's still worth a playthrough just to have that experience. Also the grotesque factor of enemy and corpse design is amazing. Other than a few areas where graphic settings performed inconsistently, it's a nice package for the 9GB of storage required.

I don't intend to buy the DLC unless I see it on sale for a few bucks; however, I'm definitely very interested in what games The Chinese Room puts out in the future. They do need to polish up and fix the bugs still present in this game though if they want to keep their fan base (like there's a known soft lock bug with the flare stack door not opening that was brought up over a year ago and it's still not fixed--I'm no programmer, but I can't imagine this is a very difficult or time-consuming task to complete).

205729184.review:
I really enjoyed Still Wakes the Deep for its storytelling and atmosphere. Before buying, I knew it was mainly an exploration and stealth-driven experience, so I wasn’t disappointed by the simple mechanics. The graphics, narrative, and horror atmosphere more than make up for that.

The visuals work perfectly with the genre. Games don’t need cutting-edge graphics to be scary (Phasmophobia proves that), but for a title like this — where exploration and immersion are key — the high-quality presentation really adds to the tension, the jump scares, and the satisfaction of exploring the world.

I also really enjoyed the DLC, Siren’s Rest. The underwater exploration gave me a sense of reflective nostalgia for the main game. Because I was already emotionally invested in the base story, I was immediately hooked again in the DLC, and it felt like a natural continuation.

The length of the game is just right. It’s not long, but for this type of narrative-driven horror, it works — any longer and it could have become repetitive.

Overall, I wasn’t disappointed because I knew what to expect, and this game delivers exactly what it sets out to do: a short but powerful exploration-horror experience with great atmosphere and storytelling.

205514969.review:
A horror game that thankfully skips (mostly) the psychological stuff; instead it's a good old-fashioned monster tale.  It's basically The Thing on an oil rig.

The reason I was interested in this game was its setting.  A period piece, set on a Scottish oil rig in the 70's... you can't say it's a cliche!

I got the sense that the dev team did some research on a real oil rig and charted out a thrill ride that takes you through it: first you're in the crew area, then you see engineering, and then down through one of pontoons but it's flooded, that sort of thing.  It's a convincingly detailed environment and combined with the period- and culture-specific details, it gives a great sense of place.

In terms of gameplay though, there's not much here.  It's exceptionally linear; there are very few points in the game where you have a choice of where to go next.  You spend about 75% of your time doing AAA style traversal stuff; now shuffle along the ledge, now crawl through the tunnel, now climb up the ladder... but uh-oh, something's falling, hold on!  At times I felt a little like my guy was Scottish roughneck Nathan Drake.

The rest of the gameplay is sneak/run/hide pursuit sequences from invincible monsters, the sort that you'd find in something like Alien Isolation or any number of horror game.  These are pretty well done and don't get miserably difficult, thankfully.  To be honest, I could have used a bit more of this stuff.

I wish they'd been a little more ambitious with the gameplay and included a bit more free-ranging exploration, but at 6 hours it's a good time in a unique setting.

Well. Just finished the game. Its beautiful
A bit of a trigger warning though, its incredibly gory (as you could probably tell from the images shown of the game.)
This game was recommended to me by my best friend and... no regrets on actually. Listening and playing it thru.
To all those wondering if they should purchase the game, do so when its on sale. The game in itself can be completed in around 3 hours if you are quick (I... unfortunately spent more time as I was afraid at the start over... the dark.)
The atmosphere, the music, the creatures, the story, the characters. Its all so... amazing.
Some areas look ethereal, some areas look like a nightmare.

205371393.review:
Absolutely great game, beat it in just over 4 hours. At the length of what it is, I would say buy it on sale ($35 for 4 hours isn't exactly the best deal) but enough about that. I'm writing this review hot off the press. 

The game is wonderful, from it's blockbuster-quality cinematic visuals and creature designs and the claustrophobic, labyrinth-like interiors of the oil rig. The narrative is very nicely done, and the game keeps you hooked from very early on. It has plenty of creepy moments and a very unique "monster" that I have not seen another game done. Honestly, this would have made for a great horror movie if not a game. The cast of characters is just the right size to not get confused - there are four main protagonists; Caz, Brodie, Roy, and Finlay, and four main 'antagonists' (really they're just humans mutated by the main 'creature' of the game... but I won't spoil who/what).  The voice acting is superb, and each of the characters feel completely unique in their presentation. Everyone talks with a range of mild to thick Scottish accents, but if you play with subtitles on (which I recommend) the subtitles Americanize all of the Scottish slang they use, so you can actually follow the conversations without getting mixed up in what they're talking about. It's a small, but very appreciated feature. 

There are a few complaints that I have, such as a bit of inconsistent responsiveness with some of the quick-time actions and movement-related interactions, like climbing ladders or ledges. The character can become a little buggy out of nowhere and just becomes frustrating to try and navigate. Also, you character's sprinting animation is a little goofy - you run with your arms flailing out like you're trying to get to the last helicopter out of Vietnam... but you move at like, a whopping 3mph. 

All in all, highly recommend playing through this game, but I recommend doing so if you can catch it at a discount. It's a great game, but very short.

205042711.review:
I was holding off until I finished this, but I'm honestly not sure I will. The atmosphere is great, the story is fine if nothing special, and there are some accessibility features that would be great [i]if they worked[/i].

For example, you can set all "hold" buttons to toggles instead. This does not apply to ladders, which require you to hold a key down the entire time you're climbing. Let it go momentarily and you launch yourself off, often to instant death. (This is a problem in itself.) At its worst, this means that if you need to jump and grab onto a ladder when you land (which happens [i]a lot[/i]), you can end up needing to hold down both mouse buttons and a couple keys for an extended period, and letting up on any of them means instant death. If you have hand issues, like me, this gets painful quickly. It's also a [i]constant[/i] problem when you're navigating the external portions of the derrick. (I'm not actually sure the toggle option works on anything, but ladders are the most egregious.) 

You can also set QTEs to require nothing but single taps, but that doesn't work either. You still need to hold, mash, etc., often while doing other things, and failure usually kills you. Again, this gets painful fast.

I was so excited to see how granular you could get with the accessibility features, and just as frustrated when I realized how many of the ones I needed just flat [i]do not work.[/i]

ETA: Apparently it's been a known issue since release that the toggle option doesn't work. WTF? Why launch with that option if you know it does nothing? Why is it [i]still[/i] nonfunctional?

Have been kinda wanting this game for a long time, and once I played it...

well. It's pretty alright. Not anywhere worth the price of 35 USD, but I'd say I'd give it a recommend with caveats once it's around 12 USD or so.

I like the story. Lovecraft stuff is very hard to ♥♥♥♥ up, and even Lovecraft Lite written by someone not a flesh mass (heh) can make for a good story. Still Wakes the Deep is no exception to that rule (obviously not, it's Chinese Room).

Technical-wise... I'd say you need a good rig. I have a 7900XTX and even then I was only getting 100 FPS with AMD FSR 3.0 Quality. Without, I was seeing lows of sub-60. Not to mention that I nearly refunded b/c for the first two weeks, I was having a glitch where it would crash in the prologue and prevent me from continuing. I'm not sure what black magic the devs performed, but it stopped happening when I decided to play through the entire game today.

Gameplay... I mean it's a horror game, what do you expect besides "throw object at corner of room and wait until monster walks over to investigate, then flee to the next hiding spot." Those were adequate I guess? The monster design leaves much to be desired, and they weren't very scary. They're all just generic flesh blobs with tentacles, and even the one that's noted to have a unique design requires you to kinda focus on him to see how he's unique. I was tense for a lot of these segments, but not very scared. I ♥♥♥♥ my pants on games like Faith and Visage, but Lovecraft flesh blobs here? Nah, hand me my plasma cutter (or wrench so I can chuck it into the corner of the room).

Funnily enough, the gameplay reminded me a lot of INFRA, which is another game about being a maintenance worker wandering through a broken-down place and fixing ♥♥♥♥ to get through to your next location.

**TL;DR** Recommend buying for 12 USD or less. No shot for 35 USD lol.

(Don't even think about getting the Karma bundle, Karma was genuinely one of my biggest disappointments of the last year. The Soma bundle looks worth, get that instead.)

204488585.review:
[h1][b]Recommended:[/b] Still Wakes the Deep[/h1]

With Dear Esther and Everybody's Gone to the Rapture, The Chinese Room has garnered a reputation for being "that studio that makes all those walking sims". While [b]Still Wakes the Deep[/b] doesn't exactly try to break this mold, it does successfully recontextualize it in a survival horror format.

The story follows Cameron McLeary, an electrician who, after a bit of trouble with the local law enforcement, decides to take a job on the Bheira 5 oil rig off the coast of Scotland. Even away from the long arm of the law, life on Bheira 5 is anything but easy. The rig is in disrepair, it's obvious the company that made it cut as many corners as possible, and the distance from his family is putting a strain on Caz's marriage.

But it looks like our man Caz has run out of luck - the police have found him, and he's fired and ordered to helivac to the mainland. Just as he's about to leave, an unidentified disaster strikes Bheira 5, causing Caz to fall overboard.

When he comes to, Caz learns that an "event" has caused the rig to become unstable, and a general evacuation has been ordered. Now he must survive the rapidly collapsing oil rig, uncover the mystery of what happened, and escape to reunite with his family.

While this setup provides a solid basis for a narrative horror experience, it's also the weakest part of the game overall. One of the creators I follow on YouTube did an hour-and-a-half-long video essay analysis of this game, which is absolutely mind-boggling to me. Don't get me wrong, I'm still interested in what he has to say, but for the life of me, I can't figure out what you'd spend over an hour talking about here. The story simply isn't, if you'll excuse the pun, all that deep.

And just to reiterate, the story of Still Wakes the Deep is [b]perfectly fine[/b]. Despite the occasional problems like plot points you'd think are important just falling out of the story, or the protagonist acting dumb for plot convenience, it's an overall fine entry in the Lovecraft-inspired horror genre, with strong themes and well-written dialogue. As some other reviewers have suggested, it's on par with John Carpenter's The Thing: a quality horror narrative, but not really something to spark a deep philosophical examination.

In the grand tradition of The Chinese Room games, like I mentioned above, Still Wakes the Deep is another walking simulator. Well, not exactly, since there's a fair bit of running involved, but you get my point. The core of the gameplay consists of traversing the now-precarious Bheira 5 rig and interacting with its various elements.

This approach is very similar to that in the Amnesia series, which is quite fitting, seeing how The Chinese Room made Amnesia: A Machine For Pigs. In Still Wakes the Deep, if you want to pull a lever, it's not enough to walk up to it and press a button. First, you have to hold down the left mouse button to grab it, then press a directional key to determine which way you want to swing it.

These interactions provide some much-needed immersion and player involvement in the gameplay. They also work really well to punctuate tense horror moments, raising your heart rate as you try to fiddle around with locked doors and electronics while being chased by a monster.

However, as the game went along, I started to realize that most of the time, I'm not in any danger and I can just take it easy. In this context, pressing five buttons instead of one can become a bit of a slog.

This general feeling of "here we go again" extends to enemy encounters as well. For one thing, there are only 4-5 of them in the entire game, and all of them follow the same basic pattern: you sneak around while lobbing objects to distract the monster, and near the end, you book it to the exit (because there aren't any places to hide) in a "climactic" finale. This works really well for the first couple of encounters, creating a sense that you're fighting for your very life, trying to outsmart these creatures. However, once you figure out what's going on, it makes all the other similar scenes that much less scary.

The presentation is the real star of the show here. The locale of Bheira 5 is rendered in exquisite detail, making for an immersive and believable lived-in environment. This holds true even after the "event", with the game combining the twisted hallways and rooms of the once-familiar rig with the grotesque and alien physiology of the creatures, managing to capture the feeling of something extremely dangerous, yet captivatingly beautiful. The voice acting is also top-notch, with cute touches like the inclusion of Scottish Gaelic as one of the dub languages.

Ultimately, Still Wakes the Deep presents a compelling and interesting cosmic horror experience, which, in a media landscape where "Lovecraft-inspired" usually means a bunch of tentacles, is a feat in itself. The game is engrossing enough that, even for a horror noob like me, it kept my attention, and I was able to overlook most of the flaws for the entire 6-hour playtime. Still, for 40€, it's a bit short on content, so I would recommend getting it at a discount if possible.

Gorgeous Lovecraftian horror game. Nothing too difficult here, just some really great character and world building. Would have liked to see a bit more time or thought put into the player deaths.  It's really just a scream and then it play the same short clip.  Other than that I felt that every death was my fault, not something to do with unbalanced or confusing mechanics.  
(well.. aside from the very quick swimming/grabbing explanation... I did drown one time and had to look those mechanics up)
Designers did an excellent job guiding me without me realizing that it was happening.  Everytime I stepped out onto the deck or into a room with multiple doors, the path I took first ended up being the correct one.    A few times I did backtrack just to double check if this was actually the case.. and it was.   Lighting and color pulls you through the world without you feeling like the game is holding your hand.  
If you're looking for a fairly short horror game with some pretty developed characters and gorgeous environments, then I really recommend picking this up.   I have a habit of giving up on games after a couple of hours but this one pulled me through in 3 play sessions.

204308509.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is a classic on-rails horror game with great atmosphere and a novel setting.

The game takes place on a richly realized North Sea oil rig, with a Scottish crew that feels grounded and believable. Things quickly go to ♥♥♥♥♥ as an otherworldly horror makes an unwelcome entrance. You, as Cameron McLeary, are caught in this heap of ♥♥♥♥♥ and desperately try to find a way off this accursed rig and get back home.

Audio and visual presentation are great. The blend of eldritch horror and body horror is on point, and it was easy to get lost in it. The narrative is mostly straightforward and enjoyable, though some subplots and symbolic meanings don’t stick the landing. But terrific voice acting and compelling characters had me invested either way.

The gameplay loop here is quite minimal, not unlike The Chinese Room’s other walking-sim games. There are no puzzles and no exploration; interactions are mostly “press button to do the thing.” For most of the game you’re walking a linear path, with some chase sequences and a few easy stealth segments. If you’re looking for more complex game design, you won’t find it here.

I don’t mind the simplicity, and I find it a great vessel for narrative delivery. And even though Still Wakes the Deep doesn’t break new ground, it was an enjoyable ride that I do recommend.

204224842.review:
An enjoyable game, that feels like it fails to achieve what it could have been..

A horror game set on a Scottish oil rig on the North sea, with ACTUAL Scottish dialogue and voice actors? The premise had me sold from the outset, the rig is claustrophoic and isolated, a perfect setting for a horror game, and beautifully brought to life in game. The voice acting is pretty good, and to allay other Scot's concerns, the dialogue feels natural and believable in the most part, not some cheap American parody of a Scottish accent.

The storyline itself is pretty good, and stays fairly believable (inside the confined of horror/fiction). My only real criticisms are that spending more time on the rig before 'the event' bonding with other members of the crew would have added more to the horror element later., and (perhaps I missed this) there seems to be plot points which are hinted at, that never get any resolution, such as a series of notes at the end of the game, which didn't lead to anywhere and felt somewhat anticlimatic. 

Gameplay wise, things are pretty rudimentary. For sections it felt like Alien isolation, having to fix things (rudimentary), and stay clear of the 'invulnreble' monsters. I enjoyed the addition of the 'action' quick time events which left you nervous crossing any beam (of which there are many). On the whole though the gameplay aspect felt underwhelming. There are places to hide from the monsters, but most of the time there were so many hiding places that I never felt in danger of being caught. The 'fixing' was button pressing, and not very difficult, they did try to add a little more of a puzzle element at one point (which might be interesting trying to solve something whilst being chased by a monster) but it was very basic and not particularly consequential. Like the story, there did seem to be elements of the gameplay which weren't realised, early on there is a whole section dedicated to being cold from being outside and needing to use a heater to warm up, which I thought was going to be a gameplay element - however after swimming around in North Sea water for a couple of chapters, apparently I was warm enough not to need a heater? I feel like this was a missed opertunity, and one that felt natural as being introduced. If your a reasonably competant gamer, that's familiar with other titles, you won't find this engaging or difficult.

Finally, the design of the rig looks amazing, but the monsters themselves are a bit of a letdown. I was never 'scared' by them, and due to the coding I felt they were more goofy than threatening (although, one big headed boss I did find unsettling). 

Overall, a decent game, with a good story and basic gameplay, that feels let down by a lack of polish and ambition. I currently have 7.3hrs gameplay, which has been enough to play through the game twice on the hardest difficulty. I'd say replayability beyond twice is negligible, so bare that in mind when thinking about picking this up.

204220433.review:
Meh, 
it wasn't for me.
I tried but could not empathize with the main character. In a dialogue heavy game like this, that worked against me.


It's a walking simulator with pretty graphics and really good voice acting, and character animations. 
The monsters look appropriately meaty and gnarly. It's like the Thing meets The Color out of Space on an oil rig. It has body horror galore.

The reason it didn't resonate with me is the cringe love story between the main character and his wife towards the end. I guess I just can't empathize with the main character and his goals. You don't really see Suze, but you get to listen to her voice through letters and old dialogues the main character remembers. His wife earlier in the game was quick to turn her back on him and threatened to take their kids away because Caz decided to defend her honor. She wasn't ride or die. She was constantly berating the main character. Because of that, I refused to invest any hope in the main character getting a happy ending because I reasoned that even if he did somehow make it back home in one piece, his life back with that she-devil would have been a constant miserable slog. Imagine surviving the events of the oil rig, only to come home to a nagging wife threatening to take away your kids and reminding you that she settled for you. That would've been the *real* horror story. Bro would've probably killed himself via alcohol poisoning if she took his kids away. And lets face it, with his run in with the law, there was no way in hell he was getting custody of the kids. His marriage to that harpy was the REAL horror.

With that type of set-up, I think it was better for Caz that things ended the way they did for him. 

Suze can go f**k herself. Caz, you're a good man, and you can do better. There are plenty of other fish in the sea. 
Also your best friend Roy is a liability. You're better off alone Caz.

The game's biggest problem is how little the well executed horror is used throughout the game. The monsters were built up exceptionally well and their designs are incredibly creative, but they make up (and this is being generous) 25% of the game and actual encounters with them are incredibly uninspired. The rest of the game? Climbing, shimmying, crawling, and swimming. Mechanics that slow the game down, it feels like padding and it gets old not even 2 hours in. Its important to space out segments of horror with brief periods of safety, but the game's horror is far too brief and you feel 'safe' far too often. Despite it's flaws, Still Wakes the Deep is still impressive enough to recommend it for fans of horror games, but by the end I couldn't stop myself from thinking how much scarier a 20th playing of Outlast 2 would be.

At some point you have to choose if you are making a survival horror, or a walking sim, and Still Wakes The Deep really got stuck in the no man's land between the two.

It's all QTEs and completely linear, tensionless strolls between rigidly scripted set pieces where if you fail, you are forced to replay a chunk of the interactive cutscene.
And that's all it really can do to punish failure, since there's no resource management of any sort. You know, no actual gameplay.
The same issue I had with Soma and to lesser extent Observer. Excellent narrative, environment and atmosphere, but the actual "gameplay" does nothing but annoy. Soma, I believe, had the decency to add a walking sim mode after the release.

203041334.review:
I am quite disappointed in this game.  

It has an interesting premise and setting, but it is poorly done.

The graphics are mostly very good and the voice acting is superb...  But the controls are CLUNKY, and to be frank I've seen this sort thing many a time before.  The gloopy meat monster is straight out of Carrion (except in that you play as it!) or Homeworld Cataclysm (the Beast) and there are many other variations such as Dead Space etc not to mention movies and tv variations.  Basically it is sadly lacking in originality.  

And the game's pacing is poor.  It spends maybe 10 minutes setting the scene (probably the best part of the game actually) and after that you are basically 'running' around (except that you can usually move at only a slow walk or crawl, often even slower if the game puts you in a threatening position - it almost always make you run away in slow motion!)  lurching from one life and death disaster to another, and of course, playing Mr Fixit on all the broken junk on the Oil Rig.  

And it is continuous.  There is almost no quiet bit or sense of dread, because you just fall (often literally) from one disaster to the next.  And if you escape from something or somewhere, it often then asks you to turn around and face the exact same problem or area all over again!  It is hard to be scared when it never lets up, and it is hard to believe one guy can survive so many close brushes with doom.

I mean the main character is right to feel he is unlucky, because he just stumbles from one disaster to another and literally everything that can fail, jam, or fall apart does so at exactly the most inconvenient moment, except when it is the most convenient moment (i.e. you would have died but something broke at just the right time to let you fall to safety or something).  Sometimes you have to even start doing something stupid before it falls apart to get you in the right spot.  At one point you have to climb UP a wrecked helicopter, away from where you want to go and something that makes no sense to do at all, because after you get high enough it will fall DOWN and get you close to where you need to be.  How did the protagonist know that would happen?  Is he psychic?  Yeesh.

And almost every plot twist or threat aside from those that don't make sense (like the above helicopter thing), is obvious and telegraphed.  So you just know that person will die, that monster will appear, that thing you are doing won't work...  all before you even do it.

Every otherwise blocked area you are in always has exactly one way out that you stumble through as well.  You never end up with multiple pathways or in a blocked area, or if you do it miraculously becomes unblocked just when it needs to be unblocked.  

Eh - Alien: Isolation did this sort of thing MUCH better, as did Soma, or Penumbra or many other games.  

I got kind of bored with this and nearly didn't finish, but it was short so I pushed through.  But what kind of 'scary' game is so predictable and repetitive it is boring?

What scares me right now is this studio is handling Vampire: Bloodlines 2.  I am not currently very hopeful it will be good, which is a huge shame because Bloodlines deserves a top sequel.  But I digress.

Yeah, not recommended.

203030854.review: Still Wakes the Deep delivers a gripping horror experience on a 1970s Scottish oil rig, with stellar voice acting, authentic dialogue, and a haunting atmosphere powered by Unreal Engine 5’s stunning visuals. The story, blending Lovecraftian horror with human drama, immerses you as electrician Caz McLeary navigating a collapsing rig overrun by grotesque entitiy. However, the game’s extreme linearity stifles exploration, guiding players with heavy-handed yellow paint cues and a single-path structure that feels restrictive. At roughly 6 hours, its R330 price tag feels steep for the short runtime and lack of replay value. While the narrative and setting shine, the simplistic game play and brief length hold it back from greatness. Worth a play for horror fans, preferably at a discount

203014264.review:
[h1] Still Wakes the Deep is a fantastic horror narrative game[/h1]  with phenomenal voice acting that makes the whole experience incredibly immersive. The performances rival some of my favorite animated shows and other story-driven games. The inclusion of subtitles for the thick Scottish accents is a great touch and makes everything even more accessible.

[h3]The monster designs are genuinely disturbing[/h3] I definitely had a few moments where I jumped in my seat. The story is gripping and the atmosphere stays tense throughout. While a few plot points are predictable, they still hit hard. Thanks to the incredible voice acting, certain scenes—especially those involving character deaths and the backstory of Caz, the main character, were deeply emotional and even brought me to tears.

Gameplay runs smoothly with only a couple of minor bugs during my playthrough.

[h3]If you enjoy strong storytelling mixed with horror, Still Wakes the Deep is absolutely worth playing.[/h3]

203006375.review: The story is great. The vibes are great. It's sad as hell though so, be warned.  I feel like they nailed the vintage look really well too.  JFC some of the stuff is traumatically scary though (water and narrow spaces).  You can turn off the 'yellow paint' type indicators of where to go if you want a harder experience (apparently some people missed this setting and complained about it in their reviews lmao).  The pacing is absolutely fantastic. None of the high-tension parts are too long where you get numb to it. There are a couple parts where you have to leap to ladders or like this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ helicopter and that's the 'hardest' parts of the game really (I am awful at parkour in almost every game).  Still though, these jumping and grabbing parts are kinda few and far between so you're not swearing up and down for very long or often. 202905150.review:
This isn’t a perfect game by any stretch, but it’s good enough that I came away feeling satisfied with the experience. What really makes it compelling is the atmosphere created by the voice acting and the setting itself. No matter where you are, there’s a constant sense of unease. Indoors, you always feel like something is lurking just out of sight, and outdoors the ocean becomes your nemesis.

The story, however, could have been stronger. It quickly slips into predictability and borders on generic in the way it’s presented. Once the anomalies are introduced, the direction of the plot is easy to guess, which takes away from some of the tension. Gameplay is also pretty barebones, it’s essentially a walking simulator with light stealth segments sprinkled in. The loop boils down to walking, climbing ladders, shimmying across ledges, pulling levers, escaping, and repeat. It’s serviceable, but it does grow repetitive the longer you play.

Despite these flaws, I still enjoyed my time with it. The runtime feels just right, delivering a complete experience without dragging things out. If you enjoy atmospheric walking simulators with a bit of sneaking and want to hear why the Scottish accent really is the best, this is worth checking out. Also, if you want to see how a serious ass game delivers some of the goofiest running I've seen in a horror game.

6.5/10

202626442.review:
To be honest, I have very mixed feelings on Still Wakes The Deep, but because I can really feel the love and passion behind this game, I'm giving it the benefit of the doubt and thumbing it up. I also feel that the things I don't like are more personal preference and not an issue with the game, really.

Let's get the good stuff out of the way: The performances are amazing, the story is engaging, the setting is so well realized, the music is beautiful and the graphics are great (though they do have that UE5 fuzziness that I really dislike).

My issues largely come down to the game's lack of potential and lack of respect for player intelligence. The entire game is earmarked by yellow 'go hear' objects, even if you turn off yellow paint in the settings. It's so constant that it's distracting and considering the game is completely linear with basically nowhere to explore and no alternate paths, it's completely unnecessary. It just gets to a point where it ruins the tension. How am I supposed to be scared of a monster chasing me when the game basically has arrows telling me exactly where to go to avoid it? This compounds with issue two, where it lacks potential. The mechanics are light here, it's basically a walking simulator, and that's perfectly okay. What's NOT okay is that the game has many mechanics that are completely underused making for an experience that's eye-rollingly oversimplified and flat. There was so much potential for interesting scenarios and puzzles to drive up engagement and tension that were totally dismissed. Whenever something blocks your path, say a jet of steam, there is a way around it, the valve to shut it off, within three meters. No puzzle, no clever solutions, no agency. It's really frustrating. Why even put that stuff there if it serves nothing to the game?

Again, overall I think Still Wakes The Deep is a solid game, I just wish they pushed the design more and made it realize its potential. It's the difference between a good game (what this is) and the greats. What a shame.

202560234.review:
!!! WARNING: Possible Spoilers. !!!

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

I'm going to give this game a solid 7.5/10. This is for the main game AND the Siren's Rest DLC. If you're looking for Outlast Meets Subnautica Meets The Thing, This is your game.

Ratings (TL:DR) :

Graphics: 6.9/10
Story: 9/10
Physics: 9/10
Audio (Music, SFX, ETC): 8/10
Difficulty: Very Easy/10
Pacing: 5/10
Realism: 7.8/10
Length: 3/10
Performance: 5.4/10

Worth it at full price? No. Get it on sale, game is worth about $30 including DLC. 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Graphics: The Graphics are quite nice in this game, they're very good in certain areas especially on the Oil Rig and underwater areas, however, it lacks in other areas that threw me off. The fingernails on your own character seem to be welded into the fingers. Grasses and Flowers are all 2-Dimensional, not going good for a game made in 2024. Graphics of the water look pretty dated, but physics of said water are really nice. 

Story: Story was great! This game is packed with an excellent, emotional story. My only gripe is they don't let you get to know anyone very much before throwing you in the game. I felt actually sad seeing certain characters die, I felt sad for others. The characters have very good voice acting. The characters felt human, and while not too fleshed out, they felt alive. The story plays out really well but pacing is a bit quick, yet extremely slow in other areas. 

Physics: Wow.. This game has outstanding physics. A prime example of this is the umbilical cable during the Siren's Rest DLC. It reacts to how you tie it around things, how your hands touch and move it, and how it gets in places so well. The only issue with the umbilical is that it looks to get "stuck" in areas and keeps reeling just fine. (Infinite cable?)
The throwable objects in this game feel really good as well, they land where you aim, and don't glitch or bug.

Audio: Audio in this game is well produced. As I mentioned earlier, the voice acting is great! My only problem is you have to listen very closely just to hear characters murmuring their words before the game suddenly slams your eardrums with loud ambiance and "horror" audio. The sound effects are quite nice, Realistic, solid.

Difficulty: I want to start this section by saying this game is EXTREMELY linear, you can explore a bit off the path but it's very easy to keep on the right path. Puzzles are as simple as "Please press M1 and hold D to unlock this" This ties into one of my issues with the game, it plays out like a lengthy movie rather than a game. 

Pacing: This game has decent pacing, it's right in the middle. On one hand, the game moves pretty fast, one moment you're talking to a few buddies and the next some eldritch horror is trying to gobble you up. On the other, your character you play is slow, unrealistically slow. "Is that a monster chasing me? let me run no faster that the speed of the world's slowest snail to get away from it!" This broke my immersion as it simply bothered me that if it were in any way realistic, the character would move a bit faster. On the bright side, the swimming is faster in the DLC story. 

Realism: The game is VERY realistic other than the bad movement. As mentioned about physics, things feel REAL, your hands don't phase through objects, they touch them and move them. The game feels like the devs genuinely have spent time on an oil rig, studying how they work and how water works. This is only a near 8/10 because of the off walking speeds, the bizarre decisions the characters make, and so on. 

Length: I had to rate this low, and this is one of the biggest issues I have with the game. You're paying (DLC included) Nearly $50 for a 6 hour game, This game can be beat in 6 hours or less if you're quick and don't take time to look at things. Replayability is iffy because of the super slow movement. The base game is done in 4 hours and the DLC 2. I beat both in under both those times and still explored what I could. Alien Isolation as a prime example of a good horror game for the price. $35 for a 22 hour game. You get a LOT of time, much to do, many things to explore, lore highly expanded. Despite being made in 2014, Isolation still holds as one of the best horror games, Then you have games like The Witcher 3, which can be up to a 200 hour game, and is highly replayability. This game does well for it's genre, but if it was 4-8 hours longer, that would absolutely Justify the price. Add some lore, details, character backgrounds. Cutscenes and time spent with said characters. Lot of potential. 

Performance: This one was a mixed bag for me, the base game ran fine, with little to no bugs, except for one occurrence where Muir's player tracking would go off, and he'd either completely ignore me or constantly follow me despite not seeing me. The real bugs happened in the DLC, Siren's Rest. The first bug I encounters happened relatively early, right before you jump off of the cliff into the ruins of the Oil Rig. The game completely froze, my PC was fully functional, but the game was frozen, audio still playing. The second bug happened, causing me later in game to not be able to look around. Mouse worked fine in the menu, could walk, could equip items, but couldn't move the camera. Third bug is that the character would "Scream" as if they were falling when I was standing on solid ground, super random and loud when the game was super quiet. Last and final bug I encountered was getting stuck in doorways and having to restart to checkpoint to fix it. 

All in all this is a SUPER solid game, though I seem to critique it a bit heavily. 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

My personal want for future games by the developers behind this game:

Longer playtime
Lore expansion (1000x more than in game)
Character Development and Backgrounds.
More Unique Creatures
Less Linear Gameplay, no single path, multiple endings, complex puzzles based on difficulty.
Co-op
Less Bugs
Better pacing, faster character, slower story pace.

202303734.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is a survival horror game set in 1975, when a disaster struck the Beira D oil rig off the coast of Scotland. The player's objective: to save the crew before the ship finally sinks, as a storm breaks and a monster approaches.
A horror adventure on a Scottish oil rig, a premise that promises something original.
The game plunges us straight into the atmosphere. We play as McLearly, an electrician who has been laid off, a layoff he considers very unwelcome. The first few minutes are peaceful, under a gray sky, we navigate within sight of the workers, both indoors and outdoors, the images are beautiful and realistic. The voice acting is also excellent, with delightful accents.
The rig quickly becomes the scene of fantastical events, as monsters straight from the depths take over the ship. The game quickly becomes a nautical survival game with a very captivating atmosphere. The threat is disturbing, and it's truly immersive.
What might put some players off, however, is the lack of direct confrontation with the creatures. There are no weapons in the game, nor any combat. For once, I even missed the jump scares.
The game's soundtrack is also very good, but not fully exploited. There was excellent potential; I would have really appreciated seeing even more spectacular things outdoors, especially since the game often allows for this with sequences and wide shots of the ocean... alas, nothing on the horizon.
From start to finish, the game favors and multiplies scenes involving climbing, balancing, and racing. It's very linear and frankly easy. But there's real work on the sensations, which are omnipresent. As for the story, we're intrigued by the events, but the mystery doesn't fully deepen. A blessing in disguise, since we draw the conclusions we want.
On the other hand, the game ends (a little too quickly, 4/5 hours) with a rather pretty and moving ending. This is certainly the only successful emotional touch, since the game can't really say that it really touches our hearts; the characters are obsessive and not very endearing. This is due to its quick prologue, which didn't properly introduce its characters.
All in all, I recommend the game to anyone hoping for a modicum of thrills and a small survival game with a solid atmosphere, graphically perfectly suited to this generation.

14/20

202286769.review:
No spoilers.

Easily one of the best games I've ever played.

I did not know a lot going into this one and I am glad I didn't. I had it sitting in my library for a while but finally got around to it. In hindsight, that ended up being a good thing because it helped to have my recent PC upgrades.

Still Wakes the Deep is The Chinese Room's best game. It takes all of the good parts of their previous titles and irons out the ones people didn't like. At its heart, it is a walking simulator, but of the best kind. Start to finish, the game is immersive and interesting even when the game-play itself is minimal.

Visually, SWTD is among the best looking games I have seen to date. While demanding, the graphics are beautiful. I look forward to the day I can play it at native AA. The environments are carefully crafted, detailed, and feel realistic. The environmental storytelling is flawless. I can't say how accurate a depiction of an oil rig it is, but it certainly felt right. I would assume the developers did their homework.

Characters are where the game really shines. The voice acting is amazing, as is the sound design in general. The characters are animated well and each have memorable personalities. The early conversations feel organic and lifelike. Without spoiling anything, their personalities become important through the rest of the game's events. The player character reacts realistically to the events you experience and comments on just about everything without ever becoming annoying. Also, they're Scottish AF.

In terms of game-play, you're mostly going to be pushing forward through the rig and reacting to simple prompts to interact with the environment. I never got lost or didn't know what to do. Occasionally, you have to be stealthy or run from a threat, but everything you do is in service to the story. You're never far from an interesting event or set piece.

At 4-5 hours, the game does not overstay its welcome or waste your time. The narrative is constantly progressing. There are a few moments to catch your breath, but nothing dull. The tension is always present, as is the sense of urgency. Other than the start of the game, there are no notes to stop and read. The pacing is flawless, yet there are clear points where you can take a break.

As far as scares, I can't speak too much. I have become immune to most scares at this point. I did feel the tension a bit, but I was never terrified and never needed to step away from the game. I would say this game isn't among the scariest out there, but others may disagree.

From a technical standpoint, I did not have any real problems. Some people have encountered bugs but I think most have been ironed out by now. I had none I can recall other than one small soft lock that set me back maybe two minutes. The game has a lot of QoL features and options to tinker with. They did not neglect field of view or cap the frame rate. The game is seriously demanding at higher settings, but DLSS and FSR are present to help. Even at FSR quality, the game looks great. I was able to maintain 120-180 FPS natively with a 7900 XTX. I use a secondary GPU, a 9060 XT, to interpolate frames with Lossless Scaling to get it to 240. I had minimal performance or stuttering issues. This is the rare Unreal Engine 5 game done right.

Well worth buying at full price if you can.

202275388.review: highly recommend for any horror movie fan wanting to experience a great story with fantastic art direction/visuals. especially if you like the direction of Annihilation or The Thing. some might call it a 'walking sim' but i feel like the devs knew what they wanted to make and the linear design suited the story and set-pieces best. i like how physical the controls feel, and the stealth/chase sequences are great. especially with the creature designs, their impressive animations, and fantastic audio throughout. both the base game and dlc are a decent length, telling a good story with no fat to cut. the dlc has a few open exploration sections which was neat for giving a sense of thalassophobia. the dlc was more ambitious than i expected, not re-using the layout of the rig but instead breaking it up into rubble you must navigate in creative ways. now exploring it in a 3D space as a diver, barely recognisable areas now turned upside down and scattered. the visuals were impressive and i could tell referenced real saturation diving footage. same goes for the rig in the base game, dedicated to accuracy and detail. if you enjoy story focused horror games or just wanna see an impressive first person view of an oil rig, pick this game up

202269571.review: This game is good, albeit a bit linear. The acting feels really well done and character deaths do feel significant. The environment and visuals really add to the vibe of the game. I'm not always one for graphics that try to be realistic, but it certainly helped here to make the eldritch horror stand out more. The gameplay itself feels quite good, though I am not used to horror games so I can't comment there too much. Monsters felt balanced (on normal difficulty) and were only sometimes silly (clipping into the camera as you hide in a vent). All in all, very good narrative driven horror game.

202238103.review: The base game is great, the DLC was meh.

202228276.review: Was very fun, good story, great atmosphere.

202224331.review:
Probably the first time I've actually enjoyed a "walking sim" type game. The atmosphere is amazing, the characters, while many of them lack depth (to be fair the vast majority of them really don't get much screen time) still act genuinely human. The few that do get significant screen time are great. The gameplay is fairly simple, mostly walking, climbing, occasional stealth sections, occasional "puzzles" if you can call them that, but the controls for how things are done add a significant amount to the game. The way the entire game looks is stunning, but the visuals on the monsters and the "growth" are a level above.

Definitely worth the price, just don't go in expecting a shooter-style horror game like Resident Evil.

202211477.review: Great game. Amazing atmosphere and provides a great feeling of a truly confined space. Looking forward to the DLC.

202164561.review: Great story! Gameplay little frustrating, nothing brakes tension like trying one QTE jump for 15th time

202162767.review:
Finally, a horror game where the protagonist swears as much as the player, and is also Scottish.

Voice acting and visuals are the strongest points, they seriously did a great job there.
The screenshots and trailers are accurate. I had to lower all the settings a little for performance, though.

The atmosphere is so good I can almost smell the seawater.

There's a lot of gameplay for a game by The Chinese Room, which still isn't a lot but enough to not be boring. I'm glad for it because I'm not really a fan of walking sims. This feels like a very linear, basic version of the sneak-and-run horror genre.
Some control mechanics can be confusing at first. It's annoying to keep dying at the same parts because of them, but it's easy after I figured out what I'm supposed to do.

Story is standard for horror, a little trope-y and not as poetic as other TCR games, which can be good or bad depending on what you like. I expected more but it's pretty solid as it is. I'm satisfied by it and didn't think the ending was bad. Good tie-in with the beginning, if you remember. I only wish the story went deeper and had the emotional impact it seemed to have on others.

Overall, I enjoyed playing this game and recommend getting it on sale. It's short and I think there's too little for the full price.

202161707.review:
3/5 - underwhelming story
voice acting is top notch, others might hate the gameplay but i really loved it - it's more like a walking simulator survival horror, no looting, no healing, no weapons, just hide and move which i really like since it doesn't stress me out.
however the story is just average. mc leaves broken/dead family/family members just to revisit the trauma through the supernatural horror stuff, i didnt like the story very much.

202147302.review: Absolutely incredible game, haunting re-imagining of the Piper Alpha Disaster.

202146304.review: I really liked game and someone may say that ending was boring or something like that but I think that's the best ending they could make.

202130970.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is a flawed game with a good setting, characters, and story.

The Chinese Room are known for pretty poorly made walking simulators and this one managed to only be slightly better than their previous titles in that you can now climb. Broken graphics, levels bloated with hiding spots, and the main character talking/yelling in moments where he's supposed to be quiet are unfortunately some of the more egregious annoyances throughout the game.

However, the voice acting is top tier and the setting is incredibly well done. If you weren't scared of oil rigs before then this game will certainly install a level of fear of working on one. Although the story is a bit rocky at times, it nails some of the more crucial parts, especially the ending.

Overall, while it's got some major flaws it still manages to be a worthwhile horror experience.

202129059.review:
This game has become my hyperfixation! I have been working for all achievements and played the whole thing multiple times because there's just so much to see and I keep noticing new details! I love that they used authentic real Scottish voice actors and the realism is so good! Plus they hurt you real hard by letting you get to know everyone at the start and feeling the dynamic between everyone before the forbidden bacon attacks. The game is well-designed and I love how you can see how things progress and get worse as you revisit places you already were, and I enjoy the meaningfulness of the ending with Caz finally facing his problems instead of running away, while possibly saving his family in the process! The music and voice acting is really good and you can tell that the developers and everyone else involved was passionate about this project!

(Spoilers for DLC below)

I love the DLC too, there's some things that could've been improved(like getting that damn flare out of my face-) and some gameplay confusion, but I kind of like how it's disorienting, it's realistic! I disagree with those who complain about Mhairi being related to Brodie and them calling Brodie "insignificant", he quite literally helps you the whole way through the main game AND we get to know him quite well before he's gone! So shut, he is NOT insignificant! I'd argue most of the character's are not insignificant due to how human the game makes them feel. The game does a good job on keeping you on edge with suspense, but while I can understand why people aren't happy that the creature wasn't around long, I do not believe that ruins the game! It has equally as great audio design as the first game and I enjoy the more free-roaming aspect of it and the branching ending! I also love how it showed more lives lost to the Baira D. Incident and other locations we didn't see as Caz, while also making a more disliked character, Rennick, feel more human and his motivations more understandable.



Summary:

Main Game: 11/10
I loved everything about it from the acting, to the game design, to the story, to the audio!

DLC: 8/10
Has some things that could've been better, mostly gameplay-wise, but is worth the buy! Has incredible audio design and the environment is well designed, plus the story is interesting and adds on well to the Main Game.

202108314.review:
[i][url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/35145066/] My review page.[/url][/i] 
[i][url=https://www.twitch.tv/nightserg] Follow me on twitch![/url][/i]

[h1]The gist of it:[/h1]
I bought Still Wakes the Deep based on its hype, and despite being extremely short, it was an enjoyable experience, though I kinda wished it was a memorable one. 

👍 [u]The good parts:[/u]
+Great atmosphere
+Well done audio design

👎[u]The bad parts:[/u]
-Unreal Engine 5 and its shenanigans
-Overpriced for its quality/content, especially gameplay-wise(6 hours includes also dlc...yeah..)

[h1]Story[/h1]
The story won't blow anyone's minds, despite what some would say, unless this is your first horror game played. It's pretty much the standard trope of being stuck somewhere secluded, and you have to escape the monstrous presence with some drama in between about your past and family. Yeah, exactly, the same as Amnesia and all of its clones. 

It saddens me that the horror genre, despite having the power to do absolutely horrific things, most don't take any risks at all. There is nothing in the game's story that will blow your mind. The characters are all well written, but the horror aspect, as always, is never properly explained, nor does it do anything special other than to chase you and push you towards the end.

The game is essentially a safe product in the horror genre, a product that attracts simple-minded people and journalists' praise. 

Story rating: 7/10

[h1]Gameplay[/h1]
Yes, it's a walking simulator with very limited "parkour" moments. There is no combat; the "stealth" moments are very basic and short, and there is basically nothing aside from pressing forward and reaching the end. A lot of newer horror games have adapted puzzle mechanics to keep things tense and the player thinking; however, It Takes Deep doesn't do anything special. There is nothing else worth mentioning because the game has nothing else aside from moving forward. There are certain moments where you have to "heat" yourself, but it has no actual implication other than the game just forcing you to do that on that spot. Never else in the game will you ever die of cold or whatever.

Gameplay rating: 6/10

[h1]Audio[/h1]
Luckily, the audio design is well-crafted, and the sound effects do a great job at creating tension throughout the game. The voice acting is also really well done and will no doubt immerse you in its time and location. 

Audio rating: 7.5/10

[h1]Graphics, performance and tech analysis[/h1]
Yes, it's Unreal Engine 5, which means its performance/visuals ratio is abysmal. Expect stuttering, expect visual inconsistency, expect light bleeding, etc. That being said, the atmosphere is gorgeous, being stranded on a rig was a pretty great idea, and if you're hydrophobic I would highly advise not playing the game. It's a shame the level design never opens up like, say, in Alien Isolation, despite the location allowing for it. There is never any freedom; you just move forward, and that's it. 

Graphics, performance, and tech analysis score: 7.5/10

[h1]Conclusion[/h1]
It's an okay game, a lesser version of Amnesia, but with modern visuals and a cooler atmosphere. Get it only on sale.

[h1]Final score: 7/10[/h1]

202079832.review: It's just amazing. All of it. It's scary where it needs to be, the audio is genuinely great, and it's thrilling almost always. 10/10, would absolutely love to replay. 202076034.review:
The Good:
Everyone says it and everyone is right - this game has stellar sound design, music and voice acting. The visuals and atmosphere are amazing, some scenes in the game were as gorgeous as they were Lovecraftian. I am impressed by the detail that was put into the oil rig, the place felt real.

The Bad:
The controls can be very frustrating and it is easy to get stuck on things. Some places were tough to get through but not because it is intentionally difficult, just because I'd bump into invisible walls. Also, the monsters are not very smart, their behaviour is extremely simple and repetitive. Plus they'd get stuck sometimes which shattered the atmosphere.

The Meh:
Most of everything else, if I'm quite honest. The characters were not given much time so they felt flat and their deaths didn't affect me. One death in particular was treated like a big moment but it felt like the game was trying really hard to get a tear-jerker and did not succeed. The protagonist and his personal story felt disconnected from the main conflict so during flashbacks to the arguments with his wife I saw it as a pretty shallow attempt to create depth. And I don't want to be too harsh, clearly this story worked for a lot of people and I'm happy for them, but for me the storytelling felt weak.
The eldritch horror was underutilised and the monsters (while cool-looking) were same-y and flat. I would have loved to understand more about the mysterious powers that overtake the rig, maybe see a clear connection between what everyone was like in life and how they ended up, but it doesn't seem that the game was interested in exploring that very much. 
Overall the story, the characters and the pacing felt under-cooked and that is a shame. I do see the bones of a great game somewhere in there, but in its current permutation it is pretty average.

Conclusion:
I truly wanted to like this game. I'm a fan of maritime horror and a lot of people recommended this one. But as the sum of its parts Still Wakes the Deep lacks impact. The story feels weak and if the game was longer and was given more time to develop its themes and concepts maybe it could have worked. As it is, this is not the standout modern horror I so wanted it to be. I'd say buy it on a discount.

202054317.review:

The Good stuff.
Cool concept. Oil rigs are underused in gaming imho. Cool enemy design. Who doesn't like the John Carpenter's the Thing.
Voice acting is great, love me some Scottish. And I really liked the protagonist jumping and falling sounds. VAs done a fantastic job.

I really liked the contros, felt immersive. 

Sound design, ok nothing special.

The Bad stuff 
Horribly optimized, massive FPS Drops goes under 60 sometimes with a high end pc (9070 XT), it's not unplayable but it is noticable. forced upscaling,looks terrible at some places, even though it's generally a good looking game. 

Yellow paint EVERYWHERE 
Even tho I turned it down in the settings. This game is more linear than a straight line. You don't need the yellow paint. You physically can't get lost in this game, it's impossible.

Monsters 
Can't scoop you out with their 12ft long tentacles from your hiding spots because you are being protected by an invisible forcefield, pretty immersion breaking and makes it immediately feel like, yepp, I'm in a game, here's how to cheese the mechanics.
And in the entire game all enemies are doing is they patrol in a circle in an arena, and sometimes they chase you in a scripted sequence. Riveting.

The swimming sections are terrible. 
Swimming is painfully slow. And I'm not sure what do they add to the game really. Also drowned a couple of times because I thougt that the protag will go up for some air through a square mile wide gap, only to bump his head in an invisible wall.

Dumb Achievements like:

And multiple achis involve dying in different ways, and you know what kills immersion in a horror game completely? That's right, dying over and over again. 

It is extremely predatory to attach the overpriced, short, badly reviewed DLC's achievents to the base game's completion. I don't wanna buy that, but now I can't 100% the game.

201937619.review:
The Chinese Room have mastered the linear walking-sim/horror formula they've been toiling with since [i]Amnesia: A Machine for Pigs[/i]. Sure, make fun of them for how simple and bareknuckled their games are, but the specific type of experience you get with [i]Still Wakes the Deep[/i] is something really hard to pull off I think. This game simply [b]flows[/b], it has better pacing and tonal consistency than about 98% of games on the market. It just goes from setpiece to setpiece, be it environmental, adversarial or simply narrative, with total ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ confidence and it nails it just about every damn time. It's a socialist-leaning, [i]The Thing[/i]-coded, lovecraftian body horror rollercoaster, and I loved nearly every second of it. Made me tear up a couple of times as well, how about that?

Bought it on sale, but would've gladly paid the full price.

201883538.review:
One of the best games I have ever played. Period. 

The writing, the vocal performances, the atmosphere, the lighting, set/level design, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ everything is absolutely gorgeous. Body horror and inexplicable, supernatural phenomena are two of my favourite tropes in horror that many people go too far with but Still Wakes the Deep is a master at it through and through. Stunningly grotesque images and a horrible (complementary) atmosphere to deliver them to the player like they're tied up in a fleshy misshapen bow. 

Both the Main game and the DLC brought me to tears in their finales, I cannot recommend them enough. 

P.S. Play the main game in monochrome, and the DLC in full colour. You wont regret it.
