201855530.review:
I don't like to use the phrase "Lovecraftian" as it tends to be used for anything involving tentacled monsters, with most games forgetting the key part of HP's stories; fear of the unknown. Having said that, this is a pretty Lovecraftian experience. Combining the isolation of being out in the ocean, the terror of not knowing what's going on, warped creatures that are truly hard to describe and a deeply human story, I think this game ticks the boxes. It also manages to combine some aspects of John Carpenter's "The Thing" with HP's best work: "The Colour Out Of Space" in a way that I am thoroughly enjoying.

The voice acting is excellent and immersive. The sound design is fantastic (use headphone) and the story unravels in a pacey but not rushed way. The graphics are beautiful and capture the feel of the game perfectly.

Yes this game is linear, but that word isn't a slur, simply a description. There are plenty of forgettable open world experiences out there that could have done with being scaled down. This game has a story to tell and it does it in the right way. Having been bored to tears by massive, empty maps, this game is a nice change!

Recommended for all horror fans!

201846930.review:
I bought this on impulse while it was on sale, and while I recommend it I only recommend the whole package (base game + dlc). However, I think most people who enjoy the base game will not enjoy the dlc and vice versa, as they're very different experiences.

The base game is about 4 hours; there is only a single ending and the game is completely linear. It's a walking simulator with occasional "avoid the monster" sections. There are no offensive tools; pretty much your only option for distractions is to throw clutter objects. In other words, the game is a much shorter SOMA without the metahumanist narrative. What really stands out about the base game is the voice acting; performances are fantastic across the board. There are just a couple places where the writing falters and lets down the actors a bit. 

The story unfolding across the oil rig is pretty compelling in that it motivates you to see the end, but I don't think it's a good story. What really bothers me are the flashbacks, which only serve two purposes: to develop the main character and to demonstrate his deteriorating mental state. However, nothing in the flashbacks matters. The story would be completely unchanged without them. If you replaced them entirely with audio and visual artefacts during gameplay nothing of value would be lost. There's not really any payoff to this concept. 

The dlc is entirely different; it's about two hours, is almost entirely swimming, has two endings and while it still has a linear structure, there are lots of large or branching areas to poke around in. The player is rewarded for exploration with dialogue and collectibles. There is no story impact, but the additional dialogue is interesting and worth hearing. It plays more like an investigative adventure game with horror elements than the base game's more straightforward body horror. Personally, I really enjoyed the dlc and found its atmosphere and slow buildup more affecting than anything in the base game, and without the dlc I would not be recommending the game. However, there is only one monster section at the very end, so people who are looking for that will not really find it in the dlc.

I think if they took the concept of the dlc, made it the main game, and had the player uncover the events of the main game through investigation, while adding some more enemy encounters, the resulting game would have been much more compelling. It's still worth playing, but depending on how many of these sorts of games you've played you might find the base game disappointing, and if you are really into the body horror and chase aspects you will find the dlc disappointing.

201775072.review:
Still Wakes the Deep offers a fresh and intriguing take on the survival roguelike genre, combining atmospheric exploration with intense underwater combat and procedural storytelling. Developed by Dan M. Hart and published on Steam, this game invites players into a vast, mysterious ocean world filled with strange creatures, hidden secrets, and treacherous terrain. Unlike traditional roguelikes that rely on dungeon crawling or fantasy tropes, Still Wakes the Deep crafts an immersive maritime experience, where the ocean itself feels like a living, breathing antagonist.

At its core, the game centers around a lone diver piloting a fragile submersible through the depths of an uncharted sea. The visuals strike a perfect balance between stylized minimalism and detailed environmental effects, creating a moody, almost haunting ambiance. The ocean is depicted with dark blues and subtle light play, emphasizing the isolation and danger lurking beneath the waves. The world feels expansive and unpredictable; every dive uncovers new biomes, from eerie sunken ruins to vast coral labyrinths, each populated by hostile creatures that challenge the player’s skills and nerve.

Exploration is the heartbeat of Still Wakes the Deep. Procedural generation ensures that no two journeys are identical, offering a strong replayability factor. Players must manage resources carefully — oxygen, power for the submersible, and ammunition — all while navigating hazardous underwater terrain. The game’s survival mechanics are unforgiving but fair, rewarding cautious, strategic play rather than reckless bravado. Discovering caches of supplies or unlocking shortcuts feels genuinely thrilling, especially as each descent pushes the player deeper into unknown territory.

Combat in the game is tactical and pulse-pounding. Players must engage with a variety of sea creatures, each with distinct behaviors and attack patterns. The submersible’s weapons are limited and require precise aiming and timing, turning fights into tense, almost puzzle-like encounters. The threat of damage is always looming, as even a few hits can force a retreat or a carefully calculated risk. This tension is heightened by the game’s dynamic sound design—ambient ocean noises mixed with the sudden bursts of creature growls or mechanical alarms keep players on edge throughout their expeditions.

Storytelling in Still Wakes the Deep is subtle and atmospheric, relying more on environmental clues and emergent narratives than on traditional dialogue or cutscenes. Logs, fragments of lore, and cryptic messages scattered across the ocean floor hint at a greater mystery behind the submerged world and its strange inhabitants. This approach encourages players to piece together the backstory organically, deepening immersion and sparking curiosity about the ocean’s secrets. The lack of a heavy-handed narrative allows the mood and atmosphere to take center stage, making each dive feel like an unfolding chapter in a larger, enigmatic tale.

The game also features a progression system that encourages repeated playthroughs. Upgrades to the submersible can be unlocked between dives, allowing players to improve hull strength, weaponry, and mobility. These upgrades provide a satisfying sense of growth and mastery, essential in a roguelike where initial runs may feel punishing. The combination of procedural generation, escalating difficulty, and upgrade options strikes a good balance, keeping players engaged without frustrating them unduly.

While Still Wakes the Deep excels in atmosphere and mechanics, some players might find the learning curve steep. The game demands patience and careful observation, and newcomers to roguelikes or survival games might struggle with its unforgiving nature and sparse tutorialization. Additionally, the minimalist art style and slow pacing may not appeal to those seeking high-octane action or flashy visuals. However, for players willing to immerse themselves in its unique underwater world, the game offers a richly rewarding experience that feels distinct and memorable.

In conclusion, Still Wakes the Deep is a beautifully crafted and thoughtfully designed survival roguelike that captures the mystery and danger of deep-sea exploration. Its combination of atmospheric visuals, strategic combat, and procedural storytelling creates an engaging experience that stands out in a crowded genre. The game’s tension, exploration, and subtle narrative weave together to form a haunting adventure beneath the waves, inviting players to confront the unknown and survive its many perils. For those looking for a challenging and atmospheric journey into the depths, Still Wakes the Deep delivers a unique and captivating experience that resonates long after the submersible surfaces.

Rating: 8/10

201730875.review:
An incredibly beautiful, scary and well-written (and voiced) survival horror game that isolates you on an oil rig (that drilled too deep) and forces you to find a way off and to safety. 

This game is gorgeous. The colors, the environments, from the raging waves to whatever is lurking - it's so pretty and scary at the same time. The writing is great, you get fully realized characters with awesome voice acting and enough personality to successfully pull them out of any cliché trap. 

The pacing is great. There was not a single moment when I thought it took too long or was rushing, the balance of action vs. exploration was just right. And I loved the story. 

Above all: this was scary. The setting is genius, because the open sea alone is scary as heck, coupled with something weird going on in such a confined space created a tense atmosphere and I like that the game avoided cheap jumpscares and also kept the horror balanced, so you don't get bored by it. 

One of the best horror games in recent memory. Just outstanding.

201725754.review:
It's an interactive fiction story overall. You are playing to experience the story only. I can't say there is much game play in the traditional sense but you are always active. Some aspects of stealth felt a bit jankie and dying for some unknown reason, can ruin the mood. It has two modes, but i think you will get the full effect either way.

The story was good. I do like these types of stories like Cloverfield and the "found footage" style. Overall it was a good 4 hours. Beat it in one sitting. I honestly feel a little sad because a lot of work and effort went into this, and it feels short. Characters are well acted and they do a great job. The only downside is that, getting to even know everyone and to then recognize who they are later was difficult. I relied on Caz's dialogue and realizations to carry through, but there was a lack of context for a lot of the crew. Maybe talking to more people at the start might have helped but even then those you meet once, and then things go sideways, it's kind of a rush.

Scare factor was comfortable to me. I don't usually like horror games, but this was suspense more than horror, which fits well. There are secrets and stuff for achievements but unless you want to 100% the game, I don't really see the point. I personally wasn't going to keep searching for stuff.

Overall, I felt like Caz, learned about him, and got to the end of the game. I can recommend this to anyone that is interested in "interactive fiction" more interactive than Visual Novels and less action than God of War (2018). I might have wanted more, but that's just my old habits of games that take 40+ hours to complete. It's a year out, so a sale is worth it. I may get the DLC just to see what it's like, but it's even shorter so I'll likely wait.

Buy at full price only if you really want to give the money to the studio. It's worth a sale now that it's a year old.

201713183.review:
I thought this game should entartain me because I'm kinda into this genre. So I bought without checking which engine it runs and how it performs. Because it's not a triple A game, it's a story driven walking sim and should work just fine on a ordinary rtx card. But afterwards I'm shockingly devastated. 

Horrible optimization. Probably because UE5 and locked Lumen so they did not have to design lighting manually. This is a perfect example of how developers started to being literally lazy. They could have done most of the lighting baked (static) to gain immense amount of performance and maintain similar visual fidelity. Because most of the lighting in this game doesn't require dynamic GI aesthetically. At least they should have provide more optimized lighting at lower graphic settings. But no, they only want flip a switch and let lumen does all lighting automatically for them. I should not have to pay for some 4090 in order to play this basic story game smoothly. Again I'm not complaining about game itself. I'm sure it's awesome atmospheric horror which I really enjoy. It's really sad that I have to spend thousands of dollars in order to experience it as it intended.

And I can't even return it because steam says you can't refund purchases in 14 days. It's a real shame that I didn't have a chance to try it out in 14 days. Lesson learned I suppose.

201349253.review:
This is a review of both the base game and the DLC as I don't have that dog in me to write two separate reviews for six hours of content. It's somewhat of a semi-spoiler free, so feel free to read if you aren't scared to know the basic concepts of the game before probably playing it.

Short story - it's not that good. Definitely not as good as some would say. My honest review would've been neutral but since Steam is divisive like that, I'm not recommending paying for this.

Long story - this game was marketed to me by my peers as the best Lovecraftian horror out there. It's not. It's not CLAIMING to be one outright, but it kinda has the vibes and whatnot so I might compare the game to other Lovecraftian games later.

Let's start with the good parts. There's precisely two good parts:
1. The characters
The characters are good, and they have little right to be this good because they're good for two reasons - them being Scottish and the voice actors delivering a spectacular performance. You believe in the characters, you feel for them, and all of that because the voice actors and the writers sold you those characters. It's good and you worrying for the characters themselves makes up for the lackluster horror aspects I will be covering later on.
2. The curbside appeal, including sound design, graphics and interior design
The sounds of the game are great. Can't say I remember the OST but the monsters' screeches, the oil rig's creaking, the machinery, yada-yada, it all sounds just as it should sound. It sells you the idea of what's going on.
The graphics are good, although I've seen better work of lighting (we will cover the topic of how the game runs later on), the interior design is really, really good, although I didn't get enough claustrophobia and thalassophobia, but we will cover that later on as well. When you walk around the rig you feel like you are there. It's really good.

Alright, done and done with the good parts, let's talk bad ones.
1. Performance. The big bad for me.
This game WANTS to be played at 60 fps. It hates your PC.
I have a 4070, i5-13400K, 32 gigs worth of DDR5 RAM and an M2 SSD. I can safely run modern games at 100fps and more with a 2k monitor on ultra settings. This game alongside with Battlefront 2 (only on Geonosis for some reason) are the worst optimised games I've ever encountered. DLSS or not, the drops are abhorrent, the performance is around 60-70fps which doesn't really sync with my 155Hz monitor. The DLC avg fps was 50-60, and my PC's temperature wasn't even that high. I suppose the game just isn't using everything your own rig might offer. Oh, and by the way...
UE5 SUCKS. 

2. The horror. The Lovecraftian horror. Boo.
Not scary. I am not a conoisseur of horror games, although I enjoy a good spook every now and then. I like all kinds of horror. My last walking sim horror game was probably SOMA iirc, and it was scarier than Still Wakes the Deep. 
This game is SAFE. Like, really safe. You get a carefully curated experience where the monsters can't reach you if you are considered to be in the cover, even though they very well could and should be able to do that. In this game you are being guided by hand and you get a ton of resources to distract the monsters, making them a practical non-issue. The most tension is when the game makes you worry for other characters. 
The Lovecraftian aspect - it's pretty mild. 
The Lovecraftian aspect doesn't have to include specific mythos or have the specific themes of tentacles or the ever-present Cthulhu, but the Lovecraftian horror is really a space horror, a futile fight between a person and a force that is on another level of existence. You can grasp certain elements of it, maybe know at least something about it, but you never know the full picture.
This game had its Lovecraftian "we never had a chance" moments, but they were so few (only a couple) and far between (the middle and the end of the game) that the monstrosities you encounter mostly remind of Dead Space or Resident Evil. Those are mostly formless abominations who have only a speck of their former selves inside them. Two of them, Muir and Trots specifically, have interesting designs, but the rest fall flat. 
I know that 2018's Call of Cthulhu is a mediocre game with a mishmash of Lovecraftian themes, but I got more fun playing it than playing SWtD.
If you want your fear of the oceanic depth to kick in, feel free to play Subnautica. 
If you want your Lovecraftian stuff, play Darkest Dungeon.

3. The gameplay. 
Sheesh it's boring. You have three gameplay loops - you explore the linear surroundings, you play hide-and-seek, you run from something. I know that those are all the walking sim horrors, but this... this is just nothing. The hide-and-seek tension is non-existent due to the huge amount of resources you get to get rid of the monster. The running away aspect is also really mid because it's really just a QTE section, and there's too little exploration due to the game being severely linear.

I'd say that's about it! Now for the DLC.

It has none of the pros of the base game, except for the decent voice actors.
Two thirds of the DLC you don't even get anything trying to kill you, you simply explore a semi-open area, then a corridor, then a semi-open area again, while learning about what happened to the rig's workers. All that, except for a couple of 5-minute sections, happens underwater, too, and one would be tempted to say that it sounds thalassophobic and claustrophobic, but it's so slow and curated and calm that you really don't feel threatened by the surroundings.
The performance is even worse, as mentioned prior.
The last part of the DLC is a small hide-and-seek section, then a running (swimming) section, and then roll credits.
Worse than the base game, sorry devs. Even if the base game would be worth the money, which it's kinda not imo, the DLC is definitely bad.
And if you enjoy the vibe of someone visiting an abandoned marine vessel or building to ascertain what happened to the crew, you can just play Obra Dinn! It's a wonderful game. No horror elements in there, but it's ten times more interesting.

ALRIGHT THAT'S KINDA IT TY FOR READING!

5.5/10 – Average horror

Story is nothing impressive or unexpected. One of the most standard plots with standard ending.
Graphics are good, I like the setting and details.
Voice acting is very good.
Gameplay is not far from original Outlast: hide, run, activate things, repeat. Only without health/camera management.
Monsters are bad. They don't look scary, just your average blob of flesh. The only exceptional feature is the way they move.
Didn't see any serious bugs in main game.

It isn't really scary, maybe because camera doesn't lock on scenes that are supposed to build up suspense. So except for direct encounters, I didn't see monsters—only heard MC gasp and scream. And sea horror aspect is almost non-existent. It's only there to threaten you with the rig sinking before you run away.

Wish monsters and plot were better. Bought it expecting Lovecraft horrors, but it's nowhere close. It didn't even touch my thalassophobia.


DLC – 3/10

The best part of it is initial exploration in Chapter 1.
Chapter 3 is the biggest letdown with just constant chase and poor water physics. "Propel" action throws you forward right into another object, and by the time you swim over it, you're already dead.
Chapter 2 had a game-breaking bug for me. I just swam to the objective and got a cutscene, with some dialogue overlapping. After the cutscene ended, I appeared near the entrance of the wreck and could just casually swim back to the bell. Needed to check a walkthrough to understand what happened, why the marker “find exit” was somewhere below me, and why I couldn't even use the flashlight.

Story is very poor. It's almost non-existent past the first chapter. Got trip underwater – magic – found dad – credits.

It would be much better if the DLC was just like Chapter 1 but across the whole rig, with MC and Rob's comments and talks while they uncover what happened.

201300603.review:
I liked Siren's Rest for how it changes things up from the main game. To some extent, I like The Chinese Room's 'walking sim'-type games better when you're bearing witness to things that have happened; there's something a bit too animatronics-like to the non-player characters of Still Wakes the Deep (not a knock on TCR's abilities - the NPCs are well written, performed, and believably animated, but they aren't, and can't be, reactive, which makes them feel quite similar to the recorded voices and animations of Everybody's Gone to the Rapture when they shouldn't). In that sense, Siren's Rest works more like earlier games of TCM.

Where Siren's Rest doesn't work for me, much like the main game, is when there's action gameplay - because once you die, it never feels like anything other than a game. There's not enough interesting gameplay here, and what there is feels a bit like a more modern version of the old Dragon's Lair games. I understand that TCM is trying to react to criticism ('not enough game in this game!') and move out of their relatively narrow wheel house, but these bits aren't great as a game. To some extent both Siren's Rest and the main game feel a bit stuck uncomfortably between earlier TCM titles and something like Alien Isolation. For me, their games work better when they're basically site-specific theatre in video game format.

Nonetheless, there's a lot of beautiful work in this, and it's worth checking out Siren's Rest just for the sense of place.

201259797.review:
This one's a proper Lovecraftian horror — just you and some cosmic wankery that’s mutated half the rig and probably your sense of hope too.

The "monsters"? Aye, they look like yer maw before she’s had a coffee and forgiven your existence. Not exactly terrifying — unless yer into damp, fleshy regret.

It's no Alien: Isolation though. That game? I finished it once, sure. But I hit the infirmary, panicked like a wee bairn, uninstalled it for two years, then came back to finish it outta sheer spite. Never again. That infirmary can rot in hell.

But this? This is different. Still scary, aye. But it’s atmospheric — dread builds like mould in a Glasgow flat. No guns. Just survival, smarts, and shouting “oh f*ck this!” while knee-deep in sea water and psychological damage.

Noise will betray ye. Or save ye. Sometimes both in the same f*ckin’ minute.

But the story? That’s where it shines. It's human. You care. You sweat. You slip on a pipe and scream. And you keep goin', 'cause maybe — just maybe — you’ll make it out without bein’ turned into seafood soup.

Pros
✔️ Heavy accents that could cut steel
✔️ Real fear, not cheap jumpscares
✔️ Wet horror done right

Cons
✖️ Can’t fight back. Just run and swear.
✖️ Everything’s slippery and full of existential goo
✖️ Monster sounds like yer da snorin’ on a Sunday

Final verdict:
If you ever wanted to experience cosmic horror while shoutin’ “YA WITLESS CREEPIN’ MUTANT!” at the dark — this game’s for you.
And me.
And every poor sod who’s ever worked a night shift and said “this job might actually kill me.”

🌊 Bring a raincoat. And maybe a priest.

This game is an absolute masterclass of Lovecraftian Horror. Truly, it feels like this game has it all: the visuals are stunning, the story tugs at your heartstrings, the sound design/voice acting is immaculate, and the scares left me feeling more unsettled than any other game I've played. A perfect blend between ethereal beauty and gut-churning gore. 

I have very few complaints, which in the grand scheme I find do not take away from the experience. I had a handful of frustrating moments when navigating around the rig (truth be told I died more to falling/the environment than any of the monsters), most of which were difficult due to a bit of lag (however that could very well be an issue with my laptop more than anything, so take that with a grain of salt).

I highly suggest any fan of Lovecraftian or Cosmic Horror check this game out. The game itself isn't overly difficult. I played the Standard difficulty and found the game is very generous with hiding spots and objects to use as a distractions. Exploration is fairly linear and you can choose to have more visual clues in the environment to help you find your way. As well as, there is an easier difficulty setting if you're just wanting to experience the story.
Overall the game feels like it was designed for the player to be able to progress somewhat easily, as the story and environmental designs are a huge highlight of the game. 

My Ratings Include, But Are Not Limited To: 
5 Stars
A Real 10/Good
23/23 Cosmic Squiddy Bois

My recommendation cannot be understated!

200804368.review:
[h1][b] TLDR: Terrible controls, boring mechanics, and an underwhelming and predictable narrative make me not recommend this otherwise beautiful looking walking simulator. [/b] [/h1]

[h1]PROS: [/h1]
➕Excellent graphics

[h1]CONS: [/h1]
➖Unable to take two simple actions at once (holding item + open door/crouch)
➖World lacks meaningful interactivity/exploration
➖Predictable and obvious QTEs
➖Terrible controls, especially underwater

[h1] [b] Content (Game world/Narrative) – [6/10] [/b] [/h1]
The game world takes place from a first-person perspective. You play as a worker on a Scotland oil rig. The world is beautifully created and has a look-and-feel of a lived-in environment, but also contains numerous professional-looking items that would feel at home on an oil rig. I enjoy the world because the oil rig aspect feels faithful, but also the setting of the 1970s is great with the technology. I feel like this setting of Stranger Things mixed with The Thing. 

The narrative starts out innocently enough, 10 minutes into the game you get fired, and 5 minutes later a portal to hell is opened. You then spend the rest of the game going all over the ship to prevent it from sinking, only to [spoiler]end up blowing it up anyways[/spoiler]. I didn’t get much of a narrative pull, considering the majority of the time you’re just listening to the experts of the rig tell you what you need to do next while you, a simple electrician, have no idea the purpose of the levers and buttons you’re hitting. 

I would probably rate the game world alone an 8, but the narrative wasn’t great and by itself I’d probably give it a 5, as such, I landed on a 6 for this category, because the game world gradually dissipates into a nonsensical cluster of objects and goop as the game progresses. 

[h1][b] Gameplay (Mechanics/Difficulty) – [2/10] [/b] [/h1]
The mechanics I found were very disappointing. For all intents and purposes, this is a WALKING SIMULATOR. The interactivity the player has is so minimal and basic, I felt that the devs made even the simplest interactions have multiple button presses in an effort to add another layer of “Gameplay” onto the game. Things like climbing ladders take 2 button presses, turning on a heater, 2 buttons, closing a valve, 2 buttons. It's small, but meaningless considering how non-interactive the rest of the game is. 
To also expand on the controls, I literally died multiple times from how awful they are. There are numerous platforming sections where you must sprint, well sprint seems to only work half the time. The “Toggle sprint” option might be bugged, I’m not sure. The underwater sections are the worst - you swim like an atrophied slug. There’s few parts where you have to use some sort of “Boost” underwater by grabbing onto a ledge and propelling yourself forward. This just never works for me and you have to hit a combination of right click, W, space bar, and left click. It's just bad. 
When you encounter monsters, the only thing you can do is hide and throw objects to distract them. It's so contrived that you’ll notice tons of crawlspaces and places to hide with random items like bottoms and hammers deliberately placed near the hiding spots. Like can you get any more obvious? 

I found the difficulty to be fair. There were many places I did end up dying but it's not so much because it's a hard part, but because the controls are generally awful, especially the underwater parts. 

[h1] [b] Graphics (Quality/Technical) – [8/10] [/b] [/h1]
Superb. I absolutely love the art and fidelity. The game has a ton of modern graphical options like DLSS and even AMD/Intel options. Everything looks simply gorgeous. 

I played with all settings maxed and did notice some slight stutters, but it was tolerable as I played it for a bit. With maxed settings, I averaged about ~80-100 FPS with my hardware. 
There were some graphical bugs, as well as some movement bugs I encountered. Things like being attacked through walls, epilepsy inducing areas with my flashlight against specific walls, and just general animations being clearly off. Nothing game breaking though. 
i9-13900KF | RTX 4090 | 64GB RAM | 1440p @ 240hz | Windows 11 installed on SSD 

[h1] [b] Audio – [7/10] [/b] [/h1]
Audio is solid. There isn’t much music, but the environmental effects and atmosphere sound good. Perhaps the biggest complaint I have with the audio is its very noticeably quiet. I had to crank up the volume double what I normally play on. 

There is ample voice acting, and being set on a Scottish oil rig, many of the voices are, surprise, Scottish! So while I do love the accent and its faithfully represented in the game, the Scottish accent can be very thick and full of colloquialisms. I find it funny that the subtitles display the “Translated” meaning in English while the actors speak in traditional Scottish. 
Also, the main character swears like a drunken sailor, love it. Cause I too would be screaming F-bombs repeatedly as I’m being chased through the hallway by a literal giant head. 

[h1] [b] Replay Factor (Longevity) – [2/10] [/b] [/h1]
Not great, aside from a ton of achievements, including some missable, there isn’t a great reason to replay this. It's a linear walking simulator with zero exploration or collectables. 

[h1] [b] Final Verdict – [5/10] [/b] [/h1]
Still Wakes the Deep (SWD) is an interesting game. Its horror isn’t too terrifying, it's a decent take on Lovecraftian horror in a place you wouldn’t expect- an oil rig. So while the setting is good, and the graphics are too, I was immensely let down by the mechanics. 

Biggest negative for me = Mechanics.
Biggest positive for me = Graphics. 

I am generally a big fan of the “Walking Simulator” genre, but I believe in order to be light on the mechanics, you must have at the very least an interesting narrative. Something to distract the player from, well, not really doing anything else. Unfortunately I think SWD fails in the two biggest areas of a game for me - mechanics and narrative. I think if you’re going to go bad on an aspect of a game, it can be one or the other of the above, NOT both. 
So what I’m left with here with SWD is a very beautiful game with a decent audio experience. Its becoming somewhat of a habit with Unreal Engine games to showcase a beautiful, but shallow, experience, and that seems true for this, at least for me. 

So overall, I cannot recommend this game. I think if you want to check out a much better walking sim, look into The Invincible ([url=https://steamcommunity.com/id/Synikx/recommended/731040?snr=1_5_9__402]review here[/url]).

[h1] [b] Recommend? (Price) [Y/N] [/b] [/h1]
[h1]No[/h1]
A ~4.5 hour game for $35? Big negative on that price-per-hour ratio. I see the lowest its gone was around $15, which I would only recommend picking it up around that price point. 
You can find my curator page here: https://steamcommunity.com/groups/Synik_GR

200200712.review:
[h3] 100% Achievement Review: A quick review based on my experience completing all the (available) achievements. [/h3]

[u] [url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/45897386/] Feel free to follow my curator for more 100% reviews! [/url] [/u]

(DLC REQUIRED FOR 100%)

Title: Still Wakes the Deep

Summary: Lovecraftian horror action game 

Personal notes: Still Wakes the Deep is a great game. You are Caz, a man running from certain responsibilities by working in one of the most remote places in the world, an offshore oil rig. This is until something is discovered while drilling that turns the whole rig upside down. The story itself had me intrigued the whole way through with some unexpected twists. I thought the art, music and overall story design was really well done. As such the characters felt really well made and down to earth which is always nice. The game play is fairly simple but honestly well done, personally it has been my favourite from The Chinese Room so far. The pacing is also good with its mix of story telling and nail biting moments running from death. The soundtrack was very fitting for this horror story and only heightened the experience. All of this combined made for a very tense and exciting play-through. My only real complaint would be that it is too short :P. For reference, I was able to complete the game and its DLC twice in under twenty hours. I also heard from others it was a little buggy for them but I honestly ran into nothing in both my play-through's. The achievements for this game are a little challenging, with a lot of them being out of the way or requiring specific things. Not to mention you have a handful of DLC achievements that are also out of the way. I listed the ones I had the most difficulty with below. All things considered, I really enjoyed my time with Still Wakes the Deep. I thought the story was great and the DLC addition was a great tie in as well. Say hi to Gibbo for me! 

Time it took to complete: 19hrs

Most difficult achievement(s):
- Body Count (Find every reachable dead crew member)
- Not so Good with the 'Leccy (Die in all electrical hazards on the Beira D)
- Sat Dive Certification/Mastery (Thoroughly explore the sea floor/trench valley)
- Bheir an cuan a chuid fhein a-mach (Completed the game in Scottish Gaelic)
- Moch 's mi 'g eirigh (Cuir crioch air an sgeulachd le dusgadh)-(DLC in Scottish Gaelic)

200122259.review:
What people saw in it, I really don't. I love horror games. This one sadly doesn't crack my top 5, maybe not even my top 10. It's sad too because I want to love it.

The +:
The setting is great, very unique and different. I'm tired of Manors and Asylums. This one stands out above the rest. 
The dialogues are well written and feel authentic for the most part. Characters are charismatic and the Scottish banter really adds to the experience and makes you care even about minor characters. Visually stunning if your PC can run it. Creatures are terrifying. Overall, the presentation is top notch. The back-and-forth in the levels is a nice touch and seeing the Rig changing as it progressively gets destroyed is very nice. Too few games do that.

The -: 
The controls, god the controls. Sprinting is a joke. I got the achievement of spending X amount of levels without running solely because it didn't feel useful at all outside of a couple of scripted sections. When I first sprinted, my reaction was: "Really? That's how fast you can go?". Seeing the animation of your character running made me laugh out loud. It really feels as if you'd try running at full speed while taking the smallest steps possible. Try it, you'll understand what I'm saying. Your character feel sluggish, slow. The worst part is the swimming, which makes up a significant portion of the game. I just could not get used to the controls during these parts. 
I encountered some bugs, but nothing major. One game-stopping bug though where my character was stuck in one camera angle, leaving me stuck in a room without any way of opening the door.
It's very, very short. I went through it in a little under 5 hours and I took my time. Things on the rig go hay-wire very fast. Within 5-6 in-game hours, the whole station is gone. I think it would've been cooler to spend more time on the Rig, see it getting destroyed over a longer period of time. Trying to save more people, multiplying creatures encounters, deepening the lore, etc. Would've given more impact to some character's deaths as you would've spent longer than 30 minutes total talking to them. 

The middle-way:
My PC is showing its age and I could not run the game on high or medium setting. I'm talking PowerPoint framerate... in the menu. Actually, PowerPoint framerate during the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ company logos introduction right after you boot the game. To be fair, with a more modern PC, my experience on this front might've been different. If you have a potato like me, I suggest you wait a bit so you can enjoy the complete experience. 
Story-wise, I found it okay. Not great, but not terrible. Didn't really care for the Shape as an antagonist. It feels very dollar-store Lovecraftian. I do get what they were going for though and again, I want to like it. I was involved in the smaller things in the story: bits of background on different characters, Caz' backstory, the character's relationship/banter, etc. But as a whole, it felt predictable.

The scares:
I felt I had to make it its own category. I'm tough to scare, honestly. Few games have done it. I know this and I try to stay indulgent when I play a new one since I know I'm a hard client. But honestly, I did not find the game scary as a whole. There were moments here and there where my heart rate went up. But as a whole, the creature encounters were easy to predict and thus reduced their impact. Creatures were easy to avoid and bait. Ambiance was great and even though I wasn't super scared, I can understand if someone might be in these sections.


It makes sense that this was made by the Chinese Room, because I have very similar vibes to Amnesia: A Machine for Pigs. It has similar strengths and weaknesses. My expectations where so high for a life-changing monologue at the end, but nope, not in this one. 

I can see why some people might enjoy this, why some people might be scared while playing it. But it didn't do it for me, as much as I wanted it to. As a horror game, I have a couple of others I'd recommend to people aiming to get into the genre.

199790516.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is in all honesty a mix bag for me. There are things I like about this game, and then it's followed by things I don't like. For every positive thing I can say about this game, there's a "but" or "however", and it leads me with a game where I half like it, but I half don't like it. Maybe there is something I am missing because I can see glimpses to why people would love this game, or maybe this game just really isn't for me. 

Graphically, Still Wakes the Deep looks fantastic. The main setting of the oil rig looks great in a lot of areas, the interiors are very detailed, the rain effect looks gorgeous, and it made you feel like you were on a giant oil rig. If there is one thing I can give Still Wakes the Deep some credit for at least, it's that it is visually great. I had 1 visual oddity happen during my time playing and a low res texture here and there, but that's about it. I'm also going to say that the music of Still Wakes the Deep is fantastic and fits the game perfectly!

Story wise, I didn't really care about it. You play as an Engineer in the 1970s named Caz who is on the run from the police after assaulting a guy, you are fired and told to leave, but a drilling incident causes a thing below the sea to take over the oil rig and infect people. I was really interested in the story just from the premise, but there isn't much of a story to be had here. The beginning of the game is where most of the story is and where you do get some world building, but after that there's not much of a story other than characters barking orders at you. It is a shame because the beginning is actually really strong, you get introduced to the rig and the characters, you're given a little background that gets you interested and some of their personalities, but then once that's done and you're thrust into the thing taking over, it's just it feels like the story goes on auto-pilot. You'll get small flashbacks, but nothing is really ever built on. Caz's backstory is never really built on other than some flashbacks, and that goes double for the other characters. Everyone else is gets like a few seconds of character, and then just dies. I didn't really feel anything for the death of any character, including the ones that you were clearly meant to supposed to be sad about. It just feels forced, like you meet this one guy at the beginning, throughout the rest of the game he's just constantly telling you "You need to go fix this", and then his death is treated as though it's this big sad thing. What probably baffled me the most was at the end, you are told "be brave for once in your life". I'm sorry, what? I spent 4 hours facing literal death every 5 seconds while everyone else does near nothing, and I "need to be brave for once in my life". What I can at least give is that the voice acting for this game is actually really good!

Gameplay wise, Still Wakes the Deep is not great. I heard this game be called a walking sim, and it feels like it half wants to be a walking sim, but the other half wants to play like a horror game, and the combination doesn't feel right. Much like the story, the gameplay seems to feel like auto-pilot. You don't really get a chance to really explore, there's nothing interesting to find except for at the beginning of the game, and you're not in any real danger most of the time due to constantly being pushed forward. Speaking of dangers, the monsters are a disappointment. The monsters do look horrifying and you will see some horrible imagery, but I wasn't scared at any point. The monsters while they look horrifying, don't really pose a threat, and if anything they're pretty much brain dead. Just find an item to throw and it distracts them for so long that you can practically get to where you need to before they turn around. The worst thing that can happen is that the game bugs out and the enemy gets stuck and can't do anything, but cry non-stop. The actual time where they do pose a threat are in chase scenes, but even then so long as you're moving forward, they can't get you. 

I want to love Still Wakes the Deep, I love how much the game leans into its Scottish characters and even having Scottish Gaelic as a language setting, I love the setting being on a giant oil rig, I love the inspirations that it took from, I love the music, it has potential to be scary, and it is visually great for the most part. However, if I had to sum up the game, I would have to quote my favorite Scottish chef, "It's raw. It's pink, and stone cold in the middle." This game simply needed more time in the oven to bake. If it had more story, character development, gave you more to explore or encouraged you to explore more, and just had more depth to it, I would absolutely recommend this game. I just can't, I don't think it's a great game, but I don't think it's a bad game. To me it's just painfully average. 

I do know that there is a good amount of love for this game, and I can completely see where people can and do love this game and I can see some people finding more enjoyment out of the game than I did. I really think that this just wasn't a game for me personally.

199771970.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is an enjoyable immersive adventure mystery. It's a gorgeous, richly designed world full of wonder, shivers, and the occasional chase scene. Aesthetically, it's a feast -- stunning world-building on the oil rig, outstanding character voice acting, really cinematic animations, haunting sound effects. The main adventure took me about 7 hrs to complete, and the DLC added on an extra 3 hrs, which I found myself really craving. Nonetheless, I'd say this is a sale game at best. It's lovingly made, no question, but it's also very, very linear, and too short to justify the full price.

I think of Still Wakes the Deep as drawing inspiration from games like SOMA, Outlast, and even as far back as the original Half-Life. We get regular barriers that can only be crossed by finding a switch or a particular key or an alternate root. There are a few sneak-past-the-monster moments, and the monster animations are really satisfying. And then there are a few chase sequences. In the DLC, this all changes slightly -- the DLC is more like the underwater portions of SOMA. More about immersion and less about anxiety.

As great as those inspirations are, SWtD does not retain the wider openness of certain spaces in SOMA, and it lacks its commanding depth in the story. Outlast's strength lay in both its survival horror mechanics and in the tension and terror of trying to tease out the geometry of a space in order to successfully sneak past a monster. In SWtD, monster mechanics are very straight-forward, and progression is often easy to discern. Add to all this the severe linearity of the whole game. There's a couple spots where exploration is permitted for bonus achievements, but otherwise nearly every decision and path is straight-jacketed on you, leading to some occasional mindless path-following. By the end, I kept finding myself wishing they'd unleash the mechanics, give us an open space or two to really explore and put all the different mechanics together (finding heaters, turning off sparking electricity, distracting monsters with noise, etc.). The moment never comes. Does that mean this isn't worth playing? Not in the least. I just think it had a lot more potential than the end result

The DLC is a must-have if you get the base game. It's really immersive to explore the oil rig wreckage, and the 'crashed geometry' of the wreckage is mind-bending at times. While I wasn't convinced it added much to the story, it's a really satisfying immersive add-on that eeks out enough playing time to really make everything seem worth it.

199570929.review:
My redemption arc with The Chinese Room comes full circle in 2025 with last year’s Still Wakes the Deep.

The story is actually short but very much set in stone: in 2019 I played Everybody’s gone to the Rapture: I disliked that game so much that I was sure I would never play another Chinese Room game ever again. It had pacing issues, narrative issues, a gameplay to die for (of boredom) and the list goes on. The few good things that it did have were overused and abused and it made you hate the game even more.

At least that’s what I thought at the time of playing it, your experience may vary!

Come 2024, when Still wakes the Deep was first announced, I was interested in its setting and story to begin with, and was ready to learn more about it, but I had my doubts. I had my reservations on the main character (your everyday Scottish electrician working on an oil rig during the 70s…?  Would that work all together? (and don’t become boring after the first 15 minutes?) Would Chinese room be able to improve on their previous game? (especially considering it was a long way to improvement)

Now, one year later, after just finishing the game, I have to say this has been one of the more solid experiences I had recently.

First, the setting and the story. This is as much a fantasy cosmic horror story as it is an historical drama set in an oil rig during the 1970s with chronological appropriate colors and furniture and iconography to go along (almost to the point of being depressing) but wait! all is put to good use for setting the scene and the atmosphere.

The game is still a walking simulator, like the previous game, but you will have more things to do than “just walk” and some new mechanics that you normally would find in other genres. These new mechanics will be used in service of telling the story or making the experience a certain way, so they are not just for adding gaming time. In fact, there are no collectibles or side missions in this game, gameplay is very focused, every thing serves a purpose, that in my opinion just reinforces the main experience and doesn’t make it longer, it makes it stronger. Still wakes the deep delves into some stealth mechanics when you are hiding from “the thing” or use objects to create some distraction (reminding in a way of Alien Isolation in that sense) but then being back to be your lineal path, walking sim.

One thing that the game nails absolutely right is the feeling of movement and the controls in general and how they respond to your input (at least with a gamepad). This is a hundred times better than their last game, it feels natural, never slow (but never rushed) you feel like you are watching a film or someone else move but it’s you, and it doesn’t get tedious or unnecessary long. This has to do also with how you move through space, and how levels are designed to help you navigate them. Everything is related to create the illusion of moving along naturally, and fast while never getting stuck or glitched through a wall.


I said before that I had my doubts about the main character and the setting. While this is a cosmic horror story set in an oil rig (and there’s a chance that if you don’t like cosmic horror or historical dramas set in oil rigs? You’ll probably won’t fancy this) but even if you like some of that, you always need a good main character to take you through all of it (especially this being a walking sim first, narrative horror second).

Luckily, one of the strong points of Still Wakes the deep is its full cast of characters and voice actors. Alec Newman playing Caz is a solid, endearing character, someone you care for and want to be alright in the end despite him not being a role model, and being flawed, you don’t mind spending a lot of time with him, and also the rest of the characters you meet, both the good, the bad and the monsters.

One notable thing to mention about the full cast of characters, is that no one overstays their welcome, for example there’s a back story character for Caz, his wife appears only in flashbacks. I feared initially that this would delve into a long string of unnecessary memory scenes that would cut me from the flow of the game every time they appear, but to my surprise this character appeared only a couple of times, in very short scenes which were actually much more powerful and poignant because of that, and more amazingly so for the fact that this character was beautifully portrayed by the actress.

Another way in which this game showed me how well it manages time. It makes really good use in how much time you spend in every feature, the story, the gameplay, the levels. The game itself is short, but it feels just the right length.

The story has to be said, is very simple, but it works fine for this kind of experience. The game is mostly about surviving and escaping what you find in that fateful day. Feeling the horror of the situation. And that’s why it’s even more impressive to have such a great cast of voice actors in this gameworld that it doesn’t only look good, but it also doesn’t forget to be emotional, to be dramatic but also to have quick and fluid gameplay.

I have to say, this time, Chinese Room nailed it, and all the pieces of this puzzle merged together and became a whole in Still wakes the Deep, the definitive walking sim game I never knew I could get from them but now I will always be looking forward to.

No pressure! 😊


This review ©2025 [b] Aleena. [/b]

199513610.review:
**SPOILERS** This game is truly special; from the beautiful voice acting, the amazing graphics, and the captivating story, Still Wakes the Deep always keeps you immersed and interested in the world.  I played the game on Xbox Game Pass when it came out originally, and I have just finished playing the game a second time, as well as the Siren's Rest DLC.  The game is just as good as I remember, and this DLC in my eyes deserves to be awarded the best DLC of 2025.  

Siren's Rest introduces you to a new character and allows you to explore the aftermath of the original game.  The ambiance and setting are jaw-dropping (unless you suffer from Thalassophobia, in which case, you may want to avoid this DLC).  Albeit quite a short story, Siren's Rest accomplishes a lot in that short period.  My favorite part of the DLC is exploring the wreckage of the Beira D.  As Mhairi, you discover what happened to the Beira D, all while collecting mementos of the crew and photographing the remains of the unfortunate souls that suffered horrible fates all those years ago.  The ability to choose between two distinct endings is a great touch to an already amazing DLC.  I played both endings and to me, both are equally great ways to conclude the thrilling story of Mhairi.  

Overall, I would highly recommend Still Wakes the Deep, as well as the Siren's Rest DLC.  This is a perfect game for those that enjoy story-rich walking simulators that keep you on the edge of your seat.  Both the base game and the DLC are must plays in my eyes.

199172846.review:
Stranded at sea in a claustrophobic maze of machinery and crawlspaces with twisted horrors descending upon you.
I very much enjoyed Still Wakes the Deep - it felt incredibly immersive, with an engaging story. The atmosphere is masterfully crafted, encounters are intense, and there were moments where I felt a deep and visceral dread anticipating what lay ahead. The graphics were beautiful and the monsters stunningly fluid.
I managed to complete the game in a single session, and though some later sections feel a little unnecessary it didn't overstay its welcome. It's a very linear game where the focus is on your environment, and I'd say best experienced if you go into it with that expectation.
I would recommend getting this game on sale - it's just not long enough to justify the full price. For horror fans it's definitely a must-play, however, and I'm looking forward to checking out the DLC.

I played this with my friend who gave it a solid 5/10. For good reasons such as no puzzles, poor stealth sections, minimal to nil character backgrounds and development. All valid. I gave it higher than this, about a 7/10. He was surprised but I explained this is because there are so few good Lovecraft games to choose from. And that may bias my rating when it really doesn't deserve it. It's possible. He doesn't know much about Lovecraftian horror and I absolutely love it. So here's the thing: if you know nothing about Lovecraftian horror, I would recommend you avoid this - there's not much here that will satisfy you. But for any fan of Lovecraft, this is a bite (or tentacle) sized piece of horror that aligns well with his short stories. I enjoyed the visuals, the voice acting, and the cosmic horror elements - but I feel there really could have been a little more cosmicism to flesh out the run time.

One more thing: even at the regular price point, for a Lovecraft fan, I would avoid it until it is at least 50% off.  Otherwise, I think this was fairly well done. Would recommend.

Great cosmic horror game! I enjoyed the story and characters, and the gameplay is good as well. At first it throws hints of a lot of different mechanics at the player, and I was worried I'd have to remember the crash course while under pressure of a threat or time limit. Luckily, the game will remind you how to do certain actions if you need it (or you can turn it off if you don't want it). Some other elements of the game that are introduced wind up being more for immersion and "realism" which I appreciated (I'm trying to keep this vague intentionally). I also liked that the scares and horror elements didn't rely on cheap jumpscares and loud noises, but stayed true to the classic themes of cosmic horror. Overall, I wouldn't personally say this game is THAT scary but it depends on what you find frightening. I'd warn potential players of a lot of gore and body horror, as well as frequent swearing (this doesn't bother me typically, but it's quite a lot).

I beat the game in one sitting and played the DLC the next day and really liked both! The main campaign has a "silver screen" feature that has you play in a cinematic black and white visual style that I opted to turn on and thought it was pretty cool! If you like short horror games or generally Lovecraftian mythos, pick this up!

walking simulator cosmic horror game heavily influenced by Lovecraft.  

strong start with the oil rig location, kind of original and interesting. Scottish English was really a nice surprise, couple jokes were on point. graphics are pretty good, were even too heavy for my poor video card.

as a whole game, it was a bit shallow, certainly didn't deliver. main weakness was the story for me. although sad and tragic it was too superficial for this specific genre. needed more environmental storytelling, greater mystery, not just about people and their loved ones. honestly i didn't really care about all the characters because this connection wasn't very well made.

i felt gameplay even for a walking simulator was too boring, tasks were repetitive and uninspired. there were couple of stealth and chase sequences which were pretty average. annoying QTE, bad swimming sections. but gameplay is definitely secondary to story, and story unfortunately was average. i struggled a bit to finish the game even though it is very short.

the best game in this genre (horror walking simulator) is Soma, pretty much uncontested, because the story was phenomenal. 

i mark this review as positive because it was a solid effort, at least graphic wise, and it's not a bad game per se, but it definitely falls short, not even considering other better games in this genre.

198798191.review:
Oh, Still Wakes the Deep is truly stunning—a masterclass in atmospheric horror and environmental storytelling. It is the kind of game that lashes you like a tempest, dragging you down to an oil-slicked abyss, a deep, dread-filled well, an intricately woven tapestry of despair—and you thank it for every agonizing moment. 

First off - the setting. An oil rig cracking apart amidst the roaring tempest of the North Sea? It stands as more than backdrop; it is a character by itself. The narrow corridors, the decaying metal, the endless creaking that is strained under the weight of unseen might—every pipe rattle sets your skin crawling, creates a claustrophobic immersion of the soul, stifling in every sense like diesel-soaked regret. It’s as if Ridley Scott’s Alien was doused in diesel and despair.

And don’t even get me started on the audio design; holy hell. Whoever engineered the sound design deserves a BAFTA. The way each shriek resonates has bone chilling effects while bringing forward lesser heard sounds. It's visceral in the most grotesque way. Screeches from afar, electrifying distant wails, the slithering of horrid beasts behind jagged walls, the mounting anxiety of each breath your character inhales; it is the most delicate blend of primal agony, utter torment.

The images are equally as striking. The detail of the environment — the way light cuts through fog and grime, the water’s weight, almost like it’s filth laden and alive — supports the narrative wonderfully. And when the terror does reveal itself? It is abstract and incomprehensible yet revoltingly captivating. Unlike the typical horror movie, the shocking elements doesn’t leap out from behind things.

10/10

198738521.review:
Still wakes the deep is something special especially for anyone who's looking for some narrative rich horror. The game-play that it offers is a tad light and you should know that going into it in case that's not your thing. Its in the vein of similar games like what remains of edith finch or everybodys gone to the rapture, with a light mix of outlast thrown in for good measure. 

The set up is great. You play as Cas, a man working on an oil rig off the coast of Scotland who's trying to avoid some trouble with the law while keeping his marriage and family afloat when wouldn't you know it the oil rig drills into a lovecraftian like monstrosity and everything begins to collapse. 

I think the setting is one of the strongest things that Still has going for it. I actually grabbed both the base game and DLC after watching David Jackson play the dlc on Playstation Access. Within Five minutes of seeing the underwater traversal of the opening segment I turned the video off and knew it was something I needed to experience for myself. Now the base game doesnt quite put you under the sea, though there are underwater segments but the rig itself is just a whole other world. There are numerous set pieces within the game that put you within full view of the sea with the chance of falling into a watery grave and its a sight to behold. Waves lap up against the rig in violent fashion and you really get the sense that you are stranded right out in the middle of all this water and it doesnt take long for you to imagine what could be underneath you. As the drill unintentionally drills down into something straight out of lovecraft the rig begins to fall apart, the people begin to change and the nightmare ensues. 

I went into this mostly blind and I think I was so much better so if thats how your trying to go into this as well heres your spoiler warning,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,







Throughout the game the creature, entity or whatever this thing is begins to envelop the rig and its a site to behold. Its like some king of alien kracken wrapping its tentacles around every part of it, crushing cabins, breaking through walls, spilling out of onto unsuspecting crew members. The aftermath of it all is pretty crazy, I took numerous screenshots. Your friends and some enemies that arent killed in the destruction transform under the influence and because of proximity to this thing. What was once human becomes a myriad of tentacles, limbs and most look like they came straight out of John Carpenter's The Thing. 
Its grotesque in the best way and if any of this sounds like your thing then I think you'll love what spectacles Still has to offer as did I, even if the gameplay side of things cant quite live up to the visuals. 

The gameplay is definitely where the negatives start to show and I don't think that's unusual for this time of experience. Similar to fire watch, or what remains of edith finch before it Still is more of a story that your walking through and experiencing as opposed to typically playing through. (Not to keep harping on this trope but coming into this wrong is about the only real way I could see walking away from this package with a negative experience.) Your time spent on the rig is mostly just getting to each new area and crew member that needs your help only to then send you to another area that needs you and so on and so on. Along the way youll see a circus of body horror as crew members are melded with the rig and with the ever growing beast. You'll have to hide from time to time and get around some of the monstrosities by throwing various things you find laying around, diverting them and whatnot. Its just as tiresome as it sounds and by the second encounter like this I found myself just wishing I could skip through those sections. Not to mention that there's alot of trasversal that can be clunky to say the least. I found myself repeating numerous jumping sections or holding sections that just felt poorly designed. There was also once or twice with the swimming sections where cas got stuck on nothing and drowned and that took me out of it alittle. 

All in all though I finished Still wakes the deep in right around 6 hours and all the negatives seem small in comparison to the story as a whole. I enjoyed my time in this nightmare and I felt like I did all I could to get us back to Suze and them girls. 
Excited to jump into this dlc forsure.

198735330.review:
This game and it's DLC will be winning no Game-Of-The-Year awards, but I will say: I think this is one of the best games I have ever played. The voice acting and visuals are amazing all-around, with an extensive soundtrack which really captivated the game, the animations feel like they have weight and feel like how a person would really move. The characters feel legit and real and have a weird sense of 4th wall self-awareness which honestly worked so well for your main playable character, Caz.

The DLC Siren's Rest was an amazing addition with a much slower paced feel, but really captured and integrified the original story and did all the characters we heard die off-screen or via phone calls justice. The female protagonist had a reason for being their as one of the Beira D members was her father. All around, the story was very well done. The only issue I had with the DLC as opposed to the main game is that both endings felt very abrupt which didn't really work

Are there issues with the game? Minor ones that all got ironed out extremely fast
Is the game great? Yes
Should you play the game? Absolutely! The game itself is short, with 100% completion only taking around 8 hours, but it really is a heartfelt experience and has a deep lore. 10/10 in my personal opinion

This is what you would call an horror game. From eery beginning to eventually uncovering the truth of the whole situation. The only noteworthy issue being with the swimming controls over the keyboard and mouse but otherwise solid experience. 

Going through memories while writing the review was enough to give me chill and anxiety. Everything works so perfectly for their favor: theme, music & SFX, atmosphere, visual presentation, NPC’s… everything. 

Seems like it’s time to spend some time under water with the DLC content.

This is a good one. More atmospheric that abject creepy, but with enough body horror and tense situations to keep you on your toes. The very adaptable difficulty/accessability settings let you get to a point where you can strike a perfect balance between stress, but getting the feeling you got outta there juuuuuuuust in time. 

Horror-style-wise expect something between The Thing and Lovecraft....if all the characters were E X C E S S I V E L Y scottish. 

[spoiler]Also liked how the Lovecraftian elements didn't manifest in "oh look, Cthulhu" or finding the diary of the one weird crewmember that knew it all along but nobody believed him". Instead it actually is an incomprehensible thing. What is it exactly? Don't know. Where does it come from? Fugged if I know! What does it want? STOP ASKING QUESTIONS IT'S KILLING US ALL!!!![/spoiler]

The DLC adds a solid 2.5-ish hours to the around 5 it takes for the main game and adds a really nice twist onto the main story, while still being its own thing.

Greatly recommended if you're looking for "one of those" games.

There were many aspects the dev team nailed and were fantastic:
- The graphics were stunning at times, the kind that makes you stop to take a screenshot of a cool environment in the middle of the action.
- The characters, the relationships and banter between the crew of the Beira D felt real, and was nailed home by the animation, bringing each character to life.
- Environmental storytelling, another thing this game excels at, complemented by the fantastic lighting and sound design.
- The story, it started out with small hints and clues and eventually revealed itself (it being the details leading up to this event, mainly relation to the protagonist Caz's personal life), and I thought it was all tied together in the ending very nicely.

The gameplay itself - was pretty good. There were some parts that were like a walking simulator, but they were helped out by the previously mentioned points and some small things you'd have to do on your way like breaking a lock, deploying a ladder, or shimmying along a ledge holding on for dear life as to not fall in the north sea. The horror sections so to speak, where there was an... active threat... were some of the best I've played. They did a great job of making me think constantly "Am I going to die?" because the first few times around, you don't exactly know how to avoid death, you just know roughly. It freaked me out a lot and that aspect and absolutely lives up to its steam tag of horror and psychological horror.

Overall great story game (not much replayability to my knowledge outside steam achievements), definitely worth what I bought it at.

WOW. I haven't even finished it yet and I'm just blown away. I knew I had to leave a review, which I almost never do.

I've played a lot of horror games of this style (like Amnesia, Soma, etc.) and something about Still Wakes The Deep has left me wildly impressed. There are so many points I've just stopped to be totally immersed in the environment the game creates, and there is something absolutely unique about all of it; it feels like some kind of synthesis of an X-Files episode, the films Annihilation & The Mist, and the game Soma. During one intensely foggy outdoor sequence, I was so stunned by what I was seeing and hearing that I was almost too afraid to move - and this is a common experience! The things you encounter on the rig are downright TERRIFYING thanks to some absolutely masterful sound design/engineering and visuals - it's just pure art. During some of the chase sequences I've actually found myself running scared because what chases you is so genuinely revolting and terrifying, rather than because I don't want a game-over screen (which is typical of games like this - the thrills get old and stop being scary - not so in this game!). 

I know this kind of walking sim / playable movie-type game isn't everyone's cup of tea, but man - this is special. Definitely don't miss this one for an evening with the lights off. Fantastic job by the developers, voice actors, artists, sound design - just wow.

198281042.review:
/!\ MIXED /!\

Still Wakes the Deep is a blunt yet passionned attempt at delivering an atmospheric, immersive and narrative SCI-FI experience.
The game sets you as Cameron McLeary (husband and father of two), an electrician working on an Oil rig in the North sea during the year 1975. Something unexpected broke the otherwise uneventful routine, and will have you and your coworkers struggle and solve unspeakable situations in your quest for survival.

This game left found memories in me, which is in itself pretty amazing for a product I didn't know much about several months ago. If I had to compare it I'd say it's somewhere between Alien Isolation and Outlast, with a pinch of poetry here and there : you can't fight back, you only run, hide, listen or think. The rythm is particularly balanced between quiet and blood rushing sequences. 
There are also moving moments, and jokes that manage to land at the worst possible time, akwardly enough.
SWTD has heart and good crafting material.

As a proof, the characters are really giving it. The writing is smart, and the lines delivery is top notch. If you can make sense out of that scottish accent that is ! And altogether it gives soul to the story unfolding and everyone feels in place because you can tell the voice actors are pros having fun.
What I liked a lot too, was the sound design. Occasionnally invading and annoying, it gets interesting when the scary bits hit. Playing with headphones is highly recommended.
Lastly, the game is technically impressive. Granted it doesn't show much more than a closed environnement with mostly steel structures, it achieves to give a sight from time to time, and has nice lighting, post-process and textures. It all serves the atmosphere perfectly.

So much so that it sadly is an unoptimized mess. I never spent this long in the video settings in a game, to make it somehow playable. The framerate is hard to tame, no matter the sampling used (and there are lots) or advanced options combined.
I had to sit between a 30-50 fps experience, sometimes even worse.

And here are the other parts that could have been better for the sake of this ambitious project :
- Underwater sequences suck big time, for lack of direction, visibility and the aweful sluggishness
- Input recognition is a huge problem, I had to aim several times the same doors or edges to be able to display the UI trigger before pressing the expected key. And if you missed the spot, you had to step back and forth again to retry
- The game is short, and it shows in the gameplay and environnement. It gets redundant halfway through

By all account the game is nice. Not a great story, but a good tale made with talent.
Maybe a wee bit more polish would have make it a perfect journey.

198227486.review:
Pros: Gorgeous graphics, amazing voice acting, awesomely cool atmosphere of being on and inside an oil rig, terrifying ambience and chase sequences. 
Cons: Very linear gameplay. You can't explore the rig much at all, doors are locked and pathways blocked to steer you around. Zero inventory, all you ever get is a flashlight. No weapons, food, nothing. The controls during sudden falls/drops are very wonky and easy to miss, forcing you to do the whole scene over again. The story is hugely depressing, with no sense of closure, accomplishment, redemption, or a good ending. If you have any sort of anxiety or trauma in your past, you might want to read the content closely before playing. 
Conclusion: The game is "good" in terms of quality of content (graphics, sound, music, voice acting), but it is incredibly depressing and traumatizing to experience, and I didn't care for the strictly linear angle, as I love to explore and wander around.

198031670.review: I am unsure if the developers read these but this game was one of my favourite games this year so far. I was not hyped for it as some of the reviews seemed negative. But i trusted my gut instinct and i am so glad i did. The 1975 setting is so amazing. the care and detail and research in creating this 70's world really did pay off. Within moments i felt i was back in time. The story i loved. really felt honest and real. The voice acting was spot on. Luckily i am pretty good with the Scottish accent so i didn't need subtitles haha but the Scottish dialect is strong so some pay need them. I felt so immersed. It is a little linear and one of the main criticism i have seen has been that it should have been more open and less controlled and less linear. But i disagree. Played on Linux using Proton Exp, I have an Intel I7 6700 and NVidia RTX 4060, Kind of took some time to figure out the best settings to run this Game, DLSS for me caused fuzzy graphics mostly with water texture/shaders, AMD FSR crashed randomly when loading, so had to force stop and play again, lucky the save system seem to work so that help and no starting way back in the Game, discovered that this is a Bug with FSR with this Game, so then tried Intel's XeSS this worked fine but was slower (Lower FPS) then DLSS or FSR so had to drop some detail options to medium, Unreal 5's TSR or TAA cause a massive 50 to 60 FPS less then the other 3 Options so i just settled on XeSS.

NVidia Frame Generation switched on caused strange jumping frames back and forth after a while of play so was unplayable, so had to turn that off as well, I'm not sure if this is a bug with Frame Generation, The Game or Linux/Proton, AMD Frame Generation does nothing on NVidia. 

I bought both the Game and DLC and both only took me 6.2 Hours to complete both, so for the Price a bit on the short side.

Graphics are okay mostly, The DLC did not look as good but still okay, Sound, Music, Voices are Great, Game play is pretty much a Walking Sim with good Story, No Puzzles, No Combat, minimal items to pick up's and view, and some Stealth Sections which you can pick up objects to throw to make noise so the monster moves towards the sound, and run away from the monster sections.
The DLC does have some different type of play, which is use a Camera to take pictures of dead body's you find and also an underwater cutter to cut some rust patches on doors, grates, etc to open them, and it's more underwater then on the surface (as seen in it's trailer).

So it's an okay Game as long as you like Story Games, Easy Stealth, and run away from monsters, with shortish Game Play.
I do recommend the Game but better to wait for a sale for both the Game and DLC.

197988016.review:
Fantastic game! I thought that playing a linear walking simulator can't be exciting anymore after having played so many of these games, but Still wakes the Deep is a very special game. 

It might be linear but the sound effects, atmosphere and story were playing out so well that you truly felt like being the main character in a very good, nerve wrecking horror movie. The monsters were terrifying. While you couldn't fight them, you pretty much only had a chance when you were distracting them while sneaking around them Outlast style. You were feeling helpless the entire time and were constantly on the edge.

The main character was amazing. I couldn't help but fell in love with him. He was such a good guy and you literally felt all the pain that he was going through. Every close jump, every fall, the cold of the freezing water, the hallucinations, the memories of his family, the arguments with his wife, his struggle to survive and the love for all the other people... all of this made him such a great and relatable hero. You could easily feel the nightmare, the tour the force that he was going through.

The game also had it's funny moments. The voice acting and Scottish accent, the rough seafaring people... everything was so highly enjoyable.

I'll definitely play this another time for all the achievements that I'm still missing. And after that, I'll play the dlc, hopefully learning a bit more about whatever the people on the oil rig had set free from the bottom of the ocean.

Great game, great experience that truly made me feel a lot, and this is exactly why we play games. Because we want to feel.
