197882583.review: This game is short, yes, but it's also wonderfully atmospheric. If you're looking for big, open world adventure, this is not the game for you, but if you enjoy a slow-burn story with fantastic characters (with equally fantastic voice acting) then Still Wakes the Deep is very much worth your time. At full price, it may be a smidge expensive, but if you can get this in the sale then I'd absolutely recommend it. The DLC is also very well done, and continues the story very cleverly. Also, as a Scot I can vouch for both the dialogue/accents/speech patterns and the weather. It really is that bleak in the north sea. Haven't come across any horrible beasties myself, but I daresay it's possible. Certainly feels that way when you look out across the Atlantic on a rainy day...

197880451.review:
Wow... Just wow... Still Wakes the Deep is one of the most immersive, haunting, and beautifully crafted games I've ever played. From start to finish, it grips you with an atmosphere so thick with tension and dread, you genuinely feel like you're right there - cold, scared, and desperate to survive.

The story is powerful and personal. It's not just a horror game with monsters (though those are chilling and brilliantly designed too). It's a human story. You connect deeply with the protagonist and the other crew members. You care about them. They aren’t just background NPCs, they feel like real people caught in an impossible nightmare. Every interaction, every desperate shout or quiet moment of reflection, hits hard.

The setting is just phenomenal. The North Sea oil rig is rendered with incredible attention to detail, and the visuals are gorgeous in the most eerie, atmospheric way. You feel the isolation, the cold, the danger in every hallway, every flickering light, every groaning steel beam. The sound design and the soundtrack elevate everything to a whole new level - subtle when it needs to be, and intense when it has to be.

Gameplay-wise, the pacing is spot on. It’s not about jump scares or constant action, it’s about building tension, making you feel like you’re always just a second away from disaster. And somehow, even the moments where you’re just crawling through narrow spaces or running for your life feel incredibly cinematic and emotionally charged.

You can tell that every decision the developers made was intentional. Nothing feels random or filler, everything serves the story, the atmosphere, the experience. Even the creature design is astonishing. Disturbing, yes, but in the most fascinating way. It's horror with meaning, with artistry.

I can't recommend this game enough. If you're into narrative-driven horror experiences that stay with you long after the credits roll, this is an absolute must-play. Bravo to the devs. This one is truly something special!

197748911.review:
I really hate to give this a negative review. I wish Steam had a 'neutral' option.

I see a lot of reviews say it's like an interactive movie, but it's not. The pacing and structure is that of a game, and one you've likely played before. You get fifteen minutes of introduction and then your character blacks out and when you wake up, it's straight into 'almost everyone is dead and there are monsters you have to avoid'. If you took Soma, then stripped out all the interesting themes about the nature of consciousness and identity, what you'd be left with is this game: A beautiful, well-voice-acted, but ultimately hollow succession of monster encounters and scenery-chewing emotional moments. It sets up some strong themes about your character's identity, those of some of the secondary characters, and the nature of the antagonist, but then does nothing with them- running through the same story beats repeatedly until an abrupt end.

It's not /bad/. It's just four to six hours of extremely basic interaction prompts and occasional monster-hiding-events, and then you're done. I didn't expect much in the way of gameplay, but this falls short of the interactive movie it's billed as. The story is okay, the gameplay is threadbare, it really leans on the voice acting and that isn't enough to sustain it.

The DLC is more of the same. I really enjoyed the change of environment, but I also got through it in about three hours, and it felt as much tedious as fun. There was no character development, no payoff to the mechanic of collecting evidence, just A-to-B with tremendous atmosphere but no substance.

tl;dr Concept is great, execution is shallow, game is short. Oh, and if you do decide to play it, be sure to set the subtitles to English (United Kingdom). Having Scottish colloquialisms translated is weird.

197519158.review:
I'm a Scotsman, and even so, it's hard to recommend this "game," most importantly, because it's not a game. It's a movie with some minor interaction along the way. There's so much to like here, but simply put: there's no gameplay. There are no mechanics to think about. There are no items to collect, there are no secrets, there's no management of health or, really, anything! And how can it be, that we're playing the role of an electrician, but we have no electrical puzzles to solve? We're on an oil rig, with everything falling apart around us, and we're useless. The "movie" of the game does everything for us, makes every decision, and we're just along for the ride, a camera watching it all go down. We have no decisions to make. None. Nothing we do matters. There is one path and one path only. And on that path, there's an NPC (Finlay) telling us everything that we need to do, because *she* must be the real electrician and we aren't, or something (she's a plumber when she's first introduced). That's right, our character, the electrician, doesn't know how to find relays, or how to power on generators, or how to troubleshoot electrical faults, but wait, wasn't he getting paid a fortune to do just that? It turns out that all of these different "puzzles" are simply walking here and clicking this button, and then walking there and clicking that button. You don't have to think about it, the game tells you exactly where to go. The game is a walking simulator with zero challenges. You have a long journey to go on and lots of ladders to climb and oil to swim through and buttons that the game tells you to press, and that's about it. 

One recent "survival" horror game with a similar price point and production quality and strong narrative might be A Quiet Place: The Road Ahead. While cheaper at $30 USD, it actually has many gameplay mechanics to remember, decisions that matter, puzzles to solve, items and health to manage, and secrets to discover. Funnily enough, there's more electrical work to do in that game, and the main character isn't an electrician!

Another similar horror game, with respect to having an "alien" lifeform, on an "industrial rig," with "technical tasks" to accomplish, would be Alien: Isolation. Yet, beyond that description, this game isn't in the same league and can't even be compared. Alien: Isolation has a crafting system to create traps and weapons, for which you need to gather materials and tools and make crucial decisions. The space station in that game is also a bit "open world" in some sense, allowing you to explore different areas when you're ready, and you can sometimes accomplish tasks in different orders, with NPCs/monsters appearing randomly, so no two play-throughs are the same. There's endless lore and secret collectibles to find and tools and craftables to create and intense quick-time events and jump scares... You won't find anything like that in Still Wakes the Deep.

Yes, there are some tense moments in this game, and gorgeous graphics, and great voice acting, but there's just no gameplay. Because I'm a Scot and an engineer, I actually really enjoyed spending a few hours looking through the intro environment (cabins, rec room, cafeteria) and immersing myself in the 1975 North Sea oil rig lore before getting into the game. I saw this game briefly on someone's stream and I was so hyped I shut it off to prevent myself from getting any spoilers... but, even then, beyond the intro environment, this game was all downhill. I think this was a truly wasted opportunity. There could have been decisions left up to us, puzzles to solve, items to collect, secrets to find, enemies to kill or outsmart, but there's nothing but a single path to walk and no challenges, a true shame.

Also, there's endless profanity, and I mean endless. Your character complains and drops an F-bomb and curses God every time he jumps or touches a ladder or whatever else. He was made out to be such a weakling, complaining about literally everything. If you can't handle dropping F-bombs and saying the Lord's name in vain every 30 seconds, this definitely won't be the game for you. 

The last sore-spot: the main character's storyline is that his wife is threatening to leave him, so for about 5 hours, imagine your wife is endlessly threatening and nagging you about a decision you made, long ago, to perhaps actually defend her honor, which is pure irony. As a man, this was honestly really insufferable, and made the game's finale more enjoyable than it otherwise would have been, for multiple reasons.

Price: ~$35 USD
Gametime: ~5 hours
Value: ~$7 per hour of film-like immersion without any gameplay

197445714.review: There isn't much to do here; no fighting, no inventory , and no puzzles to solve. There are no dialogue options or plot twists to unravel. Instead, you'll be simply walking through the entire game. So, why does it earn a 10/10 rating from me? It's hard to put into words. There’s something mesmerizing about this place. At start, as I wandered across the platform, listening to the cries of seagulls and the rhythmic crashing of waves, accompanied by industrial noises in the background, I could almost smell the sea with the scent of oil in the air. The creators crafted a realistic representation of the oil rig's facilities — a secret world that most of us will never experience in real life. By the time the horror struck, I was deeply engrossed in the place, understanding Scottish accent as if I had spent years working there. In that heightened state of mind, horror hits with a profound intensity. The abnormal, blatant, yet haunting kind of horror, the monstrous and supernatural dread that seeps right into your bones and mind. This experience, I dare to say, would have been weakened by typical gameplay elements like combat, puzzles, or dialogue options. Still Wakes the Deep stands as a prime example of why the walking simulator genre holds a unique value in gaming industry.

I gave a thumbs down overall. I'll go over what I liked and disliked but I don't recommend buying this game. Wait until it's free somewhere.

Likes:
Visuals - graphically beautiful game and the environments are neat 

Voice acting - the Scottish accent and "slang" they use (that you'll notice in subtitles) is fantastic. Each character has a small layer solely because of that

The IDEA of the game - horror on an oil rig. Pretty straight forward there

Now the reason I gave it a thumbs down:
Gameplay - overall pretty lackluster. It is insanely linear. It is pretty much just a bunch of prompts to grab, crouch, swim, climb. Not much thinking is needed. I beat it in just under 4.5 hours. 

Story - Again, another pretty lackluster story. Dudes wife is mad he went to work, and you meet some other characters that have little to very little development or any sort of emotional need to be in the game. Like, talking to Roy, and grabbing his insulin just for him to die seemed pointless. I get that thats kind of the theme here but like...com'on...

Mechanics - Really clunky and pointless. At one point it says to enter lockers to hide from enemies. The problem is, there is only ever one enemy at a time, and even then that was the only locker I ever encountered. Plus, I got an achievement for "defeating" a certain "boss" when literally all I did was walk through a hallway. 

Last thoughts - Like I said, I wouldn't pay for this game. I'm pretty disappointed over the hype it got and even more disappointed I paid for it. Luckily I got it on sale, but if you can wait until it's free somewhere do that.

197333971.review:
A very atmospheric lovecraftian-esq horror game that centers around one of my biggest fears: the ocean and the absolute horrific monstrosities that dwell in it. The fear of the unknown, more so.

I went into this game knowing nothing about it besides the fact that the character you play is scottish and you are on a oil rig in the middle of the ocean, I don't know what I was expecting, but once I saw the grotesque monster designs and played through the sequences where you have to hide and make your way through said grotesque monsters - I realized that my expections couldn't have ever actually topped the segments themselves. I rarely ever get scared in a video game, maybe only a handful of times over many many horrors games that I've played, but there was some genuinely really unsettling parts in this game that made me jump (I accidentally made a sound near one of the monsters and I looked up to the ceiling and all I saw was the things face coming right at me in the span of like 1 second and then I died, it scared the hell out of me).

I sort of wish there were more horror segments though with the monsters, I feel like for as great as the segments were, they were few and sparse while most of the game revolved around getting from point A to point B after being ordered around from some of your fellow crew members. Which, I don't really mind, with how detailed the rig itself was and how atmospheric the entire game is, that alone kept me entertained even while just walking around trying to fix things (seriously, the rig is beautiful, I've never really seen one in detail but from how it is modeled in this game, I imagine it would look near identical to the game). 

Another thing I absolutely love, is the characters. All of them, they all feel real, they act like real people, they are scared and confused and yet they make jokes and banter even while in the midst of a nightmare just to ease some tension, it's exactly how I'd imagine almost anyone to act if they were going through this. The voice acting is one of the best parts of the game, as well as the characters themselves. I truly love characters like Caz in horror games, or even any horror media. For some reason horror media has a tendency to make the main character try and be a badass even while fighting terrifying monsters and going through literal hell (looking at you Resident Evil, even though that's one of my favorite horror franchises...) and it takes away from the horror aspect a lot since you feel like you have nothing to be scared about since not even your character is scared, but this, this game, Caz is horrified. You can hear it in his breathing, in the way his voice shakes and even his hands shake, his comments upon seeing his friends corrupted and mutilated by some unknown sea... thing, he feels like a truly shaken up broken man who just wants to get off the rig and see his family again. I wish more games did that, and it's strange how little they do because it really adds to the fear factor, as simple as it is.

The game is beautiful, in an odd way, even the main monster itself. The entire game is like a siren ironically enough, on the surface, everything is normal, enticing even, and then the more you dig, the deeper you get, you realize somethings wrong and things aren't how they should be. For one, the rig itself, again very beautifully detailed, floating out on the waves of the ocean, the people there all working together and joking around, and then you learn about Rennick and the horrible work conditions he is putting all of these people through because "He is a king" [spoiler]Literally Roy dies not from the same fate as everyone else, that being the monsters, but from diabetes because his insulin was down in his cabin. There was a comment on a video I saw that said this "this entire game has the background of them being overworked and kept to an extremely tight schedule for maximum profit at the risk of their own health. I can imagine Roy only being allowed to take his insulin during breaks, not being allowed to keep it with him, and probably having so little of it due to poor medical coverage he keeps himself running on the bare minimum. His death is all the more tragic because it wasn’t caused by the thing from the deep, but another monster entirely". And I feel that perfectly encapsulates what is going on, on this rig.[/spoiler] There then is also the monster(s) itself, the main sea thing looks oddly beautiful, the colors of it, the way the light moves in waves on its skin(?) Everytime you get a glimpse of it from the outside, you can't help but just stop and stare at it, trying to figure out what it is you're even looking at, even Caz is just like "My God..." every time he sees it. Two scenes that spring to mind is the one where you're going up the elevator and you look straight up and the most heavenly music begins to play with your ascent as you're staring up into this giant mass of colors and shapes and lights. And then the end scene where you are right in the heart of it, after everything you've been through, everything that this thing has done to both Caz and everyone else on the rig, and you still just stare in a oddly beautiful moment. And yet, even though it's pretty on the surface, it is deadly, and it changes those around you, and you see the disgusting products of it and what it can do to people. It calls you in to get closer, entices you with voices of your loved one, and before you know it, your body has become a disgusting amalgamation of limbs and guts and horror, and you're still fairly aware of all of it but you can no longer control yourself. That's something even worse than death, and you can't help but feel bad for the members that suffered from this fate, and you can tell Caz truly feels sorrow for them, tries talking to them, but it's far too late now, and now you can only hope you don't end up the same.

Love this game, I can't wait for the DLC to release, we need more horror games based around the ocean because even in real life it truly is a scary concept, just the fact alone that we know more about space than we do our own ocean is horrifying, and I don't think enough people truly realize that.

Another thing I'd like to bring up, it's great how accessible this game is, there are many settings you can tweak to make the game more accessible for you, I'm very thankful for the FoV setting which for some reason a lot of other games don't even bother to add because they think it'll ruin the immersion or something (but really it just gives me a headache...) but while I was scrolling through the settings, there's a lot of things you can change I noticed, so thank you game devs for doing what should be the bare minimum, but in reality not many other games add.

Solid game, pick it up whenever you can.

197241235.review:
I have long been a fan of both TheChineseRoom as well as walking simulators so not recommending Still Wakes the Deep doesn't feel good especially when I followed so much of the developer diaries for the game and heavily anticipated it's release. I watched the game get played through multiple times through youtube and now that I finally can play it myself, I genuinely feel like I've already played the game just by watching it. So to not muddy this review, let me break it into sections:

Story:
There's absolutely no debate that the story itself and it's lovecraftian horror elements work really well here, you become invested in characters stories. The voice acting is extremely well done and the genuine accents and communication between characters really makes you care for the cast. My only gripe with the story is the interaction between characters, or more specifically, Caz's reaction to character deaths. The voice actor for Caz does a lot of heavy lifting with his emotional range of convincing the player that Caz is genuinely upset at some of the corpses he recognizes but sometimes I feel like there could've been a little more done to make the player care as well to fully establish that emotional moment.

Gameplay:
Probably the biggest blight of the game. There's very little gameplay here to even review but since it is a walking simulator, that should be expected. There's a chase here and there and small moments of throwing an item to distract an enemy but it's all pretty shallow and mostly there to tell the story in small segments. Exploration is non-existent outside of a few small moments which are all heavily tailored to funnel the player forwards meaning the gameplay just turns into traversing a long hallway to the next door.

Graphics:
I'm entirely mixed on this whole aspect of the game and will likely be an outlier with this opinion, I am not blown away by the graphics or environment. I think TheChineseRoom should've stuck with Cryengine like with Everyone's Gone to the Rapture simply because I think UE5 is riddled with problems and those problems are very much present here. First off, yes, the environment itself looks nice but a common problem that I personally find wrong with UE5 games are that if I can recognize a set of assets at first glance, it immediately ruins my immersion. Second, character models are not the best, the primary set of characters are better but all the background characters are not good, the initial walk through the rig at the beginning is a prime example of this. Lastly, I really wish I didn't follow the devblogs because while they genuinely studied oil rigs, it's like 90% of their research went nowhere as they chose a rig from the 70's which were incredibly basic compared to the ones used even semi-modern rigs. I understand that an oil rig isn't the most varied in terms of environmental variety but like if you're gonna use a basic rig, make it somewhat explorable instead of being a system of hallways.

Conclusion:
At it's asking price, it's not worth it, even for it's incredibly good story and voice acting. Even on sale, this is a tough buy because you're getting 4 hours of gameplay and virtually no gameplay in it. Honestly, you could get the same experience watching a youtube playthrough but that's up to you.

197188095.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is a beautiful, touching, and heartbreaking narrative horror adventure. The story takes place on an oil rig when disaster strikes. Everything about this game seems typical and bland on the surface. Make no mistake about it; it's a walking simulator. Various events are triggered from entering a room or going around a corner. But what the game truly is at it's core, is a wonderful narrative about family, love, and loss.

The game is not very long. Totaling a playthrough for me at a little over 5 hours. But in those five hours, I laughed, yelled, jumped, died (in game), and nearly fell in love with the heart wrenching story about a man and his troubled relationship, and his love for family and friends. I could go on and on about the story, but I do not want to spoil anything, as I feel the only way to experience the game at it's fullest potential, is as blind as possible.

The atmosphere of the game is pure isolation. The graphics do a phenomenal job at detailing how alone everyone is on the oil rig. When the disaster occurs, each environment gets more and more distressing...More disturbing than the last. The controls are great as well. Not clunky or overly complex. It's not exactly a pick up and play game because of the "tedious" walking simulator moments, but this game is one of those rarity's that should be played start to finish in one sitting.

The voice acting alone is brilliant. Emotion, comedy, heart, all of the various story beats are met with great voice acting by a very talented crew. The characters you ultimately meet, you will either love or hate them, and you WILL know it.

I can't say what I was expecting of this game originally. All I knew is that it was a horror adventure game, and I love walking simulators personally. Walking sims, when done correctly, are story first, gameplay second. Still Wakes the Deep is an interactive adventure that pulls no punches and it's been some time since I've found a narrative game that I've enjoyed so much.

Bravo to everyone involved with the creation of Still Wakes the Deep.

[b]Overall[/b]: 9/10
[b]Sound[/b]: 9/10
[b]Gameplay[/b]: 8/10
[b]Horror[/b]: 7/10
[b]Atmosphere[/b]: 10/10
[b]Story[/b]: 10/10

I can't wait to try the new DLC.

195944808.review:
Still Wakes the Deep is an excellent linear body horror story, with tense mechanics, and believable, grounded characters, and a main character arc that resolves well. It's a bit short, clocking in at about 4.5 hours, but it's a lean and well-developed 4.5 hours. 

There are a few missed opportunities that could have made it a truly legendary game, but it's definitely still worth your time if you are a fan of classic body horror stories like "The Thing," and Lovecraftian horror like "In the Mouth of Madness." 

If you enjoyed Amnesia, you'll probably like this too.

[h1]The Good[/h1]
-The story is excellent, with well-developed believable characters elevated by realistic writing and superb voice acting (honestly among the best I've heard). 
-The monster designs are freaky, evoking the best of body horror classics. ESPECIALLY the static "environmental" monsters you find, major existential horror.
-The music is absolutely top-notch. Atmospheric and tense when it needs to be, somber when it calls for it, frantic when there's action. This is a soundtrack I'd use for D&D sessions or listen to outside of the game.
-The gameplay is tense, but intuitive. The controls kind of pantomime the actions you're doing, which is somewhat immersive, and they don't needlessly change up quick-time events so once you learn the controls you respond to the event itself, rather than an on-screen button prompt.
-A lot of people are saying they disliked the ending, but it completes the arc of the main character quite well. [spoiler]Don't expect a happy ending though.[/spoiler]
-It doesn't overstay its welcome. I think it could have been a smidgen longer, but it resolves itself well, and doesn't try to pad things out.

[h1]The Bad[/h1]
-The interactions are intuitive, but lack variety. Quite a few "puzzles" are reused.
-While it's definitely not a "walking simulator" like many people are saying, it lacks openness and exploration.
-The monster appearances are all scripted "stealth arenas" or "chases." They aren't awful, but could have been better.

[h1]Missed Opportunities[/h1]
-I would have preferred a more open structure where you could explore a bit, with some minor resource management mechanics (batteries, health, distraction items, body heat, etc.). 
-I think having a hub-and-spoke style system, with 3 or 4 "chapters" using the same core map layout, but in different states of damage/decay/infection, would have benefited the game and enhanced the horror. The game already somewhat does this artistically, I just would have liked it to do that in a more mechanically significant way too.
-To this end, an open structure with the creatures "patrolling" the entire hub would have enhanced the fear factor in my opinion, as opposed to the scripted stealth arenas and chase sequences it currently has.
-This could have even enhanced the character relationships. If Roy, for example, was always in the "Safe Room" and you had to revisit him to save your game, the relationship would have been a lot stronger. Maybe they could have even had a Resident Evil "Ink Ribbon" style system [spoiler]where you bring him his insulin.[/spoiler]
-They could have even worked in some of the static "environmental" horror pieces as spotters, where if they see you or you touch them they alert the mobile monsters to your position. That would have been pretty cool!

[h1]Summary[/h1]
Overall, I think Still Wakes the Deep is a good game that [i]could[/i] have been an absolutely legendary game with a few key changes. I still love it, and I do think it's one of the most well-acted, well-written stories in gaming, so I still highly recommend it. I just can't help but think with a few changes it would have been an easy top 20 for me.

195619925.review:
I've played horror walking sims that I enjoyed. I didn't enjoy this. It's a bit like an interactive movie, but the game stuff is so simplified and marginalised that it feels like the interactivity mostly detracts from the experience. And at the same time, there's not enough of a story that this could ever hold up as an actual movie or short novel. It's barely a creepy pasta, and in order to experience it, you have to contend with a nauseating camera, janky controls and frustratingly sluggish movement. There's no incentive or opportunity to explore. You just walk forward, press the buttons that flash up on screen, and occasionally hide from a monster.

It's not all bad. The acting is solid. The monsters look cool and the cosmic gore is quite striking. But it wasn't enough to keep me interested and it doesn't escalate much or do anything that will really surprise you. And while the graphics are nice, I'm getting pretty tired of these UE5 effects that look nice in screenshots but have horribly noisy, distracting artefacts in motion, and tank the framerate while they're at it.

Overall, not a terrible game, but not good, and hard to recommend even if you can get it at half price.

195572769.review:
Overall I very much enjoyed this game, the team did a great job with building atmosphere and tension. I also enjoyed how the scenes were reused through the progression of the story turning from familiar to alien.

As far as the main game play loop, it felt reminiscent of games like SOMA or Alien isolation though a bit simplified. Even simplified, they added enough twists and unique situations that kept the game play outside of the story from getting too stale for the ~5 hours it took me complete the game.

As far as the story and characters I felt they were well written and their interactions felt natural as a snap shot out of a longer story. It would have been nice to see more of the characters before the main story occurs but as a focused narrative it came together pretty well. Having some pretty great environmental story telling and dialog with the exception of a few scenes feeling forced(mainly due to my attachments to character being limited due to my time with them).

Bottom line a 5 hour game for $35 may feel a bit steep but the quality was definitely there and I am in no way regretting getting it at full price, though it would be a steal at anything less.

195328012.review:
[b][i]Still Wakes the Deep[/i] follows the main character, "Caz", as he runs away from his past, to the ends of the Earth, onto a dreary oil rig in the North Sea. Navigate the rig, as a grim accident unfolds. Live vicariously through Caz, as he battles the supernatural and his own sanity.[/b]

I really enjoyed Still Wakes the Deep. I haven't worked on an oil rig, but I've heard endless personal stories. In fact, I had no idea there were oil drilling operations north of the UK. I'm not European, but I have Scottish heritage, and it turns out that I actually have familial ties to similar operations.

[h2]The Good[/h2]

The game does an amazing job of capturing the feeling. Between the environment itself, and the genuine Scottish accents in the English voicing, it literally put a smile on my face. All the banter, the stories, etc. [i]really[/i] sold it for me. Throughout the game, the voice acting was a mainstay. The passion & emotion in the dialogue is A grade.

The environment is highly detailed and (visually) expansive. From the start of the morning, between the dreary weather, and grandeur of the rig, to the evening, with the beautiful lighting & VFX from the stormy weather, to the end of the game, with all of the supernatural effects; it looks absolutely stunning. Even throughout the game, I found myself seeing little tiny details here and there. You can genuinely tell the developers put a lot of effort into this world.

[h2]The Bad[/h2]

[b]Slight Spoiler Warning[/b]

[spoiler]
As much as I enjoyed my experience, there really isn't that much [i]going on[/i]. [i]Still Wakes the Deep[/i] isn't a survival game, but there really isn't much in terms of gameplay, aside from the occasional "don't-fall-off-the-ladder" quick-time event, and a few sequences where you need to swim under something. The animations, experience, and ambience aren't bad; it's just not exactly... engaging.

At the start of the game, you have a little bit of free reign to explore around and talk to NPCs. As the game progresses, the gameplay areas become smaller and smaller, until the end, where you're basically just following an objective marker. I would have loved to see any sort of puzzle or interactions that persisted outside of what was immediately in front of me. Truthfully, it felt more like a movie than a game; I can't remember any part where I felt part in the experience.
[/spoiler]

[b]Very Spoiler Warning[/b]

[spoiler]
As the end of the game grows near, the story really takes a turn towards the obvious. There were multiple spots where the writers could've inserted twists or [i]false hope[/i] or literally [i]any[/i] plot device, but the story just steers towards the "inevitable".
[/spoiler]

[h2]The Ugly[/h2]

I won't sugarcoat it; this game runs worse than a clogged toilet. With high specs, the game probably is alright. I played on Steam Deck at 720p, and it wasn't a good experience. The game is packed with several forms of TAA & AI upscaling. At such a low resolution, it was a blurry gross mess, to the point of being unplayable. Fortunately, turning all of them off (they don't make it easy) gave me no performance penalty (game is clearly majorly CPU bottlenecked.

I'll give credit where credit is due; the game is highly detailed. Most of the open areas ran at 24+ FPS (about what I would expect), yet some of the darkest, emptiest, most enclosed spaces dropped below 5 FPS, and my only option was to turn on AMD framegen (which [i]did[/i] help!)

HDR was also a pain point for me. Maybe this is just a skill issue, but when I turned it on, the game became way too dark. No matter how high I cranked my brightness, the vibrant visuals just got blindingly brighter, but all other elements just stayed dark.

4/10
